Louis Sullivan (1924), who tabulated and analyzed anthropometric observations obtained by the Bayard Dominick field parties, wrote that “the Polynesians are a badly mixed people for whenever there has been a general disagreement as to the racial affinities of any group it has been found almost invariably that the group was a non-homogeneous group” (22; emphasis added). From the perspective of modern population biology such statements—antiquated at best, perniciously racist at worst—are representative of the typological thinking of this period. In the decades leading up to World War II, they helped to fuel white American views on racial purity and the desirability of restricting alien immigration, especially of “Asiatic races.”6 One outcome of this attitude was a particular shaping of the historical theories of the prominent Polynesian scholar Te Rangi Hiroa.





TE RANGI HIROA AND THE “MICRONESIAN ROUTE” TO POLYNESIA


Te Rangi Hiroa (Sir Peter Buck)7—one of the most prominent Polynesian scholars of the mid-twentieth century (fig. 1.7)—was raised in a bicultural world, learning the language and customs of his Maori mother while aspiring to the cultural institutions of his Irish father. Born in 1877, Hiroa attended Te Aute College, an Anglican boarding school for the children of the Maori elite, and earned his medical degree at Otago Medical School in 1910. Government service as medical officer to the Maoris and a term in the New Zealand parliament were interrupted by his enlistment with the much-feted Maori regiment, which barely survived the debacle at Gallipoli. Even before the Great War, Hiroa was becoming entranced with ethnological research. In 1927, Hiroa accepted an appointment to the Bishop Museum staff to carry out field studies in tropical Polynesia. Nine years later, the Bishop Museum trustees invited Hiroa to take the position of director on the retirement of Herbert Gregory.




FIGURE 1.7 Te Rangi Hiroa (right) and Kenneth P. Emory (left) of the Bishop Museum at the investiture stone of Hauvivi marae, part of the Taputapuātea complex on Ra‘iatea Island, during the Mangarevan Expedition of 1934. (Photo courtesy Bishop Museum Archives.)





As director of the Bishop Museum, Hiroa held a prominent academic position (the director also carried the title of professor at Yale University). Yet Hiroa found himself, part Polynesian, in a society shackled by ideas of innate differences between human “races.” He was proud of his Maori ancestry, yet painfully aware that many whites viewed the Polynesians as inferiors, classified by some anthropologists as a mixture of “Negroid” and “Mongoloid” elements.8 Despite his wartime service and academic fame, Hiroa had failed to garner the ultimate form of recognition from his mother country, a knighthood (Sorrenson, ed., 1986–88, 3:262; see also Condliffe 1971:197–98).9 This led him to seek American citizenship, but here too he ran up against racial prejudice, for to many Americans Polynesians were part of the feared “Yellow Peril.”10

In this social and political context, Hiroa developed a new theory of Polynesian origins, not surprisingly one that avoided any association between the Polynesian people and the dark-skinned inhabitants of Melanesia (then classified as “Oceanic Negroids”). Most theories of the time traced Polynesian migrations through Melanesia, implying that the Polynesians had a Melanesian “racial” component. As early as 1930, in reviewing Rivers’s History of Melanesian Society (1914), Hiroa wrote to his friend Sir Apirana Ngata, “If you can cut out any survivals of Melanesian influence on [Polynesian] social organization so much the better” (Sorrenson, ed., 1986–88, 2:78).11 In 1938, Hiroa published his mature theory in Vikings of the Sunrise (1938b), his grand synthesis of the Bishop Museum’s ethnographic survey of Polynesia (see also Hiroa 1944; 1945:12–13). The endpapers comprise a map of the Pacific with arrows, bearing the caption, “The Polynesian Triangle with the northern Micronesian route and the rejected southern Melanesian theory” (fig. 1.8) To avoid any taint of contamination with Melanesia, Hiroa had the ancestral Polynesians migrate from island Southeast Asia through the atolls of the Caroline and Kiribati archipelagoes. He invoked the anthropometric studies performed by the Bishop Museum, claiming that “the master mariners of the Pacific must be Europoid for they are not characterized by the woolly hair, black skins, and thin lower legs of the Negroids nor by the flat face, short stature, and drooping inner eyefold of the Mongoloids” (1938b:16).12 Hiroa put his case as follows:

In light of recent comparative study of the material cultures and social organizations of Melanesia and Polynesia, it seems improbable that the great migrations into the Pacific passed through Melanesia. In general the Polynesians are physically very different from the Melanesians. Had they stopped at Melanesian islands to refit their ships and gather new supplies, it is probable that racial intermixture would have taken place and that Negroid characteristics would appear consistently among Polynesians. . . .

Strong support in favor of the Micronesian route lies in the positive evidence against the route through Melanesia. It is unfortunate that the original population of Micronesia had been overlain by Mongoloid elements that crept in after the ancestors of the Polynesians had passed through. Yet, in spite of the imposition of a new language throughout the area, numerous Polynesian words occur to mark the ancient trail. (1938b:41, 45)




FIGURE 1.8 Te Rangi Hiroa’s theory of Polynesian origins, depicting the posited “Micronesian route” of migrations. The dashed lines indicate secondary migrations. (From Hiroa 1944.)





Determined to avoid any taint of Melanesian influence on Polynesia, Hiroa ignored several problems with his Micronesian route theory. One of the most serious was that the source of the Polynesian horticultural complex lay in Melanesia and could not be derived from the Micronesian atolls, a fact that Hiroa himself acknowledged (1938b:307; 1945:13). Moreover, the sophisticated stone tool industries of Polynesia would have had to be reinvented after a period of adaptation to shell technology in Micronesia. To resolve these problems, Hiroa had the Polynesians reacquire food plants and domestic animals from the Melanesians in Fiji, via Samoa and Tonga: “Though the Polynesians traveled into central Polynesia by the Micronesian route, such important food plants as the breadfruit, banana, yam, and finer taro were carried from Indonesia to New Guinea and relayed by Melanesians to their eastern outpost at Fiji. . . . The richer food plants which reached Fiji had to be relayed to central Polynesia through volcanic islands. The first relaying station in western Polynesia was provided by Samoa or Tonga” (1938b:307–8). It was hardly a parsimonious explanation.

I have dwelled at some length on Te Rangi Hiroa both because he was the prominent authority on Polynesia in the mid-twentieth century and because his work demonstrates how any scholar’s work can be influenced by his or her sociopolitical context. The Micronesian route theory—never well supported by empirical evidence—would fall completely once stratigraphic archaeology and modern comparative linguistics were brought to bear on the problem after World War II. Relying almost exclusively on comparative ethnographic data—especially material culture—Hiroa was nonetheless able to mount a convincing argument. His work marks the culmination of the period of ethnographic domination of Pacific prehistory, with archaeology a mere bystander.13





THE DISCOVERY OF TIME DEPTH AND CULTURE CHANGE


World War II focused unprecedented attention on the Pacific, as thousands of American GIs fought their way through the jungles of Guadalcanal, New Guinea, Saipan, and other exotic locales. Partly as a result of this heightened interest in the Pacific, at war’s end several regional anthropological programs were launched. While Pacific anthropology in the first half of the twentieth century had been dominated by ethnography, archaeology rapidly came into its own soon after midcentury. Edward W. Gifford of the University of California, Berkeley, who as a young ethnologist had led the 1920 Bayard Dominick Expedition to Tonga, decided in 1947 to turn his hand to archaeology. Convinced by the prevailing views of Hiroa and others that there was no cultural stratification in Polynesia, Gifford chose to tackle Fiji:

As tropical Polynesia has yielded archaeologically only the early phase of the local cultures which were flourishing at the time of discovery, I decided to look farther west for a succession of cultures. Fiji seemed a likely place, and moreover I reasoned that it might show traces of early Polynesians, if they had come via Fiji. Also, I reasoned that large islands rather than small ones would be likely to produce a succession of cultural horizons; early colonizers would presumably be attracted to large land masses rather than to small ones. Hence Viti Levu, of more than 4,000 square miles area, was selected. (1951:189)

Gifford explored Viti Levu for six months, discovering thirty-eight sites and excavating two, Navatu and Vunda (see chap. 5), both well stratified and producing a sequence of changing pottery styles. Gifford was uncertain of the ages of these sites, but based on external stylistic comparisons he thought the stratigraphically earlier materials might correlate with “the Bronze Age of southeastern Asia via Borneo and the Solomons” (1951:237). The modern period of Pacific archaeology was launched.

Buoyed by his Fijian results, Gifford organized a second Pacific archaeological expedition in 1952, this time to the large island of New Caledonia (Gifford and Shutler 1956).14 Willard Libby’s pathbreaking radiocarbon dating method had just been developed; Gifford seized the opportunity to date charcoal samples from his New Caledonian sites, as well as previously excavated samples from Fiji. At a place called Lapita on New Caledonia’s west coast, Gifford and his student Richard Shutler Jr. (fig. 1.9) found a distinctive kind of stamped pottery, which Gifford recognized as nearly identical to the sherds that his Bayard Dominick Expedition coworker McKern had found in Tonga in 1920. What truly astounded Gifford, however, was the age of the associated charcoal samples as dated by 14C: 2800 ± 350 years B.P.




FIGURE 1.9 Professor Edward W. Gifford (in pith helmet at left) of the University of California, Berkeley, and his graduate student assistant Richard Shutler Jr. (with dark glasses, on right), with their local workers, during their 1952 expedition to New Caledonia. It was during this expedition that the site of Lapita was discovered. (Photo courtesy P. A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology.)





Suddenly all the old assumptions about shallow time depth for Pacific island cultures seemed to crumble away; Gifford’s excavations revealed just the kind of cultural stratification that the previous generation of archaeologists had claimed did not exist. Here was a type of finely decorated pottery showing an early connection between Melanesia and Polynesia, quite contrary to Hiroa’s Micronesian route theory. Indeed, what soon came to be called the “Lapita” pottery style—crossing the boundary separating Melanesia and Polynesia—would in time force even the ethnologists to rethink fundamental categories they had inherited from the time of Dumont d’Urville (e.g., Thomas 1989; Tcherkézoff 2008).

In 1949–50, Alexander Spoehr of Chicago’s Field Museum of Natural History explored the archaeology of the Marianas Islands on the western fringe of Micronesia (Spoehr 1957). He too found well-stratified sites enmeshed in a ceramic sequence, as well as stone architecture that could be fitted into the later part of the cultural sequence (see chap. 6). Spoehr took advantage of the new radiocarbon dating method to obtain a basal date of 1527 ± 200 B.C. from the Chalan Piao site on Saipan. He boldly argued that while the “reconstruction of Oceanic prehistory” had previously drawn on other branches of anthropology, “it is very largely on the data of archaeology that future knowledge of Oceanic culture history must be built” (1957:17). Appointed to succeed Hiroa as director of the Bishop Museum after the latter’s death in 1951, Spoehr was soon in an influential position to advance that research agenda.

Less than a year before Te Rangi Hiroa died from cancer, the Bishop Museum’s Kenneth P. Emory had been the first to heed Willard Libby’s call for charcoal samples from Pacific Island sites.15 Emory, engaged in excavating the Kuli‘ou‘ou rockshelter on O‘ahu Island, sent Libby a sample from a hearth at the base of the shelter’s cultural deposit: “On February 19, 1951, Buck [Hiroa] called Kenneth to his office and read aloud a letter just received from Chicago. Libby had dated Kenneth’s sample of charcoal from the cave at Kuliouou at A.D. 1004, plus or minus 180 years. It was the first carbon date for Polynesia. ‘Boy, was I excited,’ said Kenneth later. ‘Immediately it opened a whole new vista of possibilities’” (quoted in Krauss 1988:338). Emory soon launched a major program in Hawaiian archaeology, excavating sites throughout all the main islands, leading to the definition of a sequence based not on pottery—which was absent from Eastern Polynesia—but on changes in bone and shell fishhook styles (Emory, Bonk, and Sinoto 1959).16

But Emory in Hawai‘i was not the only ethnologist-turned-archaeologist now searching for new evidence with which to reconstruct the Polynesian past. A decade earlier, Roger Duff had commenced excavations at Wairau Bar in New Zealand, which contained both habitation deposits and burials.17 Duff opened his classic monograph on the Wairau Bar site with this notable statement: “The student of the ethnography of Polynesia has at hand a remarkable human laboratory in which to study whatever laws determine the evolution of human culture in time and space.” He observed that if human culture had “remained static one might have expected the culture of any one island to have remained exactly like that of any other” (1950:1). Obviously, this had not been the case in the Pacific, even though the ethnologists had attempted to explain cultural differences as the result of successive migrations, or “waves,” of people. But in New Zealand, Duff and his colleagues discovered direct archaeological evidence of local cultural change, in part due to adaptation to the temperate conditions of these islands. Duff concluded that “the major contribution of this study to the theory of Polynesian cultural change is the demonstration that the culture may alter by a continuous and mainly self-motivated process of change” (12). At last, cultural change and process were being integrated into Pacific archaeological and anthropological research.

The rejuvenation of Pacific archaeology immediately following World War II owes a great deal to radiocarbon dating. This independent, scientific means of direct dating of cultural materials freed archaeologists from hazy guesswork based on presumed stylistic correlations, often over vast geographic distances. Moreover, the ages for the first dated samples from New Caledonia, Saipan, and O‘ahu exceeded what orthodox ethnographic theories had predicted. Henceforth, Pacific prehistory belonged to archaeology. The dominance of comparative ethnology and the diffusionists was a thing of the past.





THE SEARCH FOR POLYNESIAN SEQUENCES


Inspired by their colleagues’ successes, other researchers began to join the Pacific archaeological scene. Despite the exciting finds from Fiji, New Caledonia, and the Marianas, the greatest focus remained on Polynesia. In Hawai‘i, Emory was joined in 1954 by Yosihiko Sinoto (fig. 1.10), a young Japanese archaeologist, who convinced Emory to abandon his method of excavation by arbitrary six-inch levels in favor of real stratigraphic control (Sinoto with Aramata 2016). Joined by William J. Bonk, a University of Hawai‘i graduate student, Emory and Sinoto excavated stratified rockshelters and sand dunes—sites rich in fishhooks—throughout the Hawaiian Islands. Sinoto realized that he could use stylistic changes in fishhook shapes to seriate Hawaiian archaeological sites, in a manner similar to the way that pottery types were used in other parts of the world to establish chronological frameworks (Emory, Bonk, and Sinoto 1959; Sinoto 1962).




FIGURE 1.10 Kenneth P. Emory and Yosihiko Sinoto excavating at the Pu‘u Ali‘i sand dune site, South Point, Hawai‘i, in the early 1950s. They are kneeling on an exposed pavement of basalt cobbles, examining a remnant baulk of the overlying cultural deposit. (Photo courtesy Y. H. Sinoto.)





In New Zealand, the University of Auckland appointed its first academic archaeologist in 1954. Jack Golson, a graduate of Cambridge University, brought to New Zealand a materialist tradition of archaeology stemming from his mentors V. Gordon Childe and Graham Clarke. Golson set out to professionalize the standards of excavation in New Zealand, organized the New Zealand Archaeological Association, and published several key papers outlining the prehistoric cultural sequence in these large islands (Golson 1959; see also Groube 1993b).

In 1947, the world was enthralled by the Kon-Tiki balsa raft adventure of Thor Heyerdahl, a young Norwegian zoologist who tirelessly promoted his theory that the Polynesians had originated not in the west but from the Americas (Heyerdahl 1952). Riding on the fame of Kon-Tiki, Heyerdahl led an expedition into southeastern Polynesia in 1955–56, this time on a converted Greenland trawler equipped for archaeological research and staffed with four professional archaeologists (E. Ferdon, W. Mulloy, A. Skjølsvold, and C. Smith). The expedition’s main objective was Rapa Nui, but work was also carried out on Rapa Iti and Ra‘ivavae in the Australs and on Hiva Oa in the Marquesas (Heyerdahl and Ferdon, eds., 1961, 1965). On Rapa Nui, the team concentrated on the statuary cult (fig. 1.11), with surveys and excavations at the Rano Raraku statue quarry and at several temples sites (including the Ahu Vinapu complex), in large part because Heyerdahl had proposed that similarities between Rapa Nui architecture and statues and those found in parts of South America supported his migration theory.




FIGURE 1.11 Statue 295 on the slopes of the Rano Raraku quarry, Rapa Nui, after the completion of excavations by the Norwegian Archaeological Expedition in 1956. (Photo courtesy Kon Tiki Museum, Oslo.)





What the Norwegian Expedition failed to do, however, was convince anyone of the veracity of Heyerdahl’s South American origins hypothesis. The American archaeologist Robert C. Suggs lost no time leveling a searing attack on Heyerdahl. In his widely read synthesis, The Island Civilizations of Polynesia (1960), Suggs parodied Heyerdahl’s claims to have proved his raft theory:

Heyerdahl’s Peruvians must have availed themselves of the classical device of science fiction, the time machine, for they showed up off Easter Island in A.D. 380, led by a post–A.D. 750 Incan god-hero, with an A.D. 750 Tiahuanaco material culture featuring A.D. 1500 Incan walls, and not one thing characteristic of the Tiahuanaco period in Peru or Bolivia. This is equivalent to saying that America was discovered in the last days of the Roman Empire by King Henry the Eighth, who brought the Ford Falcon to the benighted aborigines. (Suggs 1960:224)

Heyerdahl did not fare much better in the scholarly press. Golson (1965:78) asserted that all of the archaeological evidence uncovered by the Norwegian Expedition supported a more conventional view that the island had been settled by ancestral Polynesians from the west, with subsequent internal cultural developments on Rapa Nui itself.

While the Norwegian Expedition was at work on Rapa Nui, Robert Suggs was carrying out the first stratigraphic excavations in the Marquesas Islands of Eastern Polynesia (fig. 1.12). Suggs’s trend-setting research reflected changing agendas in Americanist anthropology. In the post–World War II era, an evolutionary perspective had arisen under the influence of such scholars as Julian Steward and Leslie White, as had an interest in human ecology (see Harris 1968:643–87). Although Suggs did not neglect artifact classification and seriation, he was intrigued by broader anthropological questions of culture change: “I wanted to collect comparative information on much more general topics. How did various kinds of ancient temple architecture in Polynesia fit in with the religious philosophies of the natives? Was there evidence of warfare on other islands? If so, did the situations resemble that which had evidently arisen on Nuku Hiva during the period when so much of the population had sought shelter in caves in the remote reaches of the island? How did the village plans in various Polynesian societies fit with the social organization of these groups?” (1962:222).




FIGURE 1.12 Robert C. Suggs cleaning a stratigraphic section through the Ha‘atuatua dune site in the Marquesas Islands, on the day he discovered the site in 1956. The Ha‘atuatua site revealed the earliest period of Eastern Polynesian occupation. (Photo courtesy R. C. Suggs.)





Rather than just enumerate a sequence of changing artifact types over time, Suggs’s (1961) synthesis of Marquesan prehistory constituted a model for how the Marquesan culture had evolved in relation to such factors as island ecology and human demography (fig. 1.13). His research meshed well with changing perspectives in Oceanic cultural anthropology, which had abandoned the old comparative ethnographic approach of Handy and Hiroa in favor of controlled comparisons and the study of culture process, a perspective exemplified in the work of such influential anthropologists as Ward Goodenough (1957), Marshall Sahlins (1958), and Irving Goldman (1970).




FIGURE 1.13 Robert Suggs’s cultural sequence for the Marquesas Islands, as graphically summarized by American Museum artist Nicholas Amorosi. Suggs defined his cultural periods as much in terms of settlement patterns and population dynamics as artifact types. (Courtesy R. C. Suggs.)





In the early 1960s, following their success in finding sites rich in fishhooks and other artifacts in Hawai‘i, the Bishop Museum’s Emory and Sinoto extended their research to the Society Islands. Disappointed by the lack of stratified, artifact-rich sites in Tahiti, Sinoto shifted his efforts to the Marquesas (Sinoto 1966). The Hane dune site on Ua Huka Island in the northern Marquesas, excavated by Sinoto from 1963 to 1965, yielded a rich assemblage of fishhooks, adzes, and other artifacts (fig. 1.14). These discoveries led Emory and Sinoto to propose a new model for the settlement of Eastern Polynesia, with the Marquesas as a dispersal center from which people moved to other archipelagoes of the region (Emory and Sinoto 1965; Sinoto 1966, 1970). Their Marquesas-centric model of Eastern Polynesian settlement is depicted in figure 1.15.




FIGURE 1.14 Excavations by Yosihiko Sinoto at the sand dune site of Hane on Ua Huka, Marquesas Islands, exposed simple pavements of waterworn stones on which dwellings were erected. (Photo courtesy Y. H. Sinoto.)





FIGURE 1.15 The model of Polynesian dispersals proposed by Emory and Sinoto (1965), based on initial results of excavations in Eastern Polynesia, emphasized the Marquesas Islands as a primary dispersal center.





BROADENING RESEARCH HORIZONS


By the 1960s, the research agendas of Pacific archaeologists and anthropologists were broadening beyond issues of cultural origins and defining artifact-based sequences. A major innovation was the development of “settlement pattern” archaeology, first applied in Polynesia by Roger C. Green, who had been trained in this approach by Gordon Willey at Harvard (Golson 1996a). First in his study of the ‘Opunohu Valley on Mo‘orea in the Society Islands (Green 1961; Green et al. 1967) and then in a major multiyear project focused on Western Samoa (Green and Davidson 1969, 1974), Green demonstrated the power of a settlement pattern perspective. Rather than concentrate exclusively on artifact-rich sites, Green wanted to incorporate all aspects of an archaeological landscape (fig. 1.16). He argued that “with the development of settlement pattern studies in archaeology and increasing concern with delineating the social aspect of the data recovered from sites as well, the day has passed when such monuments or their structural features can afford to be treated only as contexts for portable artifacts and not as artifacts in their own right” (1967b:102). Settlement pattern studies became the norm in Polynesia, as archaeologists recognized their potential to yield insights into prehistoric social and political organization. By the late 1960s and early 1970s, such studies were being carried out in Hawai‘i (e.g., Kirch and Kelly 1975; Tuggle and Griffin 1973), New Zealand (Groube 1964, 1965), Rapa Nui (McCoy 1976), and the Marquesas (Bellwood 1972; Kellum-Ottino 1971).




FIGURE 1.16 The settlement pattern approach to archaeology, introduced to Polynesia by Roger C. Green, paid attention to all classes of sites and not merely to artifact-rich deposits. This map of a marae, or temple complex, in the ‘Opunohu Valley, Mo‘orea, was prepared as part of Green’s settlement pattern survey in 1961. (From Green et al. 1967.)





The settlement pattern approach, initially intended to shed light on prehistoric social relations and community structure, rapidly acquired an additional, ecological orientation. Combined with the recovery and analysis of faunal and floral materials (e.g., Kirch 1973; Shawcross 1967, 1972), settlement pattern studies began to encompass agricultural field systems and other evidence for economic production. The ethnobotanist Douglas E. Yen collaborated with archaeologists on a pioneering study of prehistoric irrigation in the Makaha Valley, O‘ahu, that yielded the first stratified evidence for agricultural change in Polynesia (Yen et al. 1972). Archaeological remains of agricultural systems and the identification of preserved plant remains were increasingly incorporated into Pacific research.

During the Cold War, government institutions increasingly influenced U.S. science. American research universities and institutions began to receive unprecedented infusions of federal funds, owing in part to the newly constituted National Science Foundation (NSF). As early as 1951, a subcommittee on Pacific archaeology of the National Research Council recommended a program for Micronesian archaeology, and a similar program for Polynesian archaeology was put forward in 1953 (Emory 1962:1; Spoehr 1954:24).18 Throughout the 1950s, expanding field research in Pacific archaeology was still supported largely by private funds and grants, but this changed dramatically in 1962 when the Bishop Museum received its first NSF grant for Pacific archaeology (Emory 1962; Force 1964:19, 29; Spoehr 1962:13–14). With $77,220 for a three-year period, the museum dispatched field expeditions to Rarotonga, Pitcairn, Western Samoa, and American Samoa, including representatives of Auckland University, the Canterbury Museum, and the Otago Museum (New Zealand). Alex Spoehr, who would soon turn the reins of the Bishop Museum over to his protégé Roland Force, summed up the role of the NSF and other federal agencies in Pacific research: “The financial resources required for reasonably large scale research are beyond the means of any single institution. In the United States, federal support of basic research is becoming more and more necessary” (1962:3).

The Bishop Museum implemented its second three-year program of “Polynesian culture history” in 1965 (Force 1965), with increased NSF funding of $274,554 again supporting an international group of investigators, incorporating historical linguistics and ethnobotany along with archaeology. The impact of this infusion of federal research funds cannot be underestimated; many of the advances in Polynesian archaeology during the 1960s stem directly from these programs. Moreover, the NSF has continued to fund Pacific archaeology, such as the multi-institutional Southeast Solomon Islands Culture History Program (SSICHP) (Green and Cresswell 1976) and more recently the Hawai‘i Biocomplexity Project (Kirch, ed., 2011).





MOVING BEYOND POLYNESIA: ARCHAEOLOGY IN MELANESIA AND NEW GUINEA


Although Gifford and Spoehr targeted western Pacific archipelagoes in their post–World War II efforts to revitalize Pacific archaeology, most research in the 1950s and 1960s continued to focus on Polynesia. Perhaps this was an inevitable need to achieve consensus on the “problem of Polynesian origins,” based on the new methods of stratigraphic excavation and radiocarbon dating. But Jack Golson, seeing the need for renewed work in Melanesia, seized the opportunity presented by an offer of appointment to the faculty of the Australian National University (ANU) in Canberra in 1961.

Golson mapped out a series of projects that would add immensely to our knowledge of Melanesian prehistory (see Golson 1996a). Aware of excavations by Ralph and Susan Bulmer (1964) in two rockshelters in the New Guinea Highlands, Golson sent his student J. Peter White to find and dig additional rockshelters in the interior of that huge island.19 Other students were dispatched to locations that Golson judged would be key to expanding our knowledge of Melanesian prehistory: Peter Lauer to the D’Entrecasteaux Islands, Brian Egloff to Collingwood Bay on the northeastern New Guinea coast, and Ron Vanderwal and Geoff Irwin to the Papuan coast.20 The emerging importance of the Lapita cultural complex—about which Golson himself wrote several key papers (Golson 1963, 1971, 1972a, 1972b)—was not neglected. Jim Specht reexcavated the Watom Lapita site, Jens Poulsen dug several Lapita middens on Tongatapu, and Colin Smart investigated sites in New Caledonia.21 Jim Allen, who had studied Australian historical archaeology under Golson, was appointed to the first faculty position in archaeology at the new University of Papua New Guinea in 1969. From this base, Allen carried out important work at Nebira and Motupore near Port Moresby.

By the close of the 1960s, the new fieldwork in New Guinea and the Melanesian islands was finally drawing attention away from Polynesia, to the western archipelagoes of the Pacific. Roger Green, who was deeply engaged with the question of Polynesian origins (Green 1966, 1967a, 1968), became convinced that the Lapita ceramic complex held essential clues to the deeper origins of the pre-Polynesians, as well as to the peoples of island Melanesia. In 1970, Green and Douglas Yen obtained NSF support for “an interdisciplinary investigation of the prehistory of the Southeast Solomon and Santa Cruz Islands” (Green 1976a:9). Their research design combined fundamental culture-historical problems with an emphasis on ecological diversity, economic systems, and interisland exchange networks. In 1971–72, field teams investigated Kolombangara, Ulawa, Uki, San Cristobal, Santa Ana, Santa Cruz (Nendö), the Reef Islands, and Anuta, precipitating a veritable outpouring of research results. The NSF funded a second two-year phase of the Southeast Solomons project in 1977–78, extending fieldwork to Vanikoro, Tikopia (fig. 1.17), and Taumako, with continued work in Nendö and the Reef Islands.22




FIGURE 1.17 Excavations in the deeply stratified Sinapupu sand dune site on Tikopia Island during the 1978 phase of the Southeast Solomons Culture History Program revealed three thousand years of continuous occupation. (Photo by P.V. Kirch.)





The Southeast Solomons project demonstrated the effectiveness of coordinated, multi-institutional research programs. Jim Allen (1984a) consciously adopted this approach when he organized the Lapita Homeland Project (LHP), which focused on the Bismarck Archipelago. Although they were known to have some Lapita sites and while there was a suggestion from the Misisil rockshelter on New Britain that they might have a Pleistocene history (Specht, Lilley, and Normu 1981), the Bismarcks’ prehistory was largely unknown. In 1985, the LHP carried out surveys and excavations on New Britain, New Ireland, Watom, Nissan, Manus, and Mussau. This concerted effort not only expanded our knowledge of Lapita sites (Gosden et al. 1989), but resulted in the discovery of several cave sites with Pleistocene sequences (fig. 1.18) that pushed the prehistory of the Bismarck Archipelago back to an astounding 33,000 years (Allen, Gosden, and White 1989; Allen and Gosden, eds., 1991). Pleistocene dates had been known for Australia and New Guinea, but this was the first demonstration that humans had expanded into the Near Oceanic islands at such an early period.




FIGURE 1.18 Excavations in progress at the Matenbek site, New Ireland, during the Lapita Homeland Project in 1985. (Photo courtesy Jim Allen.)





Island Melanesia has continued as the scene of much exciting fieldwork over the past two decades, especially as members of the LHP built on initial results to extend their research in New Britain, Mussau, Manus, and elsewhere. And in both Vanuatu and New Caledonia, a number of archaeologists have greatly expanded on the pioneering work of Gifford and Golson to clarify our knowledge of southern Melanesian prehistory (see chap. 5). Important new research has also been carried out in the western Solomons. Melanesia, long neglected, finally has a significant archaeological record of its own, one in many respects deeper and richer than that of Polynesia.





FRANCOPHONE ARCHAEOLOGY IN THE PACIFIC


In spite of France’s substantial colonial holdings in the Pacific, including French Polynesia, Wallis and Futuna, New Caledonia, and the Loyalty Islands, the French academic establishment was slow to engage in archaeological research in the region. Inspired by the dramatic results of American researchers in various islands of French Polynesia, in 1962–63, Pierre Vérin conducted excavations on Rurutu in the Australs (Vérin 1969). It was José Garanger, however, who was largely responsible for launching a new era of Francophone archaeological research in the Pacific. Trained by the famous French prehistorian Leroi-Gourhan, Garanger went in 1964 to the then French-British “Condominium of the New Hebrides” (now Vanuatu) at the urging of the ethnologist Jean Guiart, who was concerned that the program of Pacific archaeological research initiated at the 1961 Pacific Science Congress should have Francophone participation (Di Piazza, Pearthree, and Sand 2008:22; Sand 2004). Exploring central Vanuatu, Garanger discovered a ceramic horizon—which he named Mangaasi after its type locality—that seemed to replace the Lapita ceramic horizon in parts of island Melanesia (Garanger 1972a). Equally important, he opened up a new interpretive realm at the interface of archaeology and oral traditions by demonstrating the historical veracity of certain indigenous Vanuatu narrative histories (Garanger 1972b), particularly that of the famous chief Roy Mata (see chap. 5).23 These efforts led Garanger to establish the first French academic program in Pacific archaeology, initially at the University of Nanterre and later at the Sorbonne in Paris, where he succeeded Leroi-Gourhan as the director of the famous Laboratoire d’Ethnologie Préhistorique.

Although Garanger did not continue fieldwork in the Pacific, he sent a succession of students there; indeed, virtually all of the French archaeologists working in the Pacific were at one time or another associated with Garanger’s research team. Among the first of these was Daniel Frimigacci, who excavated in New Caledonia and later in Futuna and ‘Uvea (Frimigacci 1974, 1975, 1990, 2000). In New Caledonia, Frimigacci was joined by Jean-Christophe Galipaud (1988), then by Christophe Sand (1994, 1995). Sand, a locally born “Caledoche” whose ancestors were exiled from France to New Caledonia, has built a major program of archaeology in New Caledonia. Working closely with Sand in these efforts over many years have been the Kanak archaeologists Jacques Bolé, André Ouétcho, and Francis Wadra. Frédérique Valentin, a bioarchaeologist based at the University of Nanterre, has been especially active in recent years in several collaborative projects, including the major excavations at the Lapita site of Teouma in Vanuatu (Valentin et al. 2011).

In French Polynesia, Garanger was responsible for the establishment in 1979 of an archaeology program at the Centre Polynésien des Sciences Humaines in Tahiti, initially directed by Jean-Michel Chazine (1977) and later by Maeva Navarro. A number of French archaeologists, including Pierre Ottino, Eric Conte, Catherine and Michel Orliac, and Bertrand Gérard subsequently carried out surveys and excavations in the Society Islands, the Marquesas, and the Tuamotus. Eric Conte, in particular, has gone on to a distinguished career in the archaeology of French Polynesia, establishing the Centre International pour la Recherche Archéologique sur la Polynésie at the University of French Polynesia and training the latest generation of French archaeologists, including Guillaume Molle (2011) and Aymeric Hermann (2013).

Although there are many commonalities in the approaches of Anglophone and Francophone archaeologists working in the Pacific, there are significant differences as well (Conte 2000). Sand sees these contrasts as stemming from fundamentally different perspectives about how science should be conducted: “Simply put, European, and especially south European anthropology, believes that [theoretical] models must develop mainly from empirical field data, whereas most Anglophone human sciences reply on theoretical models built as abstract academic constructs that are then tested in the field” (Di Piazza, Pearthree, and Sand 2008:31; see also Sand 2004).





NOT AN IVORY TOWER: PUBLIC ARCHAEOLOGY IN THE PACIFIC


From the first decades of the twentieth century—when anthropology and archaeology achieved formal status as academic disciplines—to the 1960s, virtually all archaeological research in the Pacific was carried out by museum- or university-based scholars. In the United States (including Hawai‘i), federal and state laws passed in the 1960s and 1970s began to mandate archaeological surveys and “salvage” excavation. Some museums and university departments began to undertake “contract archaeology” (which came to be called cultural resource management, or CRM, archaeology). But these changes also spawned the rise of independent archaeological contractors. Initially, CRM archaeology in the Pacific was confined to Hawai‘i and to other U.S. territories, including American Samoa and Guam. However, the New Zealand government soon adopted similar laws, and in Micronesia the U.S. federal statutes began to be applied in the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands, and later by the independent governments of Palau, the Northern Marianas, the Federated States of Micronesia, and the Marshall Islands (see Cordy 1980, 1982). In fact, the expansion of archaeological work throughout Micronesia over the past forty years has been largely driven by the CRM movement.

The rise of CRM archaeology in Hawai‘i, New Zealand, Micronesia, and to a lesser extent elsewhere in the Pacific has had several consequences. A huge infusion of funds for archaeological work (in the case of Hawai‘i, totaling several million U.S. dollars per year), far exceeding what has ever been available for archaeological research either from private funding sources or from federal research agencies such as the NSF, has produced a vast outpouring of survey and excavation data. However, these results are most often reported in limited-distribution documents and are only too rarely published in traditional academic journals or books. Synthesis lags behind fieldwork and data reporting. The inevitable linking of archaeology with land development has also led to sometimes heated conflicts between archaeologists and indigenous peoples, as in Hawai‘i (see Kawelu 2015; Kawelu and Pakele 2014; Kirch 1999; Nāleimaile and Brandt 2013; Sorovi-Vundilo 2003; Spriggs 1989, 1991a).

In the postcolonial world of the Pacific, the very practice of archaeology (as well as anthropology) became the focus of critique, discussion, and renegotiation. Some indigenous activists called for a halt to archaeological work, seeing it as a legacy of white colonialism. In the early 1990s, partly in response to the hugely controversial H-3 highway project on O‘ahu and the excavation of a major burial ground on Maui (Kawelu 2015), the Native Hawaiian activist Haunani-Kay Trask (1993:172) called for a stop to “all anthropology and archaeology on Hawaiians.” Trask wrote, “There should be a moratorium on studying, unearthing, slicing, crushing, and analyzing us.” Others, such as Joseph Kabui, former premier of Bougainville Province (Papua New Guinea), regarded archaeology positively, looking to archaeological findings in their search for national identity (Spriggs 1992a:269). Certainly, as this historical overview shows, archaeology in the Pacific was—with a few notable exceptions such as Te Rangi Hiroa—until quite recently largely the work of nonindigenous scholars. But things have changed rapidly in the twenty-first century. Indigenous Maori, Hawaiian, Fijian, New Guinean, Kanak, and other Pacific islanders have increasingly become involved in the practice of archaeology in the Pacific. Today many other indigenous scholars have joined the ranks of practicing archaeologists. Some of those who have made substantial contributions include Nick Araho, Alois Kuaso, John Muke, and Herman Mandui in Papua New Guinea; Lawrence Foanaota, John Keopo, and Kenneth Roga in the Solomon Islands; Jacques Bolé, André Ouétcho, and Francis Wadra in New Caledonia; Ralph Regenvunu in Vanuatu; Sepeti Matararaba and Tarisi Sorovi-Vunidilo in Fiji; Hinanui Cauchois and Paul Niva in French Polynesia; Peter Adds, Des Kahotea, Nepia Stewart, Nick Tupara, and Dean Whiting in New Zealand; Mauricio Rufino in Pohnpei; Erika Radewagen and Epi Suafo‘a in American Samoa; and Kehau Cachola-Abad, Koa Hodgins, Kathy Kawelu, Kekuewa Kikiloi, Sean Nali‘imaile, and Hanna Springer in Hawai‘i. With these and other indigenous scholars, the future of Pacific islands archaeology is sure to take on new dimensions and explore new waters.





RECENT ADVANCES IN PACIFIC ARCHAEOLOGY


In the foregoing pages I traced some of the main intellectual themes and debates that run through the history of archaeology and historical anthropology in the Pacific. The ways in which scholars have approached Pacific prehistory, their assumptions about time depth and cultural processes, the kinds of evidence they considered—all of these have shifted in ways both subtle and dramatic. Assessing where contemporary archaeology in the Pacific is headed is more challenging.

To the outsider, archaeologists of the Pacific may seem to be endlessly squabbling over theory, methods, evidence, and interpretation. Some debates focus on historical particulars and reflect rapidly changing empirical records. These kinds of issues eventually get resolved by new fieldwork and by finer-grained laboratory analysis. Other debates reflect differences in fundamental approaches to archaeology. Still other controversies stem from how one views the Pacific islands as a theater for anthropological research. Since at least 1950, many scholars invoked the “laboratory” aspects of Pacific islands and their human populations and cultures, suggesting that in the Pacific one has a kind of “natural experiment” of cultural differentiation and adaptation (e.g., Duff 1950; Goldman 1970; Goodenough 1957; Kirch 1984a; Sahlins 1958). Others have objected to what they call the “myth of the primitive isolate” (Terrell et al. 1997; Rainbird 2004). A better approach may be that of islands as “model systems,” the fundamental premise adopted by the Hawai‘i Biocomplexity Project (Kirch, ed., 2011; Vitousek 2002; Vitousek et al. 2004). Another debate, which has subsided somewhat in recent years, entails phylogenetic versus reticulate models of cultural differentiation (Bellwood 1996b; Kirch and Green 1987, 2001).

One thing is certain: since the publication of the first edition of this book nearly two decades ago, the range of methods and techniques available to Pacific archaeologists has expanded immensely, allowing for the extraction of many new kinds of data from the archaeological record. In the critical arena of dating and chronology—on which all archaeological interpretations are ultimately based—major advances in radiocarbon dating and the routine use of accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS) dating now yield age estimates with standard errors of just ± 15 years. Botanical identification of charcoal through examination of wood anatomy prior to dating (Allen and Huebert 2014), allowing the selection of short-lived plant specimens, has largely eliminated the problem of “old wood” or in-built age, which was responsible for misleading archaeologists regarding initial settlement ages in Eastern Polynesia and elsewhere (see chap. 7). The application of Bayesian calibration for sets of radiocarbon dates has contributed further to heightened precision in our chronologies (Dye 2011, 2015). In addition to these advances, the application of uranium/thorium (U/Th) series dating to corals is yielding dates with a previously unheard of precision of just ± 2 years in some samples. U/Th dating has elucidated the chronologies of temple construction in Hawai‘i and the Society Islands (Kirch and Sharp 2005; Kirch et al. 2015; P.C. McCoy et al. 2009) and pinned down the settlement date for Tongatapu (Burley, Weisler, and Zhao 2012; Burley et al. 2015).

When the first edition of this book was published, geochemical characterization and sourcing of adzes and other lithic artifacts was just beginning to benefit from X-ray fluorescence (XRF) analysis; two decades later, improvements in XRF combined with isotopic techniques permit discrimination between sources and quarries not previously possible, allowing interisland and interarchipelago transfer of stone tools to be identified (Collerson and Weisler 2007; Weisler et al. 2016). Analysis of stable isotopes of carbon and nitrogen in the bones of humans as well as of commensal animals such as rats, pigs, and dogs has provided new insights into ancient diets and subsistence practices (Allen and Craig 2009; Kinaston et al. 2013; Stantis et al. 2015, 2016; Swift, Miller, and Kirch 2016). Another source of data on diet comes from the extraction of microfossils such as phytoliths, pollen, and starch grains that are trapped in the dental calculus (tartar) of ancient teeth (Dudgeon and Tromp 2014; Tromp et al. 2016). Such plant microfossils are also now being routinely extracted from archaeological sediments (Horrocks et al. 2003; Horrocks and Nunn 2007; Horrocks and Wozniak 2007). And, of course, we cannot ignore the huge contributions being made by genetic sequencing of ancient DNA (aDNA) from the bones of humans and other animals.

At the much larger scale of interpreting entire archaeological landscapes, settlement pattern and spatial analysis has been greatly augmented by the use of Geographical Information Systems (GIS), enabled in part by the Global Positioning System (GPS), for accurate site location (McCoy and Ladefoged 2009). Virtually all landscape-level interpretation in Pacific archaeology now utilizes GIS. And remote sensing, which previously depended primarily on either aerial photography or satellite imagery, has now received a major advance through airborne LiDAR, capable of producing high-resolution images of archaeological features, even through forest canopy (Freeland et al. 2016; M.D. McCoy et al. 2011; Quintus et al. 2015).

No doubt Pacific archaeologists will continue to debate the interpretations, hypotheses, and models derived from our evidence, even as our ability to generate ever more data increases. As the great biologist Ernst Mayr (1997) observed, science is a self-correcting system. Only time will reveal what survives and what ends up on the midden heap of intellectual history. I, for one, would be sad to see the day dawn when we think we know all the answers, for half the fun lies in pursuing new questions. My intention has been merely to show that the field of Pacific archaeology—as always—is in flux. That, after all, makes the pursuit of it stimulating and worthwhile.





TWO


The Pacific Islands as a Human Environment



In no other ocean has it been as difficult for a bit of land to be entirely surrounded by water. In no other has it been as difficult for a piece of the earth’s crust to raise its head above the water to become an island. The wonder is not that most islands are small and scattered but that there are so many of them, for the Pacific Ocean contains more islands than all the rest of the world’s oceans combined.

W.L. THOMAS JR., “The Variety of Physical

Environments among Pacific Islands” (1963)





DURING A LIFETIME OF RESEARCH I have been fortunate to visit, explore, and simply spend time on myriad environmentally and ecologically diverse islands (Kirch 2015). These have included diminutive Anuta, less than a square kilometer in land area and just 80 meters high, yet the permanent home of some 160 Polynesians; the semi-atoll formed by Eloaua and Emananus, whose reefs and lagoon harbor an almost indescribable array of marine life; Mangaia, with its fortresslike escarpment of upraised pinnacle karst encompassing an ancient, highly eroded volcano; the vast, elongated island of New Caledonia, whose ancient rocks and metallic soils are remnants of archaic Gondwanaland; and Moloka‘i, whose majestic windward cliffs rise 1,200 meters from the sea, gapped by lush amphitheater-headed valleys with waterfalls cascading down their slopes (fig. 2.1). The contrasts inherent in just these five examples testify to the amazing diversity of Pacific island environments. Such contrasts stem from varied geological origins that have raised islands from the oceanic depths, from different ages, from the work of wind and water on landforms, and from biological processes of dispersal, colonization, and evolution that have cloaked Pacific islands in a rich flora and fauna, distinct from those of continents.




FIGURE 2.1 Dramatic sea cliffs along the windward coast of Moloka‘i were created by massive landslides. Deep amphitheater-headed valleys slice into the central mountain range. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





In spite of tremendous diversity, some patterns and processes are common to all oceanic islands. Islands can be classified into several major geological types, and important biogeographic trends across the Pacific account for much of the variation in island biota. In this chapter I review these patterns, trends, and processes, providing a background for understanding the human settlement of the Pacific. The story of the movement of Homo sapiens, first out onto the larger islands and archipelagoes of Near Oceania, then in more recent times into the vast reaches of Remote Oceania, eventually to discover virtually every habitable speck of land, cannot be fully appreciated without some understanding of these key environmental variables. Winds and currents, along with island distances, have influenced the development of navigational methods and seafaring skills. Limitations in natural plant and animal foods helped to shape the subsistence economies of Pacific peoples. Variations in soil, rainfall, and climate necessitated adaptations to their horticultural practices. The availability or absence of natural materials—basalt, chert, obsidian, shell, plant fibers, and the like—played a role in the development of material culture. None of this is to say that the lifeways or cultures of Pacific islanders, any more than those of other peoples, were “determined” by their natural environments. On the contrary, humans everywhere actively modify and shape their world; in contemporary ecological jargon, they “construct” their own ecological “niches.” Yet they do so within certain constraints—and sometimes challenges—posed by the environments they inhabit.





ORIGINS AND DEVELOPMENT OF THE PACIFIC ISLANDS


The geological origins of the Pacific islands mystified scientists of the nineteenth and early twentieth century. The possibility that a great Pacific continent had formerly existed, only to sink beneath the sea, was entertained by various scholars and provided the basis for one of the more bizarre theories on the origins of Pacific peoples.1 The theory of plate tectonics is surely one of the great triumphs of later twentieth-century science, revealing that the earth’s crust consists of shifting plates separated by boundaries, themselves variously zones of spreading or subduction.2 Along the spreading zones, outpourings of magma continually create new crust, especially along the East Pacific Rise. At the subduction zones, as one plate thrusts beneath another, the oceanic crust plunges to great depths to be reabsorbed into the earth’s mantle, partly to reappear as molten lava on the surface through island-arc volcanism, such as along the Kermadec-Tonga Trench.

A tectonic map reveals the center of the Pacific basin to be a vast crustal sheet, the Pacific Plate (map 3). Originating along its eastern margin, the Pacific Plate extends from the Pacific-Antarctic Ridge up through the East Pacific Rise (on which Rapa Nui sits) and follows the western coast of North America. As new oceanic crust is created along this lineament, the Pacific Plate ever so slowly yet relentlessly moves toward the northwest (arrows on map 3). At its western margins the Pacific Plate is “consumed” by being downthrust into a complex series of subduction zones, marked by deep oceanic trenches. These include the Kermadec-Tonga Trench, the New Britain Trench, the Palau Trench, the Mariana Trench (notable for having the deepest spot in the earth’s oceans), the Japan and Kuril Trenches, and the Aleutian Trench. The sinuous line formed by these trenches has long been known as the Andesite Line, because the rocks found in islands to the west are predominantly andesitic, whereas islands on the Pacific Plate are dominated by basaltic lavas. To the west of these trenches lies not just one plate, but a maze of interlocking smaller plates.




MAP 3 A tectonic map of the Pacific basin. The heavy lines indicate major plate boundaries, and the general directions of plate movement are indicated by the arrows. (Adapted from Oliver 1989.)





Strings of island-arcs formed by a characteristic kind of volcanic activity define the western margins of the Pacific Plate. As the geographer Patrick Nunn describes the process, “The magma involved in island-arc volcanism derives from melting of subducted ocean crust. . . . The melting of the crust is the consequence of being heated through burial and from friction along the contact with the buoyant plate” (1994:34). Such plate margin volcanism has formed the extensive Tonga, Vanuatu, and Solomon archipelagoes. The complex geological history of island-arcs can include successive stages of active volcanism, erosion, subsidence, and formation of marine limestones and other sedimentary deposits, producing islands with complex structures of volcanic, metavolcanic, and sedimentary rocks.

The western margin of the Pacific also incorporates plate fragments derived from the ancient supercontinent of Gondwana, which began to break apart 100 million years ago. New Caledonia is one such Gondwanaland fragment, parts of New Zealand another; both broke off from Australia about 80 million years ago and began a slow, isolating drift to the east. Their origins in the ancient southern supercontinent account for unique aspects of the biota of these islands, including archaic conifer trees and many species of large geckos on New Caledonia.3 New Guinea too has a highly complex geological history—also reflecting its Gondwanaland origins—and contains rocks ranging in age from Cambrian or even older formations up to recent Pleistocene volcanics (Brookfield and Hart 1971:26–29).

Pacific Plate islands have a different origin mechanism from that of island-arcs. These midplate or intraplate islands typically originate from a “hot spot,” or thermal plume of magma arising from deep in the mantle. Such hot spots are stationary within the mantle, while the slowly migrating lithosphere of the Pacific Plate gradually moves over them.4 This movement is sufficiently slow that there is time for new islands to be created by successive extrusions of lava that begin with a rupture on the seafloor; the lava flows pile up, creating a volcanic mass that eventually rises above the ocean. In time, the new island moves off the hot spot as it is carried along by the conveyor belt of the crustal lithosphere. A new island then begins to form over the same stationary hot spot. Over a period of tens of millions of years, an entire linear chain of volcanic islands successively rises from the ocean depths. Midplate islands in such linear chains typically pass through the following evolutionary stages: (1) initial volcanism and shield building; (2) subsidence due to point-loading of the thin oceanic crust; (3) subaerial erosion by streams, waves, and wind; (4) often, a late stage of renewed volcanism, typically pyroclastic; (5) continued erosion and reduction of the island’s mass; (6) formation of extensive coastal reefs, if the island is within the tropical to subtropical regions; (7) complete erosion and subsidence of the volcanic cone, resulting in the formation of a coral atoll; and (8) eventual drowning of the island, forming a submerged seamount.

The textbook example of such a midplate linear island chain is the Hawaiian-Emperor Archipelago, extending from the island of Hawai‘i (where the originating hot spot is located), up through the main “high” volcanic islands such as O‘ahu, and continuing through the remnant volcanic pinnacles and coral atolls of the Northwestern Hawaiian chain (also known as Papahānaumokuākea) and finally to the submerged Emperor seamounts that end in Meiji Seamount, a total distance of 3,942 kilometers.5 The Hawaiian chain was created by a stationary magma plume situated in the southeast (where a new, submarine island, named Loihi, is being constructed today off the coast of Hawai‘i); eventually its submerged islands end their evolutionary cycle with subduction into the Kuril Trench. Based on potassium-argon dating of islands along this lineament, the entire life cycle of a Hawaiian island spans 75 million to 80 million years, during which it is above water for perhaps only the first 8 million to 15 million years.

Most islands on the Pacific Plate are oriented along such linear chains, including the Marquesas, Society Islands, Tuamotus, Australs–Southern Cooks, and Samoa. Where they lie in this evolutionary cycle determines the degree of weathering and landform development that occurs. For example, in the main Hawaiian Islands the new island of Hawai‘i (with surface rocks less than 0.3 million years old) has been weathered very little, most of its surface lacking streams or deeply developed soils. At the other end of the chain, Kaua‘i (about 5.1 million years old) is deeply weathered, boasting a valley topography with streams, rich soils, and a coastline protected by coral reefs. Such contrasting landforms were of great consequence to colonizing Oceanic peoples who, with their horticultural subsistence economy, preferred environments with ample water and good soils. Coral reefs and lagoons offered abundant fish and shellfish. The Oceanic peoples were keen observers of geological differences between islands, and they had their own folk theories about island origins. The Hawaiians, for example, recognized that their archipelago had originated progressively from the southeast, the abode of the volcano goddess Pele. The Pele-Hi‘iaka cycle of Hawaiian mythology (Beckwith 1970) describes Pele moving her home from Kaua‘i successively to the other islands, to end up at her present residence in the crater of Halemaumau on Hawai‘i.





TYPES OF ISLANDS


Different geological origins of island-arc and midplate islands provide one basis for classifying Pacific islands; another important variable is that of changes in an island’s landform resulting from submergence and the formation of coral reefs, or by later emergence of reefs. Geographers (e.g., Thomas 1963) have long used a simple four-part classification of Pacific islands that remains useful for understanding the varied island environments to which Pacific peoples adapted. The four island types are (1) island-arc islands; (2) high islands of midplate hot spot origin; (3) coral atolls, formed on volcanic masses that have subsided beneath the ocean’s surface; and (4) makatea islands, in which coral atoll or reef formations have been uplifted or have emerged through tectonic activity. The last three types of islands are illustrated in figure 2.2.




FIGURE 2.2 Examples of the principal geological types of islands. (After Kirch 1984a.)





ISLAND-ARC ISLANDSFormerly labeled “continental” islands because they sometimes incorporate ancient continental rocks, island-arc islands are among the largest islands in the Pacific, concentrated along the western margins of the Pacific basin.6 They include New Britain and New Ireland in the Bismarck Archipelago, the Solomon Islands, Vanuatu (formerly the New Hebrides), New Caledonia, Fiji, and New Zealand. Often large in scale, such islands exhibit varied habitats. On large islands such as New Guinea and New Britain, one finds interior-dwelling peoples who have no direct experience of the coast or the sea, although they trade with coastal populations. Because of their complex geological histories, these islands offer a wider range of lithic resources utilized by ancient Pacific peoples to manufacture stone tools. Rhyolite, dacite, metavolcanic rocks, chert, and obsidian are to be found in such island-arc locations.7


HIGH ISLANDSHigh islands are of midplate hot spot origin still in the earlier stages of their evolutionary cycle, before subsidence and erosion have done their work. They will eventually sink beneath the ocean surface to become atolls or seamounts. Tahiti, Rarotonga, Tutuila, and Pohnpei are typical examples of high islands. Most high islands consist of basalt, a hard, dense stone extensively used by Pacific peoples to make adzes and other implements. These islands range considerably in size, from Hawai‘i at 10,458 square kilometers down to Anuta, only 0.8 square kilometer in land area (fig. 2.3). They also vary greatly in landform, depending to a large degree on age and hence extent of erosion. Geologically younger islands often lack watercourses, whereas older islands are deeply dissected by stream valleys. Some high islands, such as ethnographically famous Tikopia, consist of volcanic cones with lakes in their exploded craters.




FIGURE 2.3 Anuta Island in the southeastern Solomons group is one of the smaller high islands in the Pacific but nevertheless home to about 160 Polynesians. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





When they are still young, high islands lack developed coral reefs and typically have wave-cut cliffs along their shorelines. In time, reefs develop along the shores of high islands, first as fringing reefs and later, as the island continues to subside, in the form of barrier reefs separated from the main island by a lagoon (fig. 2.4). However, coral reefs are confined to tropical and subtropical waters, because reef-building corals will not grow north or south of about 24° latitude. Gaps in the reef exist wherever there is substantial freshwater runoff, as at the mouths of large stream valleys. These passes were important, allowing canoes to move between the sheltered lagoon and the open sea.




FIGURE 2.4 The high island of Niuatoputapu in the northern Tongan group is surrounded by an extensive barrier reef and lagoon. In the distance looms Tafahi Island, a young volcanic cone lacking reefs. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





ATOLLSCharles Darwin (1842) first described the stages in the formation of a coral atoll, although he was at a loss to account for the geological mechanism of subsidence, now explained by plate tectonics. As islands migrate westward on the relentlessly moving Pacific Plate, they gradually subside and, in combination with erosion by wind and water, are slowly reduced. Reefs begin to develop around the island’s margin, so that in time an older high island will have a small volcanic core (or sometimes several small islets) surrounded by a lagoon and barrier reef, as with Aitutaki, Mangareva, and Chuuk. This stage of atoll formation is sometimes called an “almost atoll.” Eventually the remnant volcanic peak also becomes submerged, leaving only a ring of coral reef above the ocean. The coral continues to grow upward in the photic zone of the ocean; in time, coral heads and sand generated by storms and biological processes accumulate to form islets, called motu after their Polynesian name. The stages in atoll development are diagrammed in figure 2.5.




FIGURE 2.5 Stages in the development of an atoll, illustrated by block diagrams of various near-atolls and atolls. A, Naiau; B, Kambara; C, Fulanga; D, Tuvutha; E, Namuka; F, Ongea; G, Yangasa; H, Oneata; I, North Argo; J, Reid; K, Great Argo. (After Davis 1928.)





Atolls are among the most precarious environments settled by Pacific peoples.8 Atoll motu stand only 2 to 3 meters above sea level, making them vulnerable to inundation by waves and storm surges during cyclones. Atoll dwellers have learned that a thin lens of freshwater (the Ghyben-Herzberg aquifer) floats on the heavier salt water within the sandy sediments of motu and can be tapped by excavating shallow wells. Certain crops, such as the giant swamp taro (Cyrtosperma chamissonis), can be cultivated in pits dug down to expose this freshwater lens. In Micronesia, people developed salt-tolerant varieties of breadfruit that thrived in atoll conditions (Petersen 2009). However, cultivation on atolls is risky; many crops simply will not tolerate the saline conditions. On the other hand, atolls offer rich marine resources, and atoll peoples tend to be at home as much on and in the sea as on the land. Since atolls lack stone of any kind, their inhabitants have had to adapt their material culture, using coral and shell, especially the giant clam (Tridacna gigas), to make adzes and other tools.


MAKATEA ISLANDSWhen an atoll, or an old high island surrounded by a barrier reef, becomes elevated above sea level it forms a makatea island. Makatea is a Polynesian word meaning “white stone” (reef limestone); it is also the proper name of an island of this type in the Tuamotu Archipelago. Makatea islands are formed by tectonic uplift at plate margins, or by lithospheric flexure, in which a new volcanic hot spot island point-loads the thin oceanic crust, causing an upwarping at a certain distance from the hot spot. The heavy mass of Rarotonga in the Southern Cook Islands caused just such a warping of the oceanic crust, elevating several nearby islands as much as 80 meters above sea level. Some makatea islands are marginal environments for human habitation, lacking surface water (rainfall disappears immediately into the spongelike karst) and even soil for cultivation. One such is Henderson Island, yet prehistoric Polynesians managed to maintain a settlement there for six hundred years (see chap. 8).





CLIMATIC FACTORS IN THE PACIFIC


Pacific islands span the humid tropics to the temperate zones (New Zealand), but most of the islands considered here lie within the tropical to subtropical range. On some islands, high mountains create microclimates, as on Hawai‘i and Maui, where the higher elevations span humid temperate to alpine and tundra environments. The summits of both these islands receive snowfalls during the winter months, and Mauna Kea on Hawai‘i was capped by a small glacier during the late Pleistocene. New Guinea not only had Pleistocene glaciers, but exhibits even greater altitudinal climate differences.

The wind and current systems of the Pacific basin greatly influenced voyaging and settlement, and have important effects on island ecology.9 A wind and current map of the Pacific reveals two great gyres, or circulation cells, one each in the Northern and Southern Hemispheres (map 4). Both of these flow from east to west across the equatorial region (the intertropical convergence zone); the northern gyre flows clockwise, while the southern gyre flows counterclockwise. Given that the prevailing winds and currents are from the east throughout the main zone of tropical islands, Pacific navigators had to develop strategies for sailing from west to east (see chap. 7). There is some temporal variation, however, and the trade winds dominating the eastern Pacific are concentrated between May and September. In the western Pacific, there is a stronger seasonality between trade wind and monsoon seasons, influenced by the cyclic heating and cooling of the nearby Asian and Australian landmasses.




MAP 4 The dominant wind and current directions in the Pacific Ocean. (Adapted from Oliver 1989.)





The persistent trade winds, coming year after year from the same direction, have a major environmental effect on high islands in their path. As moisture-laden trades flow across the windward side of a high island, they are heated and rise, resulting in heavy rainfall (known as orographic precipitation). The island’s leeward side remains in a rain shadow, with significantly reduced rainfall. Over time, greater windward rainfall results in accelerated stream erosion and the creation of broad, amphitheater-headed valleys, while leeward landforms remain relatively dry and undissected. Lush rainforests cover windward slopes, while dryland shrub or parkland characterizes leeward slopes. These windward-leeward contrasts influenced where Oceanic peoples could establish their settlements and gardens. Not surprisingly, the Polynesians developed a terminology for this windward-leeward axis, tonga/tokerau, which they carried with them throughout their colonizations.10

To horticultural peoples such as those of Oceania, rainfall is critical to the sustenance of life. Rainfall over the Pacific gradually declines from west to east, with the Melanesian archipelagoes of the Bismarcks and Solomons receiving the greatest rainfall. However, there is sufficient rainfall throughout the zone of most islands to make horticulture feasible. The exceptions are along the equator (the Kiribati and Tuvalu Archipelagoes) and in northern Micronesia, where, for example, certain of the Marshall Islands suffer from periodic drought, making habitation tenuous.

El Niño and Southern Oscillation are terms that have become familiar to many people because of their effect on contemporary patterns of rainfall or drought in North and South America as well as Australia. The El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) process consists of a periodic thermodynamic redistribution of energy over the Pacific basin, resulting from differential heat buildup in the western Pacific. During an ENSO event, the normal aridity of the western-central Pacific is broken by periodic heavy rainfall episodes, trade winds decrease in intensity, and there is a significant eastward flow of warm water. ENSO events can have dramatic consequences for particular islands, including droughts in the western and central Pacific and heavy rains, floods, and increased cyclone frequency in the eastern Pacific. Fish populations, and the seabird colonies that depend on them for food, are sometimes devastated. These events presumably had serious impacts on Pacific island populations in the past.11





ISLAND LIFE AND BIOGEOGRAPHY


When a new volcanic island emerges above the ocean swells it consists merely of barren rock and cinder—but not for long. Plant colonization begins surprisingly quickly, with floating seeds washed ashore by the waves, or with tiny seeds passed through the digestive tracts of visiting birds. Insects and spiders arrive either under their own power or passively dispersed on high-altitude winds. Seabirds may colonize an island even if it is still relatively barren of plant life. Over a few hundred thousand years, the new island will have acquired a significant biota, its slopes cloaked in trees, shrubs, and herbs, inhabited by a diversity of invertebrates and of volant vertebrates (e.g., birds, fruit bats, or microbats).

Dispersal is one of the most fundamental aspects of Pacific biogeography.12 Groups of plants and animals that are good dispersers (because, for example, their seeds float and remain viable in salt water or because they are capable of over-water flight) have managed to colonize oceanic islands time and again. Other groups, such as most vertebrates, have found it generally impossible to get far beyond continental shores. Because of this, the biota of Pacific islands differs radically from that of continental regions, with certain groups well represented and others wholly lacking; biogeographers refer to this phenomenon as disharmony.

Distance—both from mainland source regions and between stepping-stone islands that aid in the dispersal process—is a critical aspect of island biogeography. The majority of Pacific islands are clustered in the ocean’s western sector; interisland distances increase dramatically as one moves eastward. Due to this uneven clustering of islands, Asia and New Guinea have served as primary source areas for Pacific floras and faunas, with a much lesser contribution from the Americas. Moreover, the large archipelagoes closest to Asia and New Guinea, such as the Solomons and Vanuatu, have much more diverse biotas, with greater numbers of higher-order taxa represented (genera and families), than islands in the central and eastern parts of the Pacific.

Another key aspect of island biogeography is the process of adaptive radiation. When a species of plant or animal succeeded in colonizing an isolated island, it often found itself without its former predators and in a position to exploit what ecologists call an empty niche. Generations descending from this original colonist multiplied and diverged as some offspring adapted and moved into different microhabitats. The classic textbook case of adaptive radiation is that of Darwin’s Galápagos finches, but numerous other examples abound throughout Oceania, such as the achatinellid and amastrid land snails of Hawai‘i or the cryptorhynchinid weevils of Rapa. Speciation on oceanic islands led to high degrees of endemism in certain islands and archipelagoes. In the Hawaiian Islands, for example, no less than 94 percent of all flowering plant species and 13 percent of the plant genera are endemic; that is, they are found nowhere else in the world.

These aspects of island biogeography, whose complexities I have only touched on, had important consequences for colonizing human populations. First, as people moved from the large Near Oceanic archipelagoes into Remote Oceania, they increasingly found the newly discovered islands lacking in many familiar plants and animals. Moreover, east of the Bismarcks, there were few indigenous plants with edible tubers or fruits; the would-be colonizers had to bring crop plants and other economic species with them. On the other hand, sizable populations of land birds and nesting seabirds offered a ready source of meat, a resource that was frequently hit very hard within the first few decades after human settlement (Steadman 2006).

Vegetation on Pacific islands varies tremendously according to island size and altitudinal range, but a few typical plant communities are noteworthy from the perspective of human use.13 In the tropical Pacific, the immediate coastal or strand vegetation is dominated by salt-tolerant trees such as Pandanus, whose leaves were widely used to weave mats; Barringtonia asiatica, whose fruit produces a poison used to catch fish; and the ubiquitous coconut palm (Cocos nucifera). A wild form of coconut had naturally dispersed at least as far as central Polynesia (Cook Islands and Society Islands) prior to human settlement, but domesticated varieties with their larger fruit were carried by would-be colonists in canoes.14 In the western Pacific, the coastal zone is often characterized by extensive mangrove (Rhizophora and other taxa) swamps or by lowland swamps dominated by the sago palm (Metroxylon spp.), which is harvested for its starchy pith. Moving away from the coast into the interior lowlands of island-arc or high islands, one enters rainforest, eventually giving way to even wetter montane and cloud forests on the higher islands. On some islands, the lower and middle elevations are dominated by grasslands or fernlands, especially in the drier leeward zones. This is the case over large parts of Viti Levu in Fiji (Ash 1992) and on New Caledonia, as well as on some smaller islands, such as Futuna, Mangaia, and Mangareva (fig. 2.6). These pyrophytic (fire-tolerant) savannas, characterized by Miscanthus grass or Dicranopteris ferns, often resulted from millennia of forest clearance and repeated burning by humans.




FIGURE 2.6 Large tracts in the interior of Futuna Island in Western Polynesia are covered in pyrophytic Dicranopteris fernlands, the result of former land use practices such as shifting cultivation. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





Island faunas, as I have noted, are frequently disharmonic in comparison to those of nearby continental regions.15 In the Pacific, mammals are limited to a few marsupials (e.g., wallabies, wombats, cuscus) and to several genera of rats (Rattus, Melomys, Uromys), along with fruit bats (Pteropodidae). Of these, only fruit bats dispersed into Remote Oceania, the marsupials and rats being restricted to the Bismarcks and the Solomon Islands. Reptiles too are of fairly limited distribution, with the majority of species of snakes, frogs, diurnal lizards, and geckos being found in Near Oceania or in the larger island-arc archipelagoes. Because of their excellent dispersal abilities, many kinds of birds managed to colonize Pacific islands, where they account for the greatest diversity of vertebrates. Seabirds (including frigates, shearwaters, petrels, noddies, and boobies) and various kinds of land birds (megapodes, pigeons, fruit doves, rails, and parrots, among them) were especially abundant on oceanic islands at the time of first human arrival (Steadman 1995, 2006). More-over, flightlessness developed in a repeated evolutionary process on many Pacific islands, especially in various genera of rails.

The richest terrestrial faunal diversity on Pacific islands was among the invertebrates, including insects and land snails, but most of these were of little direct consequence or interest to people. However, a large and highly delect-able land crab, Birgus latro, the “coconut robber” crab, was a prized food of islanders. The presence of certain insects—most of all the Anopheles mosquitoes, which are vectors for several kinds of Plasmodium, the cause of malaria—also had health implications for humans.

The Pacific islands were not rich in edible terrestrial species other than birds and fruit bats, and their floras were particularly impoverished in terms of edible plants.
