Although as Leach and Davidson (2008:322) point out that “influences from the central Pacific could have been arriving on Taumako at irregular intervals since the time of first settlement,” it seems likely that the incorporation of Polynesian-language speakers into the Taumako population was a phenomenon of the past thousand years.

Contact between Polynesian and Melanesian groups occurred not just in the southeastern Solomons but also in Vanuatu, the Loyalty Islands, and New Caledonia. Spriggs (1997:187–222) discusses the social and cultural effects resulting from interaction between Polynesian Outlier populations and the peoples of eastern Melanesia. Although this is not conclusive, both Garanger and Spriggs attribute the Roy Mata burial site to a Polynesian immigrant group. Although the five documented Polynesian Outliers in central and southern Vanuatu (Emae, Mele, Fila, Aniwa, West Futuna) have yet to be investigated in any detail, they may have had considerable cultural influences during the past 500 years of Vanuatu history. In the Loyalty Islands, a migration from ‘Uvea in Western Polynesia resulted in the takeover of Ouvea Island by Polynesian speakers. Sand (1995:203–12) suggests that the Xetriwaan group of people with immediate origins in southern Vanuatu, who were influential both on Lifou and on La Grande Terre proper, may have had Polynesian associations. As Spriggs (1977:221) remarks, “The migrants inserted themselves within the indigenous economic systems and took them over from the inside.”





ETHNOGENESIS IN LA GRANDE TERRE


At the southeastern terminus of Melanesia lies La Grande Terre (New Caledonia), a long, skinny remnant of ancient Gondwanaland stretching 400 kilometers from northwest to southeast (the total land area is 24,436 square kilometers). To the east, the Loyalty Island chain, comprising Ouvea, Lifou, and Maré, lies about 100 kilometers distant. These islands were administratively joined to La Grande Terre when the French claimed “possession” in 1853, but even prior to European influence the societies of the two island groups had close contacts and relationships.

New Caledonia’s mountainous terrain and mineral-rich soils (it is one of the world’s main sources of nickel) support a unique biota with Gondwanan roots. In the late second millennium B.C., when Lapita voyagers sailed their canoes through the island’s marvelous barrier reef lagoons and hauled ashore, they encountered such bizarre creatures as giant flightless megapodes, terrestrial crocodiles, varanid lizards, and large horned tortoises. These and other creatures, lacking any fear of the new bipedal predators, did not withstand the human onslaught for more than a few centuries; we know them today only from subfossils and remains in archaeological contexts.41 Unlike La Grande Terre, the Loyalty Islands consist of upraised makatea limestone. Maré and Lifou have cliff-bound coasts and extensive flat plateaus with arable soils formed by decomposition of the limestone and organically enriched.

In this new and biotically strange land the Lapita colonists of La Grande Terre and the Loyalties—and the generations of descendants they spawned—gave rise to a remarkable diversity of societies and language groups.42 While all of the languages spoken in these islands are of the Oceanic branch of Austronesian, there are thirty-seven distinct languages classified within seven primary subgroups. And although there is much cultural homogeneity (in material culture, for example, and in subsistence practices), the islands’ populations seem to have consciously differentiated among themselves sociopolitically and ethnically. New Caledonia thus challenges the anthropologist with the extent of ethnogenesis that occurred within a mere three millennia.

This linguistic and cultural diversity intrigued the pioneer ethnographers, who were at a loss to explain it other than through numerous and repeated migrations by groups with varied ethnic and biological origins.43 E.W. Gifford and his student Dick Shutler Jr. carried out the first modern archaeological excavations on La Grande Terre in 1952 (Sand and Kirch 2002). Finding no evidence of a “pre-pottery cultural level,” they rejected any notion of a “Paleolithic stage of culture” in the archipelago’s history (Gifford and Shutler 1956:94). While this should have been the end of the matter, when Jack Golson as well as Luc Chevalier discovered earthen mounds or “tumuli” on the Île des Pins, with radiocarbon ages as old as 10,950 B.C., the question of a preceramic, pre-Austronesian occupation was resurrected. Based on excavations in these mounds by Chevalier (1963) and Golson (1963), Green (1988) came to the realization that the “tumuli” were nonhuman artifacts created by the extinct giant megapode (Sylviornis neocaledoniae). These birds are known to scrape up heaps of earth and leaf litter with their powerful legs, in which they incubate their eggs (pieces of egg shell as well as terrestrial Placostylus snails were found in the mounds).

Thanks to the energetic work of French archaeologists—including Daniel Frimigacci, Jean-Christophe Galipaud, and Christophe Sand—our knowledge of New Caledonian prehistory has improved by leaps and bounds, as extensive excavations and surveys have been carried out throughout the archipelago.44 As elsewhere in the Remote Oceanic parts of island Melanesia, initial settlement of the archipelago was by Lapita people. An extensive series of radiocarbon dates (Sand 2010; Sand, Bolé, and Ouétcho 2002) now fixes the date of first settlement of New Caledonia and the Loyalty Islands between 1100 and 1050 B.C. The initial Lapita Phase, characterized by diverse vessel forms decorated with the classic dentate-stamped technique, ended by 800 B.C.

The production of pottery, however, did not cease with the end of Lapita proper. As Sand, Bolé, and Ouétcho (2011:59) write, “The end part of the Lapita phase is characterised by an increase and diversification of incised motifs, the appearance of a series of simplified dentate-stamped motifs, and the development of more shell-impression in some sites.” The New Caledonian ceramic tradition began to differentiate, with new vessel forms and decorative styles emerging and with the northern and southern sectors of La Grande Terre exhibiting different trajectories. These different ceramic styles provide the basis for a cultural sequence of four periods (fig 5.18). Aside from providing important chronological signposts, the sequence of New Caledonian ceramics offers a material index to the ongoing processes of ethnogenesis or polygenesis.




FIGURE 5.18 The ceramic sequence of New Caledonia, according to Sand et al. (2011). (Courtesy of Christophe Sand.)





Following Lapita, the Koné Period is marked first by the emergence of Podtanean pottery, characterized by paddle-impressing, “mostly on thin-walled pots with light carination and rims with a flat lip” (Sand, Bolé, and Ouétcho 2011:60).45 Regional differences first emerge within the Koné Period, with the incised Puen Tradition in the south46 and the Pindai Tradition in the north, the latter marked by “simple ovoid pots with incurved rims, bearing shell-impressed and applied clayband decorations” (60).

The Koné Period ends around 2,000 years ago, and although the transition may not have been simultaneous throughout the archipelago, the succeeding Naïa Period starts at the beginning of the second millennium A.D. and continues until about A.D. 1000. In the northern part of La Grande Terre, a new Balabio ceramic tradition emerges, with roots in the preceding Pindai pottery. Balabio pots are mostly characterized by simple shell impression and incision. In the south, the Puen Tradition gives rise to the Plum Tradition, which has round-bottomed vessels with handles under the rim, decorated with often crude incising (Sand, Bolé, and Ouétcho 2011:62). Noting the design similarities between Plum ceramics and a style of petroglyphs, Sand (1995:144) suggests that the rock art might have served as boundary markers, possibly a reflection of growing ethnolinguistic differentiation within the island.

The ceramic chronology enters its final phase at the beginning of the second millennium A.D., with what Sand, Bolé, and Ouétcho (2011) call the Traditional Kanak Cultural Complex, which continues into the “ethnographic present” following European contact. The north-south divide continues, with the Nera Tradition emerging out of Plum in the south and with the Oundjo Tradition in the north.47 The Nera Tradition is marked by spherical vessels lacking everted rims, with simple decorations consisting of rows of nubbins and some incising. The Oundjo Tradition has pots with incised, stamped, and relief decorations on the upper parts of the globular pots, below the rim. Indeed, it seems likely that ceramics, as well as other forms of material culture, were actively manipulated by local peoples as symbols of ethnic identity.

This kind of social differentiation, it should be said, had as one underlying basis the growth and expansion of the archipelago’s population (fig. 5.19). The early Koné Period, which lasted perhaps 1,200 years, witnessed the gradual expansion of human settlement throughout New Caledonia and the Loyalty archipelago, at first concentrated along the coasts and in the more horticulturally suited humid sectors but toward the end of the period expanding into interior valleys. Sand (1995:112–13) estimates that by the close of the Koné Period the island’s aggregate population had reached 50,000. Stratigraphic evidence from the Bopope region indicates that by the first few centuries of the Christian era, even interior valleys were permanently occupied, with landscapes increasingly transformed by human actions.




FIGURE 5.19 Major trends in the evolution of New Caledonian culture, according to Sand (1996a).





Significantly, the earliest material signals of social transformation—specifically, indications of hierarchy, competition, and warfare—are found not on La Grande Terre but on the central plain of Maré Island. Two monumental stone structures, built of coral blocks weighing up to 5 tons each, were defensive sites or forts; the rectangular enclosure of Hnakudotit (site LMA016) incorporates 23,000 cubic meters of stone (fig. 5.20). This remarkable construction dates to the first two or three centuries A.D. According to Sand (1995:133), these structures indicate the prior existence of a “political system . . . very different from that known ethnographically in New Caledonia.” The ecological setting of these structures is critical: the upraised, karst limestone plateau of Maré Island is a “dry” and somewhat marginal environment for an agriculturally based economy. The model of contrastive “wet and dry” agroecological regimes proposed for Polynesia by Kirch (1994) predicts that in such environments—where the choice of agricultural strategies is highly constrained toward labor-intensive short-fallow swidden—competition for land and aggressive territorial warfare are most likely to arise. It is not surprising then to find these early indications of competition and territorial marking on Maré, rather than on La Grande Terre, which had a much greater potential for alternative forms of intensification, such as terracing.




FIGURE 5.20 Stacked walls of massive limestone boulders make up the Hnakudotit fortification site on Maré, Loyalty Islands. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





Yet if the first signals of ecological and/or demographic stress—and consequent sociopolitical response—arose on the makatea plateaus of the Loyalties, other kinds of intensification soon appeared on the main island. Evidence from sediment cores reveals that the initial Lapita colonists, practicing shifting cultivation, had a noticeable impact on New Caledonia’s vegetation, while the faunal records show that several endemic animals, including the large flightless megapode, a terrestrial crocodile, and the giant horned tortoise, rapidly went extinct.48 But as population increased, the cumulative effects of human actions on the landscape mounted. La Grande Terre has long been noted for its permanent landscape transformation, or what Blaikie and Brookfield (1987) called landesque capital intensification. These include taro irrigation systems—the most extensive in all of Oceania—as well as Dioscorea yam mound systems.49 Anyone who has viewed the magnificently sculpted hillsides of the Col de la Pirogue, with row upon row of descending terraces for taro planting irrigated by a complex network of canals and ditches (fig. 5.21), must be impressed at the scale of landscape manipulation achieved by this preindustrial society.




FIGURE 5.21 Flights of formerly irrigated taro terraces, now in grasslands, cover large sections of the New Caledonian landscape. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





Archaeology has offered some indications of the prehistory of these impressive landscapes. Excavations in taro terraces (site WPT069) at the famous Col de la Pirogue irrigation complex near Nouméa yielded a radiocarbon age of A.D. 670–990, the oldest date for a Melanesian irrigation complex.50 Other terraces gave more recent ages, within the last two or three centuries. The Païta data suggest that intensive, canal-fed irrigation was a development of the middle to later phases of island sequences, linked both to demographic pressures and to sociopolitical transformations. Of equal note are vast areas of ancient dryland terracing and mounding that mark formerly intensive systems of “dry” cultivation, focused primarily on Dioscorea yams. The New Caledonian dryland systems rival in scale and complexity those of the New Guinea Highlands. At Tiwaka, a single dryland system includes more than 150 large mounds, totaling at least 35 hectares. Mounds may be 1 to 3 meters high, 10 meters wide, and up to several hundred meters long. The prehistory of these dryland systems remains a problem awaiting further research.

New forms of inland habitation arose during the Naïa Period and the following Traditional Kanak period, in close spatial association with the intensive agricultural systems. While the coastal regions—marked by extensive shell middens—continued to be occupied, the interior valleys and coastal plains came to be dotted with elevated house mounds, frequently organized in linear arrangements of fifteen or more around a central alleyway. The plan of Tipalet (site ETO018) in the Bopope region (fig. 5.22) shows such mounds on either side of an elongate plaza, with a substantially larger mound at each end. Other habitation mound complexes reflect a hierarchy of mound sizes and heights, possible evidence for social stratification or at least for ranking of households. Few such complexes have been excavated, but a charcoal sample from the base of a mound at Pwapwadouhi yielded a calibrated radiocarbon age of A.D. 1290–1470.




FIGURE 5.22 Plan of house mounds at Tipalet in the Bopope region of New Caledonia. (Courtesy Christophe Sand.)





According to Sand (1995:200), “It is the intensification of horticulture which appears the most characteristic” of the last two thousand years of New Caledonian prehistory. He accepts a primary causal role for demographic pressure but cautions that population growth by itself is too limiting an explanatory factor. Indeed, the very existence of these intensive agricultural systems—which are found throughout the interior valleys and plains of New Caledonia—provoked Sand to question long-held ethnographic notions concerning traditional Kanak societies (e.g., Guiart 1963). Rather than regard the sociopolitical systems associated with these intensive systems of agricultural production as “weak chiefdoms,” and the sites themselves as only intermittently occupied by a peripatetic population, Sand asks whether the archaeological evidence might not better support an interpretation of “strong chiefdoms” bolstered by substantial surplus extraction and driven by prestige rivalry.

How can we account for the disparity between the New Caledonian archaeological record, which speaks to the former existence of highly complex sociopolitical formations, and twentieth-century ethnography, which refers at best to “weak” chiefdoms? The answer—still somewhat contentious—may lie in a demographic collapse of hitherto-unappreciated magnitude, immediately after European contact. The crux of the argument is as follows. Based on a narrow reading of the available textual sources—and in particular favoring census data that did not become available until after missionization in the mid-nineteenth century (more than sixty years after initial European contact)—academic demographers maintained that the precontact population of La Grande Terre never exceeded 50,000. In contrast, archaeological data (while too thin to attempt regional population estimates) suggest that the latter figure is way too low to account for the density and intensity of the agricultural and residential landscape. In a case study of the Païta region near Nouméa, Sand (1995) shows that while the ethnologist Guiart estimated the maximum population at perhaps 1,200 persons, the archaeological vestiges of intensive terracing and habitation lead one to a conservative estimate of 8,000, with a strong likelihood of double that number. Sand, Bolé, and Ouétcho (2007:325) conclude that even the estimate by the geographer J.C. Roux of 80 to 100,000 persons for New Caledonia at European contact “is far too low.”

How can we account for the disparity in estimates? Sand argues that not only the effects of diseases for which the indigenous Kanaks had no resistance but also the political and military strategies of several decades of whalers, sandalwooders, and outright colonists had far greater impacts than hitherto recognized. The French colonials pursued a strategy of outright destruction of agricultural infrastructure, leading to famine and social collapse. Sand’s dissection of the arguments of Norma McArthur (1968) for low population densities deserves to be read by all anthropologists and historians working in Melanesia. In Sand’s words, “To appreciate the extent of the demographic collapse is to revisit the autopsy of the destruction of a society” (1995:248).





FIJI: AN ARCHIPELAGO “IN BETWEEN”


Fiji offers striking contrasts yet intriguing similarities with New Caledonia (map 9). The archipelagoes are of comparable scale (24,436 square kilometers for New Caledonia, 18,272 for Fiji) and have been inhabited for the same length of time (they were both settled by Lapita peoples), but Fiji’s history does not display the emphasis on small-scale ethnolinguistic differentiation seen in La Grande Terre and the Loyalties. There is, to be sure, intra-archipelago diversity within Fiji, but this is more limited. For example, the Fijian peoples speak only two main dialects, Western and Eastern Fijian; there is a third discrete language on isolated Rotuma Island.51 Culturally, there were important differences between coastal and offshore island dwellers and the “bush people” of the interiors of Viti Levu and Vanua Levu. In addition, the people of the Lau Islands had their own distinctive characteristics, in part derived from regular contact and interaction with Tonga.




MAP 9 The Fiji archipelago, showing the location of key archaeological sites.





Anthropologists have never quite known how to deal with Fiji. It is a sort of “between” archipelago, situated geographically closer to Western Polynesia (with whose societies there were important historical interactions), yet usually classified as a “Melanesian” culture. Of course, such nineteenth-century classifications are of little real historical or analytical value, and perhaps the Fijian case is the best example (see the introduction). Settled at the close of the second millennium B.C.—by a founding Lapita population that quickly expanded to colonize nearby Tonga and other Western Polynesian islands—Fiji shares the same foundation culture with Western Polynesia. At the same time, Fiji continued in later millennia to receive both genetic and cultural influences from the west (i.e., from “Melanesia”), inputs that in part set its historical trajectory apart from those of Tonga, Samoa, or Futuna. There is ample evidence—archaeological, linguistic, and cultural—that the peoples of Fiji, Tonga, Samoa, Futuna, Lau, and other smaller islands in this part of the central Pacific had regular exchanges (material, social, and genetic) throughout prehistory. Thus, in contrast to New Caledonia (which, while not wholly isolated, is set apart in its corner of the southwestern Pacific), Fiji was a sort of “between place”—a foyer of exchange and interaction.

Gifford (1951) pioneered archaeological excavation in Fiji, focusing on the main island of Viti Levu. He dug two sites: Navatu (site 17) on the north coast under the shadow of the imposing volcanic monolith Uluinavatu, an important place in Fijian origin traditions; and Vunda (site 26), near Lautoka. Both sites yielded materials spanning the last two millennia of Fijian prehistory, and later excavations, in the 1960s and 1970s, at the Sigatoka dunes, the Yanuca rockshelter, Natunuku, Naigani Island, and other sites filled in earlier phases of the sequence.52 The Sigatoka site, a sprawling and complex stratified dune of alluvial sands at the mouth of Sigatoka Valley, has continued to yield materials important to defining the Fijian sequence.53 The Australian National University’s Early Prehistory of Fiji Project (1996–2000) added a wealth of new data on early sites, landscape transformations, and faunal changes (G. Clark and Anderson, eds., 2009). In addition to the focus on Lapita and early sites, much emphasis has been placed on inland settlements and fortifications, in such areas as the Rewa and Navua deltas and the Sigatoka River valley on Viti Levu and on Taveuni, Wakaya, and Lakeba Islands.54 Nonetheless, given its geographic scale and number of islands, the Fiji archipelago remains archaeologically underexplored.

Gifford (1951:189) observed that pottery is the “chief characteristic of Fijian archaeology.” As in New Caledonia, ceramics are found throughout the prehistoric sequence and continued to be manufactured after European contact (MacLachlan 1940; Thompson 1938). Based on his excavations at Navatu and Vunda, Gifford defined an early period with “relief”-decorated sherds (fig. 5.23) and a late period with dominantly “incised” sherds. Green (1963b) later showed that Gifford had missed the first third of the Fijian sequence, marked by Lapita pottery, and renamed the phases as follows: Sigatoka (ca. 1200–100 B.C.), Navatu (100 B.C.–A.D. 1100), Vunda (A.D. 1100–1800), and, after European contact, the Ra Phase. This terminology, described by Frost (1979), has been adopted by most investigators and remains a useful culture-historical schema for the archipelago.55




FIGURE 5.23 A paddle-impressed pot from the Sigatoka dune site, Fiji. (After Birks 1973.)





The Fijian archipelago was settled as a part of the Lapita expansion into Remote Oceania at the close of the second millennium B.C. (see chap. 4). Bayesian calibration of radiocarbon dates from the sites of Bourewa, Naigani, and Yanuca put initial settlement at 1100 to 1000 B.C. (G. Clark and Anderson, eds., 2009:175).56 From Fiji, the Lapita expansion continued—seemingly without significant pause—eastward into Tonga and Samoa; contacts between these groups are indicated both in archaeological materials and by historical linguistic evidence.57 As elsewhere in the Eastern Lapita region, decoration of pottery with dentate stamping rapidly declined, so that Sigatoka Phase assemblages of the first millennium B.C. consist largely of plain wares, although with some paddle impressing.

The transition from Sigatoka to Navatu Phases is fairly gradual in terms of the ceramics, marked by an increase in carved-paddle impressions on globular vessels. Shaw, Frost, and others view such ceramic changes as the result of local stylistic developments. However, stylistic changes marking the boundary between the Navatu and Vunda Phases at roughly A.D. 1100 are more abrupt, with the introduction of a whole new array of incised designs. Archaeologists have debated whether these changes reflect external influences, such as the movement or migration of a new group of people into Fiji at this time, most likely from Vanuatu. Green, Bellwood (1979:264–65), and others regarded such a population intrusion likely, pointing to close stylistic similarities between Vunda Phase ceramics and those of the Mangaasi complex of Vanuatu.58 The appearance of Vanuatu obsidian in rockshelters on Lakeba Island around A.D. 250 supports this interpretation (Best 1984: 643). In contrast, Rechtman (1992) expressed profound skepticism about explanations that invoke external influences or migrations, preferring to see all change in the Fijian sequence as stemming from internal dynamics (see also Bedford and Spriggs 2008).

Burley and Edinborough (2014; see also Burley 2005) show that the transition from plain ware to Navatu Phase ceramics at Sigatoka was abrupt, with the interval between these phases having a duration of no more than forty-three years. Given the magnitude of ceramic differences between these phases, they argue that “the abruptness of this change can be explained only by exogenous replacement” (2014:295). Burley (2013) has revisited the issue of Fijian “polygenesis,” and the question of how—given a common Lapita ancestry for Fijians and Polynesians—“Fijians became Fijian and not Polynesians.” Whereas Burley sees the gradual transition from early dentate-stamped Lapita to later plain ware ceramics as a gradual continuum, the sudden appearance of Navatu-style pottery can only be explained, he argues, by intrusion of a new group of people: “The Navatu-phase ceramic assemblage represents an abrupt change, one that succinctly marks a different population of potters living on this site. Whether this population originates ultimately from central Island Melanesia or represents a population movement from within the Fijian archipelago is a central question” (Burley 2013:445). However, given the strong similarities between Navatu ceramics and pottery of similar age in Vanuatu, such a connection seems highly likely.

The cultural developments of the Vunda Phase—roughly the last thousand years—include fascinating parallels with New Caledonia in the extensive modification of landscapes for food production, yet striking differences in residential patterns. Excavations in fortified sites on Taveuni and Wakaya Islands demonstrate that these were a dominant feature of the Vunda Phase. Perhaps the most common form is the “ring-ditch” style enclosing a cluster of habitation sites, the latter often elevated on earthen or stone-faced mounds. Terraced fortifications are present in more hilly topography. Figure 5.24 illustrates a particularly complex ring-ditch site at Lomolomo in the Sigatoka River valley. Ring-ditch fortifications are ubiquitous in low-lying terrain, as on the fertile deltas and floodplains of the Rewa, Navua, Sigatoka, and other drainages. Parry (1977, 1981, 1987) identified large numbers of fortified sites, closely associated with extensive agricultural remains, using aerial photographs. The agricultural remains include reticulate systems of raised beds and intervening ditches, called vuci in Fijian, used to grow Colocasia taro as well as giant swamp taro (Cyrtosperma chamissonis). In hilly areas such as the interior of the Sigatoka Valley, extensive pondfield terraces cascade down hillsides, similar to those in New Caledonia (Parry 1987:pl. 20).




FIGURE 5.24 Plan of a village fortified with ring-ditches, at Lomolomo (site VL 16/57) in the Rewa Delta of Viti Levu, Fiji. (After Parry 1987.)





In an extensive GIS-based survey of the Sigatoka drainage, Field (1998, 2003, 2004) described three modes of habitation/subsistence: territorial strongholds, remote refuges, and constructed fortifications on the valley bottom. Test excavations at various sites indicate that inland habitation and agriculture in the valley did not begin until many centuries after Lapita colonization, between 20 B.C. and A.D. 80. Fortifications making use of natural topography first appear around A.D. 600 and continue throughout the sequence. Roos, Field, and Dudgeon (2016) used stratigraphic records of charcoal and carbon stable isotopes obtained from a core in the lower Sigatoka Valley, finding that “archaeological indicators of resource stress co-occur with persistent swiddening after 1450 cal BP, although these precede the collapse into degraded grassland conditions at 1000 cal BP” (535). Extensive agricultural modifications, along with shifting cultivation, heightened erosion rates in the Sigatoka Valley, leading to increased sediment loads in the Sigatoka River and resulting in the formation of the massive dune field now located at the valley mouth, itself an important archaeological site (Anderson et al. 2006; Dickinson et al. 1998).

The cultural processes associated with the construction of the literally thousands of fortified sites that appeared over the Fijian landscape in the Vunda Phase have been debated. Frost (1974, 1979) invoked the arrival of an immigrant population, bringing the new incised ceramic styles, who “clashed” with the indigenous people, leading to a major period of fort building and warfare. However, while abrupt ceramic changes at around A.D. 1100 may have resulted from external migration, they need not be directly linked to the settlement pattern changes, for which internal demographic, economic, and sociopolitical processes provide more likely explanations. Parry (1981, 1987) and Rechtman (1992) argue that the intensified landscape modifications of later Fijian prehistory are linked to increasing population density, competition for resources (especially prime agricultural lands), and an involutionary spiral of intergroup warfare. Roos, Field, and Dudgeon (2016:550) likewise point to the cumulative effects of landscape transformation: “It is in the context of degraded landscapes of the last millennium that evidence for socio-political inequality is most robust, in the context of further population growth and intercommunity conflict in the last 500–700 years. This pattern generally conforms to the scenario described by Kirch (1994, 2010), in which landscape degradation from swidden intensification drives cycles of interpolity conflict and socio-political complexity.” Field (2004) and Field and Lape (2010) have drawn attention to climate change effects, such as the Little Ice Age (A.D. 1300–1850) and pronounced ENSO conditions, as drivers of change in settlement pattern and fortification. Field (2004:95), for example, notes that in the Sigatoka Valley, the founding of “environmental refuge sites” corresponds temporally with the onset of the Little Ice Age, when cooler and drier conditions may have exacerbated drought and food scarcity.

Rechtman (1992:46–52) regarded the origin of fortified sites in Fiji as owing to “internal development,” spurred in the first instance by population growth and by an increased need for resources. In his view, these processes led, after A.D. 1100, to interregional contact and conflict, marked both by fortification and by the proliferation of ceramic styles, the latter presumably as an index of increased social or ethnic differentiation. As conquest warfare became increasingly common, Rechtman believes, a shift occurred in leadership, from ideological control by sacred leaders to secular control based on force. A corollary of this change was the development of highly institutionalized cannibalism.

While some anthropologists have expressed skepticism that cannibalism actually existed anywhere in the world, the case for cannibalism in late Fijian prehistory seems particularly sound. Gifford (1951:208) observed in his usual terse fashion that “man was the most popular of the vertebrate animals used for food” based on the faunal materials from his Navatu and Vunda excavations. Reanalysis of Gifford’s Navatu collections using modern criteria for evaluating cannibalistic practices confirms his view (DeGusta 1999, 2000). Based on faunal and taphonomic studies of human materials from Wakaya Island, Rechtman is equally convinced that the archaeological evidence supports cannibalism as a major practice in later Fijian prehistory. In his Lakeba Island sites, Best (1984:534) found that bones of Homo sapiens could be interpreted as “food remains,” based on frequent charring, cut marks, and “the underrepresentation of hands, feet, and heads.” Of course, the contact-period historical sources speak to the ritualized and extensive nature of Fijian anthropophagy, which until recent years has not been questioned. In my view, the issue is not whether the Fijians practiced cannibalism but why such practices should have become so pervasive and institutionalized. They may represent a kind of “competitive involution” in sociopolitical evolution parallel to that hypothesized for the Marquesas Islands (Kirch 1991a), where cannibalism is also evidenced archaeologically (see chap. 7).

To the east of the main islands of Fiji, the Lau group consists of dozens of smaller islands (see map 9), some of which have been archaeologically studied. Best (1984) investigated Lakeba, a nearly circular high island of 56 square kilometers in central Lau. Lakeba, as with other Lau islands, had important proto-historic connections to Tonga. Best’s survey of 209 sites and his excavations at six of these produced a cultural sequence commencing in the early Lapita period (ca. 1000 B.C.) that displays many of the key stylistic changes in ceramics found elsewhere in Fiji. Best suggests that population numbers were low in the early phase (settlement may even have been seasonal or intermittent for the first two centuries) but began to increase significantly in his Period II (ca. 500 B.C.), as marked by “a spectacular spread of sites across the landscape” (1984: 592). Given this inland expansion, there is by A.D. 1 a perhaps not surprising degree of environmental degradation, with a “regime of inland burn-off.”59 A population maximum was reached by about A.D. 1000, by which time large forts such as Ulunikoro (site 101/7/47) were constructed. Thus, in the Lakeba sequence, the rise of both forts and cannibalism came after a phase of initial population expansion and possible overexploitation of agricultural resources.

S. Jones (2009; Jones, Steadman, and O’Day 2007) augmented our understanding of Lau prehistory with ethnoarchaeological research on the small islands of Nayau, Aiwa Levu, and Aiwa Lailai, situated north and south of larger Lakeba. Jones’s research focused on marine exploitation and subsistence and on gender roles. Jones and Quinn (2009) used stable isotopes from human skeletal remains combined with faunal analysis to determine that throughout prehistory the Lauan diet was composed of 60 to 70 percent plant foods (primarily root crops), with the remainder made up of reef fishes, shellfish, and reptiles.

Clark and Cole (1997) used archaeological survey combined with palynological analysis of sediment cores to construct a tentative cultural and environmental history of Totoya Island, in the Yasayasa Moala group southeast of Viti Levu. Initial settlement was again by people with Lapita ceramics, “perhaps close to 3000 years ago” (1997:150). Human-induced forest clearance began shortly after colonization, leading to the expansion of grasslands and secondary forest. Settlement was concentrated around the coastal areas until about 50 B.C. to A.D. 250, when there was a “significant move inland and the establishment of inland villages” (1997:152). This change in settlement patterns is coupled with another “round of substantial vegetation disturbance.” Finally, in the second millennium A.D., the final decline of the inland forest—leading to extensive grasslands covering the central hills—corresponds to a period when hilltop forts were constructed at several locations. Clark and Cole (1997:152) suggest that “the expansion of warfare on the island may have been related in part to a large population and pressure on limited resources.”

While internal dynamics such as population growth and resource competition were surely key factors in the transformation of Fijian society over the course of three millennia, there were also external connections that linked Fijian populations with adjacent islands and archipelagoes. In Lapita sites of Fiji, there is evidence for the movement of obsidian, chert, stone adzes, pottery, and other material items between islands, some from outside the Fijian archipelago itself. Fijian potsherds have occasionally been found in Tongan sites,60 and in the proto-historic period there is ample ethnographic and oral tradition evidence for a complex network of exchange relationships linking Fiji with Tonga and Samoa.61 The people of Lau, in particular, served as intermediaries between the chiefly lineages of Tonga and Fiji, and groups of Tongan canoe builders were allowed to take up residence in Lau in order to have access to large timber trees.62 Such regular and frequently systematized contacts no doubt facilitated the spread of innovations ranging from the material to the ideological, and account for parallel cultural developments in Fijian and Western Polynesian cultures.63





LARGER THEMES IN MELANESIAN PREHISTORY


In this concluding section, I draw attention to three themes that run through the prehistory of this vast region, cross-cutting geographical divisions. These are certainly not the only patterns that others may find of interest, but to my mind they are salient.


CHANGE IN CERAMIC SEQUENCESArchaeologists are interested in ceramic change, not only because pottery styles are sensitive temporal indicators and thus form the bases of chronology, but also because ceramic styles may indicate cultural connections over space and time. Throughout island Melanesia, a well-marked ceramic horizon—Lapita—links the entire region at the beginning of the first millennium B.C. Lapita proper, in the sense of dentate-stamped ceramics, did not persist beyond the first two to three centuries, giving way to ceramics decorated with new techniques. In some localities, ceramic production is abandoned altogether after about A.D. 1–200, although pots were frequently imported from neighboring groups who continued to manufacture pottery. This is the case in both Mussau and Tikopia. More interesting, however, are the parallel style changes that occurred from the Bismarcks to Vanuatu and New Caledonia and that are echoed to some degree in Fiji. These are the decline and abandonment of the classic Lapita dentate-stamped technique of decoration and its replacement, initially with paddle impressing and then mostly with incised designs but also with designs executed using other techniques, such as end-tool impressing, fingernail impressing, relief, and appliqué. The Mangaasi ceramic complex of central Vanuatu was one of the first such post-Lapita ceramic groups to be identified, although Garanger (1972a) was uncertain of its relationship to Lapita proper. With the discovery of parallel sequences in Mussau, Manus, New Ireland, Watom, Nissan-Buka, and New Caledonia, however, we now know that Mangaasi is only one of a number of such ceramic transitions. The question thus arises whether these developments—most of which seem to occur in the period from about 550 B.C. to A.D. 250—were in any way related.

One possibility is that similarities in these style changes reflect continued interisland and intersocietal contacts and thus the sharing of technical and stylistic innovations. But incising as a technique was certainly a part of the older Lapita design repertoire; notably, it is far less labor-intensive than dentate stamping. Thus the shift from dentate stamping to incising throughout island Melanesia beginning around 550 B.C. and continuing until about A.D. 250 may simply reflect common responses on the part of potters to the changing social and economic roles and values of ceramics. As the initially widespread Lapita network began to break apart into a series of geographically less extensive, regional networks, the high value formerly placed on the elaborately decorated forms of Lapita pots could have decreased, and indeed the role of ceramics in exchange systems is likely to have declined. Since there is continuity in motifs from Lapita ceramics to the early phases of incised (“Mangaasoid,” or Mangaasi-like) pottery, it may be that potters were no longer interested in, or willing to invest long hours in, decorating their pots.

The shift to incised pottery occurred later in Fiji than in the Melanesian archipelagoes to the west. This raises the question of whether the change in Fiji—which did not proceed, as elsewhere, directly from dentate stamping to incising but rather via an intermediate phase dominated by paddle-impressed pottery—was the result of a population movement or intrusion, possibly from Vanuatu or New Caledonia. This is the position of Best (1984), Frost (1979), Green (1963b), and, most recently, Burley (2005, 2013) but one that is contested by others such as Rechtman (1992) who prefer to see the Fijian sequence in isolationist terms. No doubt the debate will continue. Much more basic field and laboratory work must be done to fill out the ceramic sequences of Melanesia before these fundamental questions will be definitively answered. We must always keep in mind, however, that ceramics have as much to tell us of ancient cultural and social processes as they do of mere chronology.


DEVELOPMENT OF SPECIALIZED TRADE AND EXCHANGE NETWORKSAnother recurrent theme in the prehistory of the “New” Melanesia concerns the rise of highly intensified forms of external exchange and of high-volume specialist trade.64 Several examples are (often still incompletely) known from the archaeological record, including the coastal Papuan systems with their pottery-producing central places (Mailu or Motupore), the antecedents of the ethnographic kula in the Massim, the Siassi middlemen traders, the “red feather money” network of the Santa Cruz Islands and Taumako, the mid–first millennium A.D. network(s) linking Tikopia and Vanikoro to northern Vanuatu, and networks connecting Fiji with the Lau Islands.

Arguably, all of these later exchange or trade systems developed out of antecedents that can ultimately be traced back to the Lapita cultural complex, itself marked by extensive exchange, including at times highly complex (multinodal) and geographically extensive systems. In the Lapita networks as we currently understand them, there was an important social or ritual component, reflected in the movement of such material items as elaborately decorated pottery and a range of finely made shell valuables. In some post-Lapita systems, this pattern was continued and elaborated. Shell valuables, for example, remained an important component of Papuan trading even after the shift to high-volume ceramic production. And one cannot fail to mention the classic kula exchange in armshells and bead necklaces, material forms that may well trace their ultimate ancestry to Lapita shell valuables.

As societies in the archipelagoes of “New” Melanesia began to differentiate and diverge in the centuries following the initial Lapita diaspora, the original widespread exchange linkages retracted to become regionalized or localized. Thus in Papua and the Massim, an earlier system that possibly linked both areas broke down into several regional networks (two of which focused on Motupore and Mailu) about A.D. 800. Jim Allen (1977, 1984b, 2017) developed a model of such transformations in which the spatial extent of networks decreases over time as their complexity (or degree of specialization) increases. This model fits the Papuan data well; further work is required to test its validity for other sequences of changing exchange and trade in eastern Melanesia.


LANDESQUE CAPITAL INTENSIFICATION AND SOCIOPOLITICAL TRANSFORMATIONIn the late phases from New Georgia, Santa Ana, Aneityum, New Caledonia, and Fiji, as well as in the New Guinea Highlands, there were parallel developments of highly intensive agricultural production systems, often accompanied by changes in settlement and residential patterns. The agricultural systems are all types of landesque capital (Blaikie and Brookfield 1987), involving the permanent transformation of a landscape through the construction of facilities such as terraces, canals, and walled garden plots. Once such transformations have been accomplished, landscapes may be put back into use even after periods of disuse or abandonment. Although they may have relatively high associated labor costs during construction, these systems have the benefit of conferring high yields, often at labor input levels that are considerably less than those seen with shifting cultivation and certainly with short-fallow swidden regimes.65

These intensive agricultural systems were something entirely new on the face of post-Lapita Melanesia. We have no evidence that the Lapita peoples themselves practiced anything other than classic shifting cultivation, along with arboriculture and probably the planting of naturally swampy areas in Colocasia or Cyrtosperma aroids. An older notion that the first Austronesian-speaking peoples who moved into Oceania brought with them Asiatic forms of terracing and irrigation technology does not stand up to the scrutiny of either archaeological or linguistic evidence (Kirch and Lepofsky 1993).

Were the terraced, canal-fed irrigation systems of New Georgia, Aneityum, New Caledonia, and Fiji the result of interarchipelago communication and the diffusion of technological innovations, or were they independent but convergent responses to common sets of pressures or challenges? I am inclined toward the latter, seeing these intensive forms of agricultural practice as logical developments out of widespread agronomic and ethnobiological knowledge having an antiquity going back to Lapita, and perhaps earlier.

If this interpretation is correct, what were the push or pull factors, and the changing sociopolitical contexts, that led to these convergent agricultural developments? An underlying factor in all cases is probably to be found in population numbers and densities, but to put the full or even the primary onus on demography is unwarranted. Environmental factors such as resource variability and degradation of landscapes over centuries of shifting cultivation are also salient factors, as both the New Guinea Highlands and Aneityum cases (and probably the New Caledonian one as well) suggest. Moreover, we cannot ignore the complex linkages between production for subsistence and “social” production (Brookfield 1972, 1984; Kirch 1994). As the societies of “New” Melanesia became larger and gradually shifted from being heterarchical to hierarchical, elites benefited incrementally from the surpluses they could extract from intensive agricultural systems and put to social and political ends through competitive feasts or other means.

I have not dealt directly with the topic of sociopolitical transformation in post-Lapita Melanesia, largely because the state of our knowledge of prehistoric social formations remains for the most part rudimentary. But implicit in much of what I have discussed is the notion that the past two and a half millennia witnessed significant transformations of Melanesian societies, with small-scale “house” societies of the Lapita period (see chap. 4) giving rise to large-scale aggregations under the control of elites whom early explorers, missionaries, and later ethnographers were frequently to label “chiefs.”66 The archaeological records of these islands hint at these changes, such as the massive forts of Maré Island, which must have taken centralized labor control to construct; the differentiated habitation mounds of later New Caledonian settlements, in which the residences of elites seem to be set off from or command higher elevations than those of commoners; or the elaborate funerary rites associated with Roy Mata. The challenge for archaeology in Melanesia is to develop the tools of a sophisticated “social archaeology” that can tease out the evidence for such sociopolitical transformation.





SIX


Micronesia


IN THE “SEA OF LITTLE LANDS”



Hidden in the jungle along the shores and in the well-watered interior lowlands of Guam are double rows of upright monuments associated with caps and accompanied by burials, potsherds, and stone and shell implements. These monument sites are called latte or casas de los antiguos by the natives, who believe that they are haunted by Taotao Mana (people of beforetime).

The natives carefully avoid these sites.

L.M. THOMPSON, Archaeology of the Marianas Islands (1932)





MICRONESIA—ONE OF THE THREE divisions of Oceania originally proposed by Dumont d’Urville—encompasses about 2,100 islands, most of which lie north of the equator but below latitude 20° N (map 10). From west to east, the main archipelagoes are Palau (also known as Belau), the Marianas, the Carolines, the Marshalls, and Kiribati (formerly the Gilbert Islands). Tuvalu (formerly the Ellice Islands) lies between Kiribati and Fiji and is considered here in relation to the early settlement of atolls, although its occupants speak Polynesian languages. Despite the great number of islands in Micronesia, in aggregate they total a mere 2,700 square kilometers of land; Guam alone accounts for 582 square kilometers. For this reason an early explorer of Micronesia, F.W. Christian (1899), dubbed it the “sea of little lands.” Many Micronesian islands are atolls, diminutive and low-lying islets encircling a lagoon (see chap. 2). The Marianas chain and some of the Palau group consist of upraised makatea, but outside these western archipelagoes there are only a few high volcanic and makatea islands. Yap, Chuuk (formerly Truk), Pohnpei (formerly Ponape), and Kosrae (formerly Kusaie) are high volcanic islands. Isolated Nauru and Banaba are upraised makatea islands, as are several of the Marshalls.




MAP 10 The islands of Micronesia.





The traditional cultures of Micronesia reflect different origins in Western and central Eastern Micronesia, as well as distinctive environmental adaptations, not to mention the underlying contingencies of history.1 All Micronesian peoples speak Austronesian languages, but not all of these belong to the Oceanic subgroup, a clue to their diverse origins. In contrast to Polynesia, Micronesia does not form a monophyletic cultural group, although its history is shallower and less complex than that of Near Oceania.

Despite important early archaeological work before and soon after World War II, Micronesia has been less studied than Polynesia or Melanesia.2 Alexander Spoehr (1957) excavated in the Marianas in 1949–50, and E.W. Gifford made his third and final Pacific expedition to Yap in 1956 (Gifford and Gifford 1959). These pioneering studies, along with Osborne’s (1966) survey of Palau, all focused on high islands. Until Davidson’s (1967b) excavations on Nukuoro showed otherwise, archaeologists simply assumed that the atolls lacked stratified deposits. Although periodic cyclones do destroy substantial segments of the archaeological record,3 atolls have nonetheless often proved to have rich and surprisingly deep stratigraphic sequences. Beginning in the mid-1970s, Micronesia saw a resurgence of archaeological work, primarily the result of cultural resource management (CRM; see chap. 1). Unfortunately, only a few CRM archaeologists have been diligent about publishing their results in scholarly journals (e.g., Athens 1980, 1984b; Athens and Ward 1995; Butler 1994).4 A list of selected archaeological sites in Micronesia is provided in table 6.1.


TABLE 6.1

Selected Archaeological Sites in Micronesia





TABLE 6.1 (continued)





COLONIZATION AND EARLY SETTLEMENT IN MICRONESIA


Both archaeology and historical linguistics contribute evidence regarding the origins of Micronesian peoples and the directions of early settlement and dispersal through the region. Almost all of the Austronesian languages spoken in the Pacific islands belong to its Oceanic subgroup.5 (See fig. 4.4 for the higher-order subgrouping of the Austronesian languages.) Significantly, however, two exceptions are found in Western Micronesia: Chamorro (in the Marianas) and Palauan.6 These belong to a different high-order subgroup of Austronesian, Western Malayo-Polynesian, which includes a large number of languages today spoken in the Philippines and Indonesia. Blust (2013:113) writes, “Palauan and Chamorro reached their historical locations from the west as a result of separate migrations out of insular Southeast Asia. In the case of Chamorro the movement was almost certainly from the central or northern Philippines.” Reid (1999) argues that Chamorro split off very early from other branches of the Austronesian family. His tentative conclusion is that “Chamorro is an Extra-Formosan language, but that it is a first order branch of the [Austronesian] family, separating from Proto-Extra Formosan, probably from the Northern Philippines, prior to the actual dispersal of the other branches of the family” (Reid 1999:49). In short, on linguistic evidence the origins of the Palau and Marianas islanders most likely are to be found in the Philippine archipelago.

All of the other languages spoken throughout the Caroline, Marshall, and Kiribati Archipelagoes belong to the Oceanic subgroup,7 correlated with the Lapita expansion (see chap. 4). All of the Micronesian languages except Yapese fall together into a single subgroup of Oceanic—Nuclear Micronesian—which has its “center of genetic diversity” in the eastern part of Micronesia, “in the region of Kiribati, Kosrae, Pohnpei, and the Marshalls” (Pawley and Ross 1993:439; see also Jackson 1986). The Nuclear Micronesian group must have split off following the breakup of the Proto Oceanic dialect chain, implying that the first settlement of central Eastern Micronesia was from the region of the initial Lapita expansion, between the Bismarcks and the southeastern Solomons-Vanuatu. Indeed, the Nuclear Micronesian languages share features with the languages of the southeastern Solomons or northern Vanuatu (Shutler and Marck 1975; but see Jackson 1986).

Subgroups within Nuclear Micronesian hint at the process of differentiation once Proto Nuclear Micronesian had split off from early Oceanic. Bender and Wang (1985) find five major branches of Nuclear Micronesian: Kiribati, Marshallese, and Kosraean (all single languages); a Pohnpeic subgroup with four languages; and a large Chuukic subgroup (centered on Chuuk) including perhaps sixty distinct speech communities, primarily on atolls. These five groups have been arrayed by Jackson (1986) and Rehg (1995) in a classic tree model, in which Kosraean, Kiribati, and Marshallese were the first to split off from a Proto Micronesian ancestor, while the Chuukic dialect chain and Pohnpeic both diverged from a Proto Chuukic-Pohnpeic ancestor.

Yapese long defied classification as either Oceanic or non-Oceanic Malayo-Polynesian (e.g., Bender and Wang 1985), but Malcolm Ross (1996a) marshaled evidence that Yapese is an Oceanic language, subsequently influenced by at least four periods of borrowing, two of these from non-Oceanic languages. Such borrowing reflects Yap’s key geographic position in Micronesia and concurs with archaeological evidence for the movement of people and things into Yap over long periods of time. Yapese is evidently a highly conservative Oceanic language, one that diverged early and whose closest relationships are probably with the Admiralty Islands language group. Blust (2013:113) says that Yapese “may have reached Micronesia from the Admiralty Islands soon after the breakup of Proto Oceanic.”

In short, the historical linguistic evidence points to a three-part sequence for the settlement of Micronesia, with people initially moving into the western archipelagoes of Palau and the Marianas directly from island Southeast Asia (most likely the Philippines), another group moving into Yap from the Admiralty Islands, and a third population (or closely related populations) moving up into the central-eastern Caroline Islands from the Solomons-Vanuatu region. The Proto Nuclear Micronesian speech community was rapidly emplaced on more than a single island; it may have constituted a dialect chain spanning at least the high islands (and probably some atolls) of central Eastern Micronesia. Further complexities arose later, for example, with Yapese, which (lying at the interface between western and eastern-central groups) borrowed significantly from Nuclear Micronesian, thus partially masking its Proto Oceanic origins. Having briefly stated the model as derived from historical linguistics, let us examine the archaeological evidence.


THE WESTERN ARCHIPELAGOESRainbird (1994:298) suggests that “the long curve of the [Marianas] archipelago perhaps provided a wide target for early navigators sailing eastwards” on voyages of discovery from the Philippines. In 1949–50, Spoehr excavated at Chalan Piao on Saipan Island in the Marianas, recovering an oyster shell dating to 1527 ± 200 B.C., demonstrating a long prehistory for Western Micronesia—one longer than many at the time were willing to accept, for the date was later repeatedly questioned.8 Renewed work at Chalan Piao in 1987, however, combined with the discovery of other early sites such as Tarague, Mangilao, and Ritidian on Guam, Achugao Point and Unai Bapot on Saipan, and Unai Chulu on Tinian, has produced numerous radiocarbon dates in association with ceramics and other artifacts dating to the initial period of Marianas settlement.9 Butler’s excavations at Achugao Point on Saipan revealed “an intact remnant of a very early occupation surface protected behind a section of raised fossil reef” (1994:15). Although initial dates suggested that the site might be as old as 1930–1630 B.C., Carson (2014:41) believes that the “earliest confirmable site-use” at Achugao Point is probably between 1392 and 1269 B.C. At Unai Bapot, Carson obtained “the oldest known secure radiocarbon dating of any site in the Marianas” (2014:38–40; see also Carson 2008); the dates, on three Anadara bivalve shells, gave a calibrated age range of 1612 to 1558 B.C. However, Petchy, Clark et al. (2016) have shown that the Unai Bapot Anadara shells were likely affected by “hardwater,” yielding dates that are too old. Additional dates on identified charcoal and the bone of an extirpated species of rail now make it clear that Unai Bapot was first occupied between 1250 and 1130 B.C. Thus, although Fitzpatrick (2014) claims that the Marianas were settled by 1550 B.C., there is at present no firm evidence for human occupation predating about 1300 B.C.

The early Marianas sites contain two kinds of ceramics, probably variants of the same ware. Spoehr (1957) labeled these Marianas Red and Lime-Filled, Impressed Trade Ware, although later work indicates that the second type was not a “trade” ware but locally manufactured. Amesbury, Moore, and Hunter-Anderson (1996:60–61) describe the Marianas Red pottery as a “thin-walled, often red-slipped or painted, calcareous sand-tempered [CST] pottery.” The decorated sherds are of the same or similar paste (also CST) and have distinctive designs (circles, lines) that were impressed (or sometimes incised) into the surface and filled with white lime after firing.

Butler (1994:23–33) defined two distinctive styles of “complex incised and stamped (impressed) decoration” at Achugao Point, which he labels Achugao Incised and San Roque Incised. The first exhibits rectilinear patterns, “with the zones between the major elements packed with tiny, delicate punctations” (Butler 1994:27). San Roque Incised sherds (of which only fifteen were found) consist of “bands of curvilinear garlands made by linking incised arches (half circles) with small stamped circles or large punctations placed at the junctions of the arch segments” (Butler 1994:27). According to Carson (2014:67), ceramic vessel forms at the Ritidian and Unai Bapot sites include a slightly restricted bowl or jar with everted rims (in two distinct sizes), a wider bowl with a distinct carination, and a flat-bottomed, shallow bowl or dish (fig. 6.1).




FIGURE 6.1 The earliest ceramic vessel forms from the Marianas Islands, reconstructed from potsherds excavated at the Ritidian site on Guam and the Unai Bapot site on Saipan. (From Carson 2014, fig. 6.14, courtesy of Mike Carson.)





As with Lapita, both Marianas Red and the lime-filled decorated variants most likely stem from a common, older tradition in island Southeast Asia (see Butler 1994:34; Carson et al. 2013; Kirch 1995). The Marianas pottery impressed designs differ from those of Lapita, yet are remarkably similar to motifs found on pottery in island Southeast Asia, such as that from the Sanga Sanga site in the Philippines (Spoehr 1973:184–91) and from the Kalumpang and Minanga Sipakko sites in Sulawesi (Van Heekeren 1972:184–90, fig. 46, pls. 101–2). The latter two assemblages, in particular, exhibit a style of incised designs with small punctations infilling spaces between the lines, similar to the pottery from Achugao Point. In addition, the vessel forms, use of red slip, and calcareous temper that typify Marianas Red are all consistent with Philippines ceramic assemblages of the early to mid-second millennium B.C. (Bellwood 1987a, 2017). Other artifacts found in association with this early pottery include Tridacna shell adzes, Conus shell beads and Conus bracelets, Cypraea shell beads, and rotating fishhooks of Isognomon shell (fig. 6.2). As noted above, the Marianas archaeological record is consistent with linguistic evidence that Chamorro is a Western Malayo-Polynesian language and most probably originated in the Philippines.




FIGURE 6.2 Stone and shell artifacts from early period sites in the Marianas Islands: A, Tridacna shell adz; B, C, chert flakes; D, carved coral pendant; E, Cypraea shell beads; F, Conus shell beads; Conus shell pendants; H, chert adz; I, Anadara shell artifact; J, Isognomon shell fishhooks; K, Conus shell bracelet fragments. (From Carson 2014, fig. 5.1, courtesy of Mike Carson.)





Paleoenvironmental evidence for early settlement of the Marianas comes from sediment cores in Tipalao Marsh (Orote Peninsula) and in the Pago Valley of Guam (Athens and Ward 1995; Ward n.d.).10 The Tipalao Marsh cores encapsulate a record extending back to 5600 B.C.; human arrival possibly as early as 1600 B.C. is signaled by the appearance of charcoal particles suggestive of human burning. By 500 B.C. significant forest clearance was under way, and by A.D. 550 extensive grasslands are evidenced in the pollen record. The Pago Valley core exhibits marked reductions in forest trees and increases in the fire-adapted fern Dicranopteris linearis, a classic signal of anthropogenic disturbance on oceanic islands. Notably, coconut (Cocos nucifera) pollen first occurs at the same time as the initial burning, hinting that this economic plant may have been a human introduction.

In Palau, the chronology of initial human settlement has been less clear. At Uchularois Cave in the southern group of limestone “Rock Islands,” Masse (1990:220) found early ceramics described as “a well constructed and heavily slipped and polished red-ware,” dating to around 50 B.C. The discovery of human burials in Chelechol ra Orrak rockshelter on Orrak Island pushed back direct evidence for settlement of Palau to at least 1050 B.C. (Fitzpatrick 2003:726). Clark, Anderson, and Wright (2006) argue from evidence at Ulong rockshelter, where the basal deposits yielded red-slipped, calcareous sand-tempered ceramics, that the southern limestone islands were settled by about 1150–950 B.C., if not slightly earlier. As Dickinson and Athens (2007) point out, however, geological evidence indicates that Palau is gradually subsiding, affecting archaeological site visibility, especially around the coastline of Babeldaob. Thus early sites on that large island may be buried under coastal sediments and mangrove swamps.

Ward, Athens, and Hotton (1998) recovered twenty-two sediment cores from sites dispersed around Babeldaob Island and analyzed three of them for pollen content and charcoal particle influx. All cores show evidence for “an apparently sudden increase in savanna/grasslands on Babeldaob Island by at least 3100 B.P., with some indication that the western side of Babeldaob may have experienced this change significantly earlier” (Ward, Athens, and Hotton 1998:5). Substantial increases in charcoal particles at the same time make it likely that this was an anthropogenic change. Moreover, pollen of two cultivated plants, coconut and betel nut palm (Areca catechu), appears as early as 2279 B.C. in one core. The authors argue that these data imply “the presence of humans on Palau at least by 3000 cal B.P., and possibly as early as 4200 cal B.P.” (1998:6).

In Yap, the oldest known archaeological assemblages date to about A.D. 1, at the Pemrang site tested by E.W. Gifford (Gifford and Gifford 1959) and later reexcavated by Jun Takayama (1982).11 But as in Palau, palynological and paleoenvironmental evidence (Dodson and Intoh 1999) indicates a likely human presence on Yap beginning around 1350 B.C., marked by “a major period of forest destruction, accompanied by fire.” Thus, as is the case on some other Pacific islands, the earliest part of the Yapese cultural sequence is probably yet to be archaeologically recovered. As Fitzpatrick (2014:5) avers, a “dedicated research effort that incorporates intensive archaeological investigation coupled with coring for paleoenvironmental data would surely be productive.”


THE HIGH ISLANDS OF THE CAROLINESWhereas in Western Micronesia people continued to make ceramics into the period of European contact, this was not so in central Eastern Micronesia, where pottery was entirely unknown. Archaeological work on Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae has shown, however, that pots were at one time in use on the high islands of the Carolines, where suitable clays are available. The later decline in pottery use, and its ultimate abandonment in the Carolines, parallels similar changes in parts of island Melanesia and in Polynesia (see chap. 7). The earliest ceramic assemblages from Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae along with associated material culture strongly suggest that one or more colonizing populations originated from late Lapita cultures in island Melanesia, most likely in the southeastern Solomon Islands.

Along the coast of Fefan Island in the Chuuk lagoon, deposits within the swampy intertidal zone (probably representing former stilt-house villages) yielded CST pottery along with Tridacna shell adzes and Conus shell ornaments (Sinoto, ed., 1984). Radiocarbon dates date these to around 120 B.C. The Fefan pottery consists of plain ware bowls or pots with constricted necks and slightly everted rims; the rims are sometimes decorated with crenations, while a few body sherds have incised lines. The Fefan pottery is similar to Kiki Ware from Tikopia, dated to 900–100 B.C., to plain ware on Anuta, Nendö, and the Reef Islands, and possibly to plain ware from Santa Ana, all in the southeastern Solomons. All these assemblages are associated with Tridacna shell adzes and Conus shell ornaments, strengthening the case for a close connection between Chuuk and the southeastern Solomons.

In Pohnpei, CST ceramics have been recovered from deposits underlying the artificial stone islands of Nan Madol; they have also been recovered from similar contexts at the Lelu site on Kosrae.12 In both cases, the peoples who used this pottery apparently occupied stilt-house settlements, for as on Chuuk the pottery-bearing deposits lie within the intertidal zone.13 Given the presence of Lapita stilt-house villages in Near Oceania, this is another connection between Lapita and early Caroline Islands cultures. The strongest evidence, however, is the CST pottery from levels dated to between 5 B.C. and A.D. 240 in Pohnpei (Athens 1990b:21) and between 108 B.C. and A.D. 244 in Kosrae (Rainbird 1994:299). Pollen cores from Kosrae date initial human disturbance to the island’s vegetation, as well as the introduction of cultivated plants, to the same time frame as the pottery (Athens, Ward, and Murakami 1996). Athens (1990b) identifies two main vessel forms at Nan Madol: globular jars with everted rims and small bowls. Again, the Pohnpei and Kosrae CST ceramics are closest to late Lapita plain wares in the southeastern Solomons (Santa Cruz group, including Tikopia and Anuta) or possibly in northern Vanuatu.14

In short, the archaeological evidence points to settlement of the central Caroline high islands directly from late Lapita pottery-using populations in the southeastern Solomons or immediately adjacent regions, corresponding well with the hypothesis predicted from the linguistic relationships. Athens, who has studied the early Caroline Islands finds closely, reached the same conclusion:

Regarding the origins of the Pohnpei settlers and, perhaps, those of Truk [Chuuk] and Kosrae, it appears that the best case can be made for a linkage with the late Lapita Plain Ware pottery tradition. . . . The chronology is consistent: it begins after 500 B.C. The pottery vessel shapes and decorative techniques are consistent: simple globular pots and bowls with perhaps some rim notching or punctation. Also, its location is consistent: late Lapita Plain Ware pottery is found in the southeast Solomon and New Hebrides Islands where linguistic data suggest the homeland for nuclear Micronesians is located. (1990b:29)

THE ATOLLS Turning from the high islands to the atolls of the central Carolines, Marshalls, and Kiribati, some key environmental factors must be taken into consideration. Evidence has steadily accumulated that the mid-Holocene sea level in the tropical Pacific ranged from 1 to 1.5 meters higher than at present, reaching a peak around 2000 B.C. (Dickinson 2003).15 Sea levels began to rise rapidly around 8000 B.C., from their last glacial maximum low of 100+ meters below present level, and continued to rise unabated for the next six millennia, successively drowning many makatea islands that had been exposed by the lowered sea levels of the late Pleistocene. Before atolls could form on these newly drowned makatea reefs, it was necessary for sea levels to stabilize and to remain stable for some time. This did not occur until sea level fell to its modern position, as early as A.D. 1 in some parts of the Pacific but not until A.D. 800 or so in the Tuamotu Islands. Weisler, Yamano, and Hua (2012) dated foraminifera sands on Utrōk and Maloelap atolls in the Marshalls, showing that islets, or motu, began to form and stabilize between 800 and 450 B.C. The Micronesian atolls were in their initial stages of motu formation and stabilization when late Lapita populations began to explore the seaways north of the equator around two thousand years ago.

These newly formed atolls would have been tenuous environments for human colonization, having but the sparsest vegetation. Atoll vegetation is typically dominated by human-introduced plants, and human manipulation of these environments was necessary to render them suitable as permanent abodes. From high-island bases on Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae, pioneers may have seasonally exploited (or temporarily inhabited) atolls in the vicinity, introducing economically vital plants such as coconut and breadfruit and excavating cultivation pits to tap the freshwater aquifers (Weisler 1999). After generations of intermittent use, atolls might have yielded to permanent settlement, and the more far-flung islands could be inhabited.

While only a few Micronesian atolls have been thoroughly investigated by archaeologists, those with stratified sequences exhibit little evidence of use earlier than about two thousand years ago. In the Marshall Islands, sites on Majuro and Maloelap date to around A.D. 100.16 For both Maloelap and Utrōk, Weisler et al. (2012) show that human colonization occurred between A.D. 100 and 160, some 900 to 600 years after initial islet formation. Beardsley (1994:161–62) reports a calibrated radiocarbon age of 167–124 B.C. from an early cultural deposit on Kwajalein Atoll. Other atolls were not settled until later, such as Ngulu and Imwinyap where the earliest dates are A.D. 800, or Nukuoro at A.D. 713. In short, atoll colonization was probably continuous, with the full spectrum of Micronesian atolls not fully occupied until well into the last millennium.





CULTURAL SEQUENCES IN MICRONESIA


Spoehr (1957) based his cultural sequence for the Marianas Islands on ceramic chronology, with a quantitative change in pottery assemblages from those dominated by Marianas Red to later ones containing only Marianas Plain Ware. The later part of the Marianas sequence was also marked by the construction of limestone-pillar structures termed latte, dominantly associated with Marianas Plain Ware, which Spoehr attributed to the last 850 years.17

For Palau, Osborne’s speculative sequence was based on a dubious ceramic typology (1966, 1979) that proved to be of no lasting value. Masse (1990) outlined an alternative sequence beginning with a “Colonial” Period from A.D. 1 to 650, continuing with a “Resource Intensification” Period (A.D. 650–1200), followed by a “Rock Island Village” Period (1200–1450), a “Transition” Period (1450–1600), and finally a “Traditional” Period (1600–1914). However, work in the Rock Islands by Clark (2005) and Fitzpatrick (2003) demonstrates that settlement of these limestone islands commenced by at least 1000 B.C. Meanwhile, CRM work on Babeldaob shows that the main island was settled by at least 1250 B.C. (Liston 2009).18 A distinctive “thin blackware” type of pottery predates the ubiquitous thick-walled and grog-tempered later Palauan pottery, associated with an early phase of inland (ridgetop) settlement on Babeldaob. At the Ngerulmud Hill site in Melekeok State, sherds of this thin blackware were dated to 230 B.C. (Liston, Kaschko, and Welch 1998:59, 82). On Babeldaob, the interior ridges and hillsides were massively reworked with vast terrace complexes; these are discussed later in this chapter.

Sediment coring and pollen analysis suggest that the first third of the Yapese archaeological sequence remains to be uncovered (Dodson and Intoh 1999). While a rough ceramic sequence beginning with CST pottery and moving to a later Yapese “laminated ware” was demonstrated by Gifford and Gifford (1959) and confirmed by later work (e.g., Intoh 1988, 1990; Intoh and Leach 1985), a complete cultural sequence remains elusive. Yap has extensive stone architectural remains (Hunter-Anderson 1983, 1984; Pickering 1990), but there have been too few excavations to place the development of this architecture—let alone its significance for sociopolitical changes—in proper temporal perspective.

Chuuk’s extensive lagoon (2,125 square kilometers) encompasses a number of small high islands and smaller islets, totaling 120 square kilometers. Thus far, archaeological work has concentrated on Tol, Moen, and Fefan Islands.19 King and Parker (1984:415–41) attempted a synthesis of the Chuuk sequence but could define only two periods, with an intervening “period about which at present we know virtually nothing,” lasting from A.D. 500 to 1300 (see also Rainbird 2004:168–79). They named the early period the Winas Pattern, characterized by CST pottery (such as that recovered from the Fefan Island sites), as well as Tridacna and Cassis shell adzes, Conus shell rings, and other kinds of shell ornaments. Settlements were confined to coastal locations, possibly with stilt-house villages. The later Tonnachaw Pattern, from A.D. 1300 until European contact, lacks pottery but has many kinds of artifacts known from ethnographic collections, such as Cypraea shell breadfruit peelers, coral pounders, slingstones, and Terebra shell adzes. Tonnachaw settlement patterns incorporated not only coastal residential sites but also interior residential and cook-house sites, shell middens on ridges and hill summits, and stone-walled forts on high ridges or summits. The emphasis on ridgetop locations—whether deliberately fortified or not—speaks to the significance of warfare and raiding in the Tonnachaw Pattern (Rainbird 1996; 2004:168–79).

The best-defined sequence for any Micronesian island is that for Pohnpei, proposed by Ayres (1990) and summarized in table 6.2.20 However, Ayres’s speculative “Settlement and Adaptive Integration Phase” is not supported by archaeological evidence; human settlement on the island probably begins about A.D. 1. In material culture, important changes include (1) the decline in ceramic use and its abandonment by A.D. 1500, if not earlier; (2) the appearance of distinctive Terebra and Mitre shell adzes after about A.D. 1000;21 and (3) the development of compound trolling lures of pearl shell after about A.D. 1200. Of particular interest is the Nan Madol Phase, associated with the construction of this massive site, discussed at greater length later in this chapter.22


TABLE 6.2

The Pohnpei Culture-Historical Sequencea





A sequence for Kosrae has not been formally defined, but it might parallel that for Pohnpei. Among the similarities are an early phase with pottery, the later abandonment of ceramics, and the development of complex stone architecture (including a major chiefly residential center at Lelu), especially during the past 500 years. Athens, Ward, and Murakami (1996) trace the history of an intensive “agroforest” or arboricultural subsistence system over 2,000 years of human land use on Kosrae, with breadfruit as a dominant tree crop (see also Levin and Ayres 2016). The transformation of the island’s original vegetation into this agroforest occurred by A.D. 400–600, well before the later “rise of social complexity as evidenced by the Lelu megalithic ruins [that] was not accompanied by any perceptible change in cultigens or agricultural practices” (Athens, Ward, and Murakami 1996:845).

For the Caroline and Marshall Islands, sequences can be constructed for atolls where extensive excavations have been carried out, as with Majuro, Kwajalein, Maleolap, Utrōk, and Arno atolls in the Marshalls or Ulithi, Woleai, Lamotrek, and Mokil in the Carolines.23 For Utrōk, Weisler (2001a; 2001b:123–28, fig. 8.1) traces the culture history of the atoll over the course of two millennia, although he does not divide the sequence into named phases. The material culture, which included shell adzes (made from Tridacna, Cassis, Conus, and Lambis shells), Spondylus shell beads, Conus shell rings, and pumice abraders, remained stable over time. Single-piece fishhooks and trolling lures of pearl shell are known for the past 1,000 years and may date earlier. Inshore fishing and generalized shellfish gathering dominated marine subsistence, while on the coral motu there were gradual increases in swamp cultivation, aroid pit cultivation, and arboriculture. None of the atolls has substantial stone architecture (other than low house alignments), and settlement patterns were highly stable.

Some parallel trends and differences suggest themselves in these island sequences.
