In a study of Hawaiian archaic states (Kirch 2010), I set out the following characteristics of such sociopolitical formations: (1) they had well-developed class endogamy; (2) they were ruled by kings who traced their origins directly to the gods, and who were often regarded as instantiations of deities on earth; (3) their political economies were to a large degree centrally controlled by the king’s bureaucracy; (4) the king’s status and power were legitimated by state cults involving a formalized temple system, overseen by full-time priests; (5) the king’s power was maintained by a monopoly of force, involving a full-time warrior cadre or standing army; and (6) the king and his court occupied special residential quarters (palaces) and enjoyed various privileges and material luxuries supplied by a cadre of full-time specialists and craftspersons. In the following two sections, I discuss how Tonga and Hawai‘i were transformed from complex chiefdoms to archaic states. Although both societies became states, the processes underlying state development and the configurations of these hierarchical societies exhibit interesting differences.


THE TONGAN “MARITIME EMPIRE”The late prehistoric period in Tonga is characterized by monumental architecture, although the forms differ from those in Samoa (Burley 1998). Several kinds of mounds were constructed, some, called sia heu lupe, for use by chiefs in their sport of snaring pigeons, others as sitting places or for elite burial. Davidson (1969) excavated two burial mounds at ‘Atele on Tongatapu and showed that these had grown in size and elevation over several periods of use. Recent survey of the Tongatapu landscape using LiDAR (Freeland et al. 2016) shows that the terrain is densely covered in such mounds (fig. 8.32). Yet another class of burial mound was reserved for the interment of chiefs; typically rectangular in plan, these burial mounds were faced with cut-and-dressed slabs of coral limestone. Quarrying and transporting the facing slabs required substantial labor, especially for the largest mounds, in which the highest-ranking elite were buried, including the Tu‘i Tonga, or sacred paramount.




FIGURE 8.32 LiDAR image of a portion of Tongatapu Island, showing the density of mound sites. (Image produced by Travis Freeland, used with permission of the Government of Australia and the Government of Tonga.)





Political power in proto-historic Tonga centered on the large island of Tongatapu, 257 square kilometers in area. In spite of its makatea geology, Tongatapu has fertile soils enriched by volcanic ashfalls and organic nutrients, as well as an extensive lagoon-reef ecosystem on its northern coast. Tongatapu’s terrestrial resources, although not as rich as those of some other high islands, were sufficient to permit the growth of a prehistoric population numbering at least 40,000. Tongan oral traditions, augmented by archaeology, indicate that three or more independent and competing chiefdoms on the island gradually amalgamated into a single polity, largely through territorial conquest.

By proto-historic times, there were at least two distinct levels of chiefs: the hou‘eiki, or lesser chiefs, who were in charge of managing local estates; and the Tu‘i Tonga, or sacred paramount lord of Tonga and his immediate household. As in Hawai‘i, the commoner population had become alienated from direct control of the land, holding usufruct rights by virtue of regular tribute payments to the hou‘eiki and through them to the Tu‘i Tonga.

Tongan oral traditions describe how the chiefship was elaborated in the final centuries prior to European contact: following the reign of the twenty-third Tu‘i Tonga (Takalaua), his senior son, Kau‘ulufonuafekai (later the twenty-fourth Tu‘i Tonga), initiated a campaign of territorial conquest extending beyond Tongatapu to encompass the Ha‘apai and Vava‘u groups, as well as farther-flung Niuatoputapu and even ‘Uvea. Sites on Niuatoputapu and ‘Uvea (Frimigacci and Hardy 1997; Kirch 1988c; Sand 1993c, 1998b; Sand and Valentin 1991) reveal extensive evidence of a Tongan intrusion around A.D. 1500–1600, in the form of elaborate Tongan-style burial mounds, pigeon-snaring mounds, and fortifications. According to the oral histories, Kau‘ulufonuafekai implemented a significant structural change in the chiefship, reserving for himself (and subsequent Tu‘i Tonga titleholders) the sacred kingship while creating the office of the hau, or secular paramount, who became known as the Tu‘i Ha‘a Takalaua. The first titleholder of the hau-ship was Kau‘ulufonuafekai’s younger brother Moungamotu‘a. This diarchy of sacred and secular leaders came to an end with the thirty-ninth and final Tu‘i Tonga, Laufilitonga, who died in 1865.

The residential and ritual center of these political elites was at Mu‘a, fronting the Tongatapu lagoon, where the dual paramounts occupied separate residential compounds, surrounded by earthen moats (fig. 8.33). This extensive complex was mapped and described by the pioneering archaeologist W.C. McKern in 1920 and recently has been the subject of renewed investigations directed by Geoff Clark (Clark 2016; Clark, Burley, and Murray 2008; Clark et al. 2014; Clark, Reepmeyer, and Melekiola 2016). At Mu‘a, successive generations of Tu‘i Tonga were interred in massive burial mounds (langi) faced with slabs hewn from coral limestone (fig. 8.34). Lapaha, the residential compound of the Tu‘i Tonga line, includes twenty-six langi mounds, the largest fronting a great ceremonial plaza. In 1777, Captain James Cook and his crew witnessed the annual rites of tribute called ‘inasi taking place at this plaza, a scene illustrated by the expedition’s artist (fig. 8.35). A stone pier or dock projected 200 meters into the lagoon, a symbol of the external power base that the Tongan elite had achieved through their wars of interisland conquest. Each year double-hulled canoes brought tribute to Mu‘a from these outlying islands, to be offered up to the Tu‘i Tonga at the ‘inasi ceremony.




FIGURE 8.33 Archaeological plan of the Mu‘a site, Tongatapu Island, seat of the Tu‘i Tonga in prehistoric and proto-historic times. (After McKern 1929.)





FIGURE 8.34 Tomb J9, known as Tauatonga, at Mu‘a, Tongatapu. This classic langi structure has four tiers of cut-and-dressed limestone facings. (Photo courtesy Geoff Clark.)





FIGURE 8.35 In 1777, Captain James Cook and his crew arrived at Tongatapu in time to witness the annual tribute presentations on the great plaza fronting the burial mounds of the Tu‘i Tonga at Mu‘a. Engraving after drawing by John Webber for the atlas of Cook’s third voyage. (Collection of the author.)





Archaeological research by Geoff Clark and colleagues has established a firm chronology for the rise of the Tongan archaic state. The origins of the Tu‘i Tonga archaic state can be traced to an even older complex on Tongatapu, the site of Heketa, long noted for the presence of a massive “trilithon” called the Ha‘amonga a Maui (fig. 8.36), which was probably the gateway to the royal compound (Clark and Reepmeyer 2014). This monument incorporates three blocks of quarried limestone with a combined total weight of 55 tons. Radiocarbon dates indicate that the stone architecture at Heketa, which includes the first known langi, dates to between A.D. 1320 and 1390. Oral traditions state that Heketa was occupied by the tenth through twelfth Tu‘i Tonga, after which the royal seat was moved to Lapaha at Mu‘a.




FIGURE 8.36 The trilithon known as Ha‘amonga-a-Maui at Heketa, Tongatapu. This structure probably served as a gateway to the royal compound. (Photo courtesy Geoff Clark.)





Soon after the move to Lapaha, one of the largest langi, a tomb measuring 28 by 41.5 meters with three tiers of cut-and-dressed limestone slabs weighing an estimated 700 tons, was constructed on reclaimed land extending into the Tongatapu lagoon (Clark, Reepmeyer, and Melekiola 2016). Radiocarbon dates put the construction of this langi, known as Paepaeotelea, at between A.D. 1300 and 1400. The Tongan archaic state achieved its height between 1400 and 1650, during which numerous other langi were constructed as burial monuments for successive Tu‘i Tonga. It is estimated that more than 2,500 tons of limestone blocks were quarried and hauled to Mu‘a to construct the tombs. Geochemical analysis of stone tools recovered from excavations at Lapaha also reveal the extent of the Tongan state’s external exchange relationships, with 66 percent of the tools coming from locations beyond Tonga, including Fiji, Samoa, and even the Society Islands (Clark et al. 2014).

According to Aswani and Graves (1998:155), the evolution of precontact Tongan society resulted from the “dialectical interaction” of “two integral and inflationary processes: intra- and intergroup competition.” The proto-historic Tongan chiefdom had evolved into a kind of “maritime empire,” maintained by emplacing junior-ranking members of the Tongatapu elite on outlying, conquered islands.47 These junior chiefs, removed from Mu‘a where they might threaten or subvert the power of the Tu‘i Tonga or of the hau, married local chiefly women of the islands they governed, enforcing the authority of Tongatapu. This elite system established connections ranging far beyond the Tongan archipelago, to Fiji in the west and Samoa in the east (Kaeppler 1978; Kirch 1984a). Regular long-distance voyaging to these foreign islands—along with carefully arranged chiefly marriages—assured a flow of prestige goods (fine mats, feathers, sandalwood, barkcloth, canoes, pottery) into Tongatapu, providing “wealth finance” that underwrote the political strategies of the Tongan elite (see Earle 1997:73).

Two isolated islands that came under the sway of the Tongan “maritime empire” in later prehistory were Niuatoputapu and ‘Uvea; both have provided archaeological evidence of their integration into the Tonga state. In 1976 I mapped the settlement pattern of Niuatoputapu (Kirch 1988), which had an independent polity prior to about A.D. 1550 but then came under Tongan political hegemony. During this late period, mounds and monuments of various kinds were constructed throughout the island, as shown in figure 8.37. The distribution of these monuments suggests that the island was subdivided into several territories, each with its own elite burial facilities, pigeon-snaring mounds, and chiefly sitting mounds. With permission of local Tongan authorities, I excavated a large stone-faced burial mound at Houmafakalele, dating its construction to the fifteenth century A.D. (fig. 8.38). This mound contained the extended skeletons of at least four adults, buried without grave goods. Black stains surrounded the skeletons, suggesting burial in charcoal-blackened mats, a practice known ethnographically.




FIGURE 8.37 The settlement pattern map of Niuatoputapu Island shows a distribution of burial mounds and other monuments, suggesting that the island was subdivided into several territorial units. (After Kirch 1988a.)





FIGURE 8.38 The Houmafakalele burial mound site on Niuatoputapu Island, a chiefly burial mound in Tongan style with cut-and-dressed facing stones of quarried limestone, contained several extended burials. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





‘Uvea Island was also an independent polity in its earlier history, but like Niuatoputapu it fell under the hegemonic expansion of the Tongan chiefs in late prehistory (Sand 1998b). Tongan domination is reflected linguistically, because ‘Uvean is a Nuclear Polynesian language with heavy borrowing from Tongan. Archaeological evidence corroborates the oral traditions of Tongan conquest, with various monumental sites including Tongan-style chiefly burial mounds and large fortifications. One of the most remarkable is the Petania burial mound, measuring 18.5 by 16 meters, with a central coral slab–lined vault. Surrounding this vault, which held one or more chiefly burials, were no fewer than 150 other individuals, “buried in six successive stratigraphic levels, placed in circles around the burial vault. . . . Most of them had been placed one body partially over another, that is, the head of one person was placed over the femora of the skeleton behind and below” (Sand and Valentin 1991:239–41). Most, if not all, of the individuals were interred at the same time, possibly as a mass sacrificial offering to the high-ranking individual in the central vault.

Daniel Frimigacci (Frimigacci and Hardy 1997) studied fortifications in the southern part of ‘Uvea, including Lanutavake (which encloses a crater lake), Makahu, Atuvalu, and the remarkable site of Kolonui. Kolonui, “Great Fort,” has defensive walls of stacked basalt boulders up to 15 meters wide and 4 meters high enclosing a large area, with a chiefly residential complex (Talietumu) in the southwest corner. These sites reveal that the proto-historic ‘Uvean society was strongly hierarchical, with chiefs who could command the labor of large numbers of subordinates and, at times, exert absolute power over life and death.

In sum, while the makatea environment of Tonga might seem insufficient to underwrite a strongly hierarchical, centralized polity, through the military integration of an entire archipelago and the careful manipulation of external sources of prestige goods, the Tongan chiefs transformed their society into one of Polynesia’s archaic states.


THE ARCHAIC STATES OF HAWAI‘IAnthropologists have long regarded proto-historic Hawai‘i as the most highly stratified of all Polynesian chiefdoms. Summarizing his lengthy research into Hawaiian society and economy at the period of initial European contact, Sahlins writes:

Everything looks as if Hawaiian society had been through a history in which the concepts of lineage—of a classic Polynesian sort, organizing the relations of persons and tenure of land by seniority of descent—had latterly been eroded by the development of chiefship. Intruding on the land and people from outside, like a foreign element, the chiefship usurps the collective rights of land control and in the process reduces the lineage order in scale, function, and coherence. (Sahlins, in Kirch and Sahlins 1992, 1:192)

In most Polynesian societies, land and resources were classically controlled by structures of kinship, especially lineage groups. In Hawai‘i, however, a structure of kingship had emerged in late prehistory (Hommon 2013; Kirch 2010, 2012). As Goldman (1970:200) summed it up, “Polynesian social evolution reached its greatest development in the Hawaiian Islands, where all changes in direction or further elaborations of traditional forms under way elsewhere finally came to fruition.” Ethnographers such as Sahlins and Goldman could only speculate on how such a transformation from kinship to kingship occurred in Hawai‘i. Rich oral traditions of chiefly marriages and alliances, wars and conquests, and other political events assembled by nineteenth-century Hawaiian scholars provide important clues from an “insider” perspective (Cachola-Abad 2000; Kamakau 1961; Malo 1951). But only archaeology provides direct historical evidence to track such fundamental changes through time, even though the processes underlying change must be inferred from the material correlates of social action.

Hawai‘i boasts the richest archaeological record for any Pacific archipelago.48 Pre–World War II surface surveys focused on monumental stone architecture, especially the numerous and structurally varied heiau, or temple sites.49 During the 1950s and 1960s, major excavation programs of the Bishop Museum and the University of Hawai‘i outlined a material culture sequence,50 later augmented by settlement pattern studies and increased attention to sociopolitical, economic, and demographic changes.51 Aggressive economic development in Hawai‘i, spurred by statehood in 1959, has led to thousands of CRM projects; while variable in quality and research orientation, these have resulted in masses of new data.52 The archaeological record from Hawai‘i is so extensive that the following overview merely touches on some highlights.

Excepting temperate New Zealand, the Hawaiian Islands, with 16,692 square kilometers of land area, constitute the largest archipelago in Polynesia (map 15). With a subtropical climate and fertile volcanic soils, the Hawaiian Islands were ideal for growing most of the Oceanic root, tuber, and tree crops. This environment posed none of the constraints to the rise of large, complex societies that we see in other Polynesian islands. And indeed, the precontact population of Hawai‘i is likely to have numbered a half million or more when Cook arrived in 1778–79.53




MAP 15 The Hawaiian Islands, showing the location of key archaeological sites.





Because the archipelago has a linear age progression (owing to its hot-spot origin; see chap. 2), the older islands, Kaua‘i, O‘ahu, and Moloka‘i, along with the western half of Maui, display deeply weathered and dissected landforms, with valleys and permanent streams well suited to irrigated pondfield agriculture. These older islands have more developed coastal reefs, with better fishing and the potential to construct large fishponds for aquaculture. In striking contrast, geologically younger east Maui and Hawai‘i—while they account for 74 percent of the total land area—mostly lack permanent streams and have large tracts of young lava flows (Kirch 2014). On those younger landscapes only dryland, rainfed cultivation was possible, and the cliff-bound coastlines lacking reef flats were not conducive to fishpond construction.

Kirch (1985a) proposed a cultural sequence for Hawai‘i, with four periods (Colonization, Developmental, Expansion, and Proto-Historic), beginning with initial Polynesian settlement around A.D. 300.54 For reasons explained in chapter 7, we now know that this date is too early; the first arrival of Polynesians occurred considerably later, around A.D. 1000.55 This has required a reevaluation of the Hawaiian sequence (table 8.2), with a collapsing of the original Colonization and Developmental Periods into a revised Foundation Period, followed by Early Expansion, Late Expansion, and Proto-Historic Periods (Kirch and McCoy 2007). The Marquesas Islands have long been posited as the immediate source for the first Hawaiian colonizers, both on the evidence of material culture (fishhooks, adz forms) and on linguistic grounds (Green 1966; Marck 1999). However, Hawai‘i did not become immediately isolated from central Eastern Polynesia. Hawaiian oral traditions speak of a “voyaging period” during which great navigator-chiefs such as Mo‘ikeha, La‘amaikahiki, and Pā‘ao made voyages to “Kahiki” (i.e., Tahiti) and back.56 Long-distance voyaging ceased after about A.D. 1400, after which the Hawaiian Islands became completely isolated from the rest of Polynesia. “Kahiki” became a distantly remembered homeland from which the great anthropomorphic god Lono returned each year during the Makahiki season to ritually fertilize and renew the land.


TABLE 8.2

The revised Hawaiian cultural sequence.





Sites of the Foundation and Early Expansion Periods, such as the Bellows Beach (O18) site at Waimānalo on O‘ahu Island, the Hālawa Valley dune site (Mo-A1–3) on Moloka‘i, and sites at Waiahukini, on Hawai‘i Island,57 have yielded fishhooks, basalt adzes, ornaments, and other artifacts that exhibit local stylistic elements (fig. 8.39), differentiating them from styles in central Eastern Polynesia. Settlements typically were nucleated hamlets in coastal settings. The Hālawa dune site contains round-ended house foundations quite different from later prehistoric Hawaiian dwellings, which were characteristically rectangular in form (fig. 8.40). At Waimānalo the dead were interred under house pavements, a practice that ceased in later times. Pigs, dogs, and chickens were all present from the earliest period. Pollen and micro-charcoal evidence from sediment cores on O‘ahu indicate extensive forest clearance for agriculture by A.D. 1200.58




FIGURE 8.39 Artifacts from the early Bellows Beach sand dune site (O18) at Waimānalo, O‘ahu Island, included pearl-shell fishhooks, coral and sea urchin spine abraders, ornaments, a coconut grater, a bracelet of pig tusks, basalt adzes, and a basalt awl. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





FIGURE 8.40 The early Mo-A1–3 dune site at the mouth of Hālawa Valley, Moloka‘i, contained small round-ended house structures of a form not seen in later Hawaiian settlements. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





The Late Expansion Period (A.D. 1400–1650) was a time of exponential growth when the archipelago’s population expanded out of the most ecologically favorable areas (what Hommon [1986] called the “salubrious cores”) into the drier, leeward, and more marginal regions. During this dynamic period, production systems were intensified, ritual architecture was elaborated, and a system of hierarchical territorial land units was formalized. In tandem, these changes radically transformed Hawaiian society.

The demographic transition in Hawai‘i is better attested through archaeological evidence than for almost any other Polynesian group. Early attempts to trace the trajectory of Hawaiian population, such as Hommon’s (1986) influential model of “inland expansion,” were based on house count data. These pointed to rapid, exponential population growth beginning after A.D. 1200, possibly peaking or even declining prior to European contact.59 An alternative approach used the curves generated from the cumulative probability distributions of radiocarbon dates as a proxy for population growth.60 This also showed an exponential increase in population after A.D. 1200 but with a peak at around A.D. 1450 and a decline thereafter. Kirch (2010:135, fig. 4.3) assembled a suite of 1,036 radiocarbon dates from four of the main Hawaiian islands, which shows the dramatic increase in numbers of dates—and by inference, in overall population—that begins around A.D. 1200 and continues up through the final century prior to European contact. These data all support an interpretation of exponential population increase, although the rate of growth may have slowed in some regions prior to European contact. Finer-grained studies of population trends have focused on local regions, such as Kirch’s (2007b, 2014) reconstruction of population trends in Kahikinui, Maui, based on radiocarbon-dated habitations, and Field et al.’s (2011) investigation of population trends in leeward Kohala, Hawai‘i Island. For Kohala, a maximum population density seems to have been achieved within the core of the leeward Kohala Field System by around A.D. 1650, with subsequent population increases being absorbed by the southern margins of this vast dryland agricultural system.

Intensification of production went hand in glove with the demographic transition, beginning during the Early Expansion Period and continuing throughout later periods; indeed, population growth and agricultural intensification were arguably linked processes (see Kirch 1994:310–12). Hawaiian agro-ecosystems were of two major kinds: irrigated and dryland (rain-fed). In the western islands—where topography and hydrology permitted—valley floors and lower hillsides were converted to ranks of terraced pondfields, irrigated by diverting streamflow into stone-lined canals (fig. 8.41). On both O‘ahu and Moloka‘i, irrigated terrace construction was well under way by A.D. 1200–1300.61 In the geologically younger eastern islands, irrigation was feasible only in limited areas, but where rainfall and substrate age were conducive to cultivation, extensive leeward slopes were converted into intensive agricultural landscapes dominated by field systems with reticulate grids of stone walls and embankments that broke up the terrain into individual garden plots. Extensive dryland field systems have been archaeologically documented for Kohala and Kona on Hawai‘i Island, for Kahikinui and Kaupō on Maui, and for the Kalaupapa Peninsula on Moloka‘i.62 The leeward Kohala Field System (fig. 8.42), which has been the most intensively studied by archaeologists, began with widely spaced field boundaries around A.D. 1300, becoming increasingly intensified after A.D. 1450 (Ladefoged and Graves 2008). The Late Expansion Period also witnessed the construction of large fishponds for aquacultural production of mullet and milkfish (Kikuchi 1976; Summers 1964) wherever coastal conditions permitted, such as along the protected southern coast of Moloka‘i (fig. 8.43).




FIGURE 8.41 Hawaiian valley floors became intensively cultivated through the construction of pondfield irrigation systems, such as the one depicted in this archaeological plan from the Anahulu Valley, O‘ahu. (After Kirch and Sahlins 1992.)





FIGURE 8.42 LiDAR imagery of a small section of the leeward Kohala Field System on Hawai‘i Island, showing the recticulate grid of north-south trending field embankments, intersected by east-west trending territorial boundary walls and trails. Several walled house compounds are also visible. (Photo from Hawai‘i Biocomplexity Project, courtesy of P. V. Kirch.)





FIGURE 8.43 Another form of landesque capital intensification in later Hawaiian prehistory was the construction of large fishponds on reef flats, such as this pond on the southern shore of Moloka‘i Island. (Photo by Thérèse Babineau.)





Both irrigation works in the western isles and dryland field systems in the eastern group constitute forms of landesque capital intensification but with rather different socioeconomic outcomes due to their different labor input-to-yield ratios and their abilities to produce a surplus above individual household requirements. Irrigation permitted higher yields per unit of labor, hence greater surpluses that could be extracted by the chiefs. In the dryland field systems, higher labor inputs were required, and the limits to intensification were more quickly approached, making the extraction of a surplus that could be put to political purposes more contentious. Two contrasting pathways to political (and ideological) transformation emerged.63 The chiefly elite of the western islands invested heavily in irrigation, and their religious system emphasized Kāne, god of flowing waters and procreation. On Maui and Hawai‘i Islands, in contrast, the chiefs oversaw the development of vast field systems in which the primary crop was sweet potato, augmented by dryland taro in the higher elevations. In these dryland regions, moreover, there was frequent war and efforts at territorial conquest, promulgating a legitimating ideology based on the cult of Kū, a human sacrifice–demanding god of war, who seasonally alternated with Lono, god of rain and thunder (Valeri 1985).

Major advances in our understanding of the dryland field systems of Hawai‘i and Maui—how they operated in relation to the “biogeochemical gradients” of these geologically youthful islands and the chronology of their development—have come from the work of the Hawai‘i Biocomplexity Project (Kirch, ed., 2011; Vitousek et al. 2004). This interdisciplinary team of archaeologists, ecologists, soil scientists, and quantitative modelers demonstrated that the dryland field systems were positioned to take advantage of a “sweet spot” running in a continuous band along the leeward volcanic slopes of these islands, a zone in which rainfall was sufficient but not too high to have leached out essential soil nutrients. In essence, the Hawaiians had learned how to “farm the rock” so as to extract high yields of sweet potato in closely spaced plots, divided by low embankments planted with rows of sugarcane.

Increased hierarchization of the Hawaiian chiefly class, combined with the use of religious ideology to legitimate chiefly dominance over the common people (the maka‘āinana), is reflected in the archaeological record through the temple (heiau) system, which became greatly elaborated during the Late Expansion and Proto-Historic Periods. Diachronic studies of heiau are less common than surface surveys, but excavations document successive rebuildings of heiau over time, incorporating increasingly elaborate architecture and greater size (both area and volume of stone).64 High-precision U/Th dating of branch coral offerings from heiau in the Kahikinui District of Maui shows a dramatic increase in the number of temples constructed from the late sixteenth century and continuing for the next 150 to 200 years (Kirch and Sharp 2005; Kirch, Mertz-Kraus, and Sharp 2015). This phase of temple building correlates with the consolidation of Maui Island as a single kingdom under the Pi‘ilani dynasty of rulers, as described in the oral traditions (Kirch 2010, 2014). Most of the temples functioned as heiau ho‘o ‘ulu‘ai, or agricultural temples, important both in the ritual regulation of cultivation and in the annual collection of tribute (fig. 8.44). The largest heiau, such as Pi‘ilanihale in the Hāna District, covering an area of 12,126 square meters, or Lo‘alo‘a in Kaupo (4,160 square meters), incorporated massive volumes of stone fill. These impressive structures were luakini, temples associated with paramount chiefs and the cult of war, at which human sacrifices were offered to the god Kū (fig. 8.45).




FIGURE 8.44 An aerial view of a midsized temple (heiau) in the ahupua‘a of Kipapa, Kahikinui, Maui. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





FIGURE 8.45 The massive luakini war temple of Pu‘ukoholāat Kawaihae, Hawai‘i Island. The Hawai‘i Island king Kamehameha I had this temple constructed to ensure success in his intended conquest of the Hawaiian archipelago. (Photo by Thérèse Babineau.)





It was probably during the Early Expansion Period that the distinctive Hawaiian system of land tenure—the ahupua‘a system—first developed. Ahupua‘a (literally, “pig altars”) were territorial units that ran from the mountain ridges down to the sea, cross-cutting the ecological grain of an island to include all of its main resource zones. Whereas in other Eastern Polynesian islands such radial land sections were typically controlled by descent groups or lineages, in Hawai’i ahupua‘a were under the control of lesser chiefs (ali‘i ‘ai ahupu‘a), who in turn held these territories at the pleasure of the king (ali‘i ‘ai moku) to whom the chiefs owed material support, especially in times of war. All of the ahupua‘a under a paramount chief constituted the kingdom at large, called in Hawaiian the moku, derived from the old Polynesian word for “island.” The commoners, for their part, held rights to work lands within an ahupua‘a in return for tributary payments of produce and labor to the chief. Thus land became alienable and the entire system one of enfeudation, tied together by reciprocal rights of tribute and protection (Hommon 2014; Kirch 2010; Kirch and Sahlins 1992).

Another distinctive transformation in Hawai’i was the elaboration of the kapu system, a complex set of prohibitions and cultural practices that differentiated Hawaiians by gender and by rank. The highest-ranking chiefs, for example, were accorded the kapu moe, “prostrating taboo,” by the commoners. Women were forbidden to eat certain foods, such as pig, bananas, and some fish, and were obligated to prepare their food in separate earth ovens. Late precontact habitation sites in the Kahikinui region of Maui exhibit evidence of such separate cooking facilities (Kirch 2014). Precontact Hawaiian houses, household clusters, and chiefly heiau all display certain structural similarities in their spatial layout, reflecting pervasive “organizational principles predicated upon notions of ritual offerings and the observance of the kapu system” (Ladefoged 1998:70).

By the Proto-Historic Period (A.D. 1650–1778) the ahupua‘a and kapu systems had become increasingly entrenched and elaborated. Thanks to the rich body of indigenous oral traditions relating to these final centuries before European contact, as well as the material evidence of archaeology, a great deal is known of the political history of this period, including the economic, military, and religious forms of power employed by the chiefly class (Cachola-Abad 2000; Kirch 2010, 2012). Social stratification reached a level at which the chiefs (ali‘i) distinguished themselves as a class from the commoners (maka‘āinana); although endogamy was not absolute, it was strongly encouraged. Chiefs claimed descent from the gods and internally differentiated among themselves through elaborate genealogies and a ranked gradation of levels. In addition to controlling the land and material apparatus of production, the chiefs encouraged and supported craft specialists who produced sumptuary items that visually reinforced their status.65 Most remarkable among these material symbols were finely executed feathered girdles, helmets, capes, and cloaks.

Wars of territorial aggression between competing chiefdoms and a progressive amalgamation of formerly independent chiefdoms into ever larger polities mark the political history of the Late Expansion and Proto-Historic Periods. By the late seventeenth century, four separate polities had emerged, focused on the main islands of Kaua‘i, O‘ahu, Maui, and Hawai‘i; the contested smaller islands were incorporated into one or another of the main units. However, the political dynamism of Hawai‘i in late prehistoric and early historic times emanated primarily from the two largest and youngest islands, Maui and Hawai‘i. Sahlins (1985:20) put this in social terms in Islands of History, when he observed that “the oldest and most senior [chiefly] lines are in the western islands, Kaua‘i and O‘ahu, whence originate also the highest tabus. But then, the historical dynamism of the system is in the east, among Maui and Hawai‘i chiefs, who are able to differentiate themselves from local competitors, or even from their own dynastic predecessors, by appropriating ancestry from the ancient western sources of legitimacy.” The Maui and Hawai‘i chiefs coveted the generously endowed irrigation systems that these western islands offered. Not long before Cook’s fateful visit in 1778–79, the Maui paramount Kahekili expanded his polity to encompass all of the islands to the west and was engaged in a fierce succession of wars with his archrival Kalani‘ōpu‘u of Hawai‘i. After the fateful encounter with the West, Kalani‘ōpu‘u’s successor—the famous Kamehameha I—made shrewd use of Western arms to bring the entire archipelago under his hegemony.

Various explanations have been offered for the rise of sociopolitical complexity and eventual transformation from complex chiefdoms to archaic state in Hawai‘i. Hommon (2013) puts emphasis on what he terms the “hard times” hypothesis. He sees the archaeological evidence from regions such as Kohala on Hawai‘i Island as indicating that the development and intensification of the dryland field systems “resulted in hard times comprised of increased workloads for men and women, rapid population growth, and diminished productivity due to a loss of soil fertility, and that by the late eighteenth century the limits of high-potential land had been reached or exceeded” (2013:259). This condition provided opportunities for chiefly elites that “resulted in escalated coercive development that contributed to the emergence of the Hawai‘i Island state” during the period from A.D. 1680 to 1790. Kirch (2010) suggests that the factors contributing to state formation in Hawai‘i included both “ultimate” and “proximate” forms of causation. Ultimate causes included exponential population growth and the pressure this put on resources (as well as the labor supply thus created), the intensification of wet and dryland agro-ecosystems, and the creation of significant surplus that could be extracted to fuel the political economy. Proximate causes—what is often referred to as “agency” in anthropological theory—included the status rivalry deeply ingrained in Polynesian social structures, as well as the strategies of marriage alliance and conquest warfare used by the elites to gain hegemony over their populations.





POLYNESIAN HISTORY: A CONCLUDING NOTE


In a classic early effort to compare the varied social and political structures of Polynesia, the pioneering ethnologist R.W. Williamson expressed his regret that it would not be possible to ever trace out “what actually had been the history” of these Polynesian societies (Williamson 1924:428). Writing in an era when archaeology had scarcely commenced in the Pacific—it was restricted in its methods to surface surveys with no temporal controls—Williamson believed that it would be impossible to obtain direct evidence of the history of Polynesian social transformations. He was wrong. Williamson could not envisage that the material remains of those societies themselves encapsulated a historical “text,” which in time would be accessible with improved methods and with more sophisticated theoretical paradigms enabling its interpretation. While we still have much to learn of the sociopolitical histories of the Polynesian chiefdoms and archaic states, we have traveled a great distance from Williamson’s speculative “history” based solely on comparative ethnography.

What makes the historical study of the Polynesian chiefdoms so intellectually engaging, in part, is the opportunity to compare both the varied outcomes—the specific social formations, whether Traditional, Open, or Stratified—and the underlying processes and trajectories responsible for those outcomes, all of which derived from a common Ancestral Polynesian culture over the course of two and a half millennia. Because Polynesia constitutes a well-defined phyletic unit (see chap. 7), it offers possibilities for comparative analysis not often found elsewhere. I have sketched some of the historical sequences of sociopolitical transformation that archaeology has begun to reveal and pointed to a few of the fundamental contexts, constraints, and processes that have influenced such changes. I have also endeavored to show how specific elite social groups used alternative forms of power—economic, military, and ideological—in their attempts to exercise authority and control over society at large. There is much scope for alternative—one hopes mutually enlightening rather than competitive—models of social change in Polynesia. After a half century of study, the search is becoming truly exciting.





NINE


Big Structures and Large Processes in Oceanic Prehistory



Historically grounded huge comparisons of big structures and large processes help establish what must be explained, attach the possible explanations to their context in time and space, and sometimes actually improve our understanding of those structures and processes.

CHARLES TILLY, Big Structures, Large Processes,

Huge Comparisons (1984)





SOMETIMES, THE MERE telling of a history is sufficient. Much satisfaction and pleasure may be had from teasing out the tiny dramas of human existence, as well as the grand sweep of the longue durée. In this sense, the preceding chapters stand fully on their own, requiring no further summation or exegesis. But an anthropological history carries the weight of a proud tradition of comparative analysis. Anthropology’s history (as opposed, with much deference to the late Greg Dening, to “history’s anthropology”)1 can have no special claim on historical understanding without comparison. And so, in this final chapter, I offer some observations on the long run of Pacific history, observations not tied to any one time or place, because indeed there are trends and movements that transcend the particular. It is in these “big structures” and “large processes” that any deeper insights may be discovered. They invite us to embark on a voyage beyond time to see—in the common experiences of human existence—some larger truths that might in small ways enlighten us.





VOYAGING AND THE HUMAN “CONQUEST” OF THE PACIFIC


The history of the Pacific is more than anything a history of voyages, and all that word entails: curiosity, courage, skill, technique, stamina, doubt, hope, and more. In their initial expansion out of Africa, commencing perhaps 100,000 years ago, our ancestors—modern Homo sapiens sapiens—moved on foot, spreading into the Near East, Europe, the Indian subcontinent, and Asia. Their expansion paused, for a time, on Asiatic shores now drowned beneath the warm waters of the Sunda Sea. But neither curiosity nor ingenuity could be kept in check forever; in due course some of those coastal-dwelling people invented the means to begin moving out beyond the continental margins, into the island world of the Pacific. In reality, we know next to nothing about this earliest phase in the history of seafaring technology, other than that it had developed at least by 47,000 years ago. But the explosive appearance of human populations throughout Sahul and Near Oceania at this time can only be explained by the existence of some forms of simple watercraft. Bark-boats, rafts, simple dugouts—all of these are possibilities; to say more is sheer speculation. Whatever the technology, it was sufficient to move people across water gaps of 90 kilometers or more. In most cases, the voyaging targets were visible from the starting shore: the object loomed above the horizon, enticing them with promises of a rainforest rich in edible marsupials, birds, and reptiles, or of pristine reefs where mollusks and sea urchins teemed.

Further development of voyaging technology and skills was painfully slow. Twenty thousand years after the first humans moved into the Bismarck Archipelago and the Solomons, we have the simplest hints of advances in voyaging (or at least in the frequency of movement between islands). The evidence is indirect and highly inferential: the appearance of obsidian hundreds of kilometers beyond its source and of animals “translocated” from their endemic homes. But something more profound is hinted at, a subtle difference in the way that people were beginning to perceive their island environments as well as themselves. As Chris Gosden observed, rather than always moving themselves to new resources, people began to apply voyaging technology to the movement of resources. The idea seems simple, almost trivial, but it underlies all that would come later, for the “conquest” of Remote Oceania could never have been accomplished without the ability to transport an arkful of biotic resources, from dogs and pigs to taro and breadfruit seedlings.

Transport distances in Remote Oceania are an order of magnitude greater than those within the Near Oceanic “voyaging nursery.” They could never be successfully crossed with the simple technology we infer for early humans in the Pleistocene, or even the early Holocene. Sophisticated open-water craft were called for; insofar as we can reconstruct its history, the sailing outrigger canoe complex emerged in the relatively calm waters of island Southeast Asia, perhaps between 4000 and 2000 B.C. The early Austronesian voyaging canoe, the *wangka, which we reconstruct largely from linguistic and comparative ethnographic evidence, was a marvel of “neolithic” technology. Its hull was probably a simple dugout (perhaps with greater freeboard achieved by lashing on separate washstrakes); the addition of the outrigger float gave it both stability and the possibility of carrying added cargo (on platforms lashed to the outrigger booms). The lanteen sail made of plaited mats harnessed the wind, freeing the canoe from mere drifting currents. The sailing outrigger canoe became a sharp instrument of exploration, discovery, and colonization, in the space of a few short millennia propelling people to the farthest ends of the Pacific Ocean.

The great expansion of Austronesian-speaking voyagers into Remote Oceania, after about 1200 B.C., was underwritten by more than just new and vastly improved technology. There was as well a new ideology, a different way of looking at the ocean, of envisioning lands the eye could not see but which the mind could populate with generations of descendants. This was the vision not of hunter-gatherers always moving on to new resources but of horticultural people tied to the land, for whom cultivated territory provided the fundamental basis of social life. This was, moreover, an ideology bound to a particular kind of social structure, one in which siblings were ranked by birth order and elder brothers became founders of descent lines, claiming the best lands and other resources as their own. Thus while the human settlement of Oceania is often thought of in maritime terms, as constituting a succession of great voyaging feats, these voyages were also the means to an end: the discovery of new landscapes that could be claimed, named, divided, planted, managed, and inherited.

Islanders were not content to always sit at home and tend their crops, or fish the reefs and lagoons of the inshore waters. In exploring and populating the Pacific, they gradually built up another ideology, one of the sea as highway, with myriad routes connecting one’s own island to many others. Sometimes these highways were so long and the voyages necessary to traverse them so arduous that the desire or ability to keep up contacts with one’s relations on other islands gradually waned. Thus the most remote islands and archipelagoes of the Pacific (such as Rapa Nui and Hawai‘i) after a time became isolated worlds unto themselves. But throughout much of the Pacific, a complex network of interconnections developed, maintained by seafarers and wayfinders who became transportation specialists, keepers of esoteric knowledge of star paths and wind compasses, such as Tupaia, who met Captain Cook in Tahiti, or the modern Satawal navigator Mau Piailug. These networks were not random; indeed, they became highly regularized through repeated economic and social transactions. By the time Europeans entered the Pacific, most (but not all) of its islands were connected to at least some other islands through intermeshed networks.

Yet the reticulate grid of complex voyaging networks linking many Pacific islands in late prehistory should not mislead us to conclude that things were always in flux, that the deeper history of cultural, linguistic, and biological differentiation among Pacific peoples had been wholly swamped by a frenzy of social (or, for that matter, sexual) intercourse. Contact was important, for trade and exchange, and sometimes for marriage partners. Most often, contact was with nearby islanders who shared a common history and who were closely related—linguistically, biologically, and culturally. Thus Tongans voyaged to Samoa and Fiji; the people of Nendö interacted mostly with those of nearby Utupua, Vanikoro, and the Reef Islands; the Yapese went to Palau or the closer atolls of the central Carolines. Moreover, interisland connections, although frequently valorized in song and myth, were the purview of a privileged few; most members of Oceanic societies commanded neither the skills nor the physical equipment (voyaging canoes were expensive items of property) to make overseas voyages themselves. Further, would-be voyagers were not always welcomed at their intended destinations: the Mangaians, for one, aggressively repulsed efforts at contact. A great deal of linguistic, cultural, and biological distinctiveness was thus maintained—and often consciously enhanced—by the peoples of the many Pacific islands.





HISTORY WRITTEN IN THE PRESENT: CORRELATIONS BETWEEN LANGUAGE, BIOLOGY, AND CULTURE


Despite the fundamental tenet of twentieth-century anthropology that race (i.e., human biological variation), language, and culture are independent, it has long been evident to historical anthropologists that for many regions of the world, and for certain periods of time, biology, language, and culture have significantly covaried. In a pioneering application of phylogenetic models in anthropology, Kimball Romney argued:

Physical type and language, we would say, have no causal relationship; there is no functional reason why a given physical type should occur with a given language family. Therefore, when these two variables do show significant concordance in their distribution this may well represent an important historical fact, namely that the explanation for their concordance can be traced to a common point somewhere in the past. A demonstration that these two factors are also uniquely accompanied by a systemic culture pattern . . . would strengthen the belief in a common origin. (1957:36)

Romney called this kind of concordance between biological, linguistic, and cultural patterns a “segment of cultural history,” arguing that the Uto-Aztecan-speaking peoples of North America constituted just such a segment.

Whether or not such segments of cultural history (or, as I have called them in this book, “phyletic units”) can be identified in the Pacific and, if so, what they have to tell us about human history in Oceania have invoked controversy and debate for more than a century. The greatest agreement focuses on the Polynesians, whose linguistic and cultural unity was observed well before anthropology became an academic discipline, and whose biological homogeneity has been extensively documented. The many Polynesian societies and cultures together form a robust phyletic unit—all speaking historically related languages, with common cultural patterns and a high degree of biological similarity—who descended from a common ancestral group. The application of a phylogenetic model to Polynesia is therefore not particularly controversial.

When it comes to other regions within Oceania, the degree to which biology, language, and culture are correlated has at times been hotly contested. The debate centers on the issue of whether the patterns of distribution and relationship among the Austronesian and Non-Austronesian languages of Oceania correspond, in any significant manner, with patterns of human biological variation, and with systemic cultural patterns, particularly as these are reflected in archaeological cultures.

Many pioneering ethnographers and linguists who worked in the western Pacific thought that they could detect such correlations. W.H.R. Rivers, who attempted a historical reconstruction based solely on comparative ethnography (a Pacific archaeological record did not exist in Rivers’s day), was well aware of the “great variety of physical characters of the Melanesian people,” opining that they displayed “a greater variety than the peoples of Europe” (1914, 1:582). Rivers explained this variation largely as a “result of mixture of peoples differing widely from one another in physical character.” Likewise, he recognized that language and biology can have radically different outcomes in the course of interaction between two groups, as in the case of a large local population adopting the language of a small (but socially dominant) immigrant group, yet having “relatively little effect on the physical appearance of those among whom they settled” (Rivers 1914, 1:594). His particular narrative of an ancient “dual people” who were successively influenced by new immigrant groups (the “kava-people” and the “betel-people”) may strike us as quaint, yet given his limited comparative data Rivers’s speculative “history” makes considerable sense; when its antique terminology is recast in modern terms, his sequence bears much in common with modern syntheses. The German linguist Otto Dempwolff (1934–38) developed similar ideas. He thought that the initial spread of Austronesian language speakers could be correlated with certain biological populations, although he argued that where malaria was rife, the malaria-resistant genes of the original, “darker-skinned and frizzy-haired” populations tended to dominate. Dempwolff, Rivers, and others of their day were greatly constrained in their ability to refine and test their theories, both by the lack of an archaeological record and by the “typological” (indeed, sometimes racist) approach then dominant in physical anthropology.

Some scholars argue that efforts to correlate biological, linguistic, and cultural patterns of variation in Melanesia or elsewhere in the western Pacific merely perpetuate an outdated paradigm of anthropology (e.g., Terrell 1986:63; Terrell, Hunt, and Gosden 1997). But new and more powerful analytical and comparative tools than were available to Dempwolff, Rivers, or their peers allow us to test phylogenetic hypotheses that “segments of cultural history” can be delineated in Melanesia. The results indicate that even in Melanesia—where the degree of biological, linguistic, and cultural variation is enormous—historically meaningful patterns of covariance do exist and can be detected.

Compelling evidence for correlations between language groups and biological variation in Melanesia has come, for example, from the long-term Solomon Islands Project (Friedlaender, ed., 1987, 2007), which examined patterns of human variation among eight populations including both Austronesian and Non-Austronesian speakers. Summarizing a wealth of detailed biometric and genetic evidence, Friedlaender (1987:354–57) drew several noteworthy conclusions. First, the large island of Bougainville, which includes Non-Austronesian language groups, and which “from a biogeographical perspective, is a logical place for relic populations to persist,” exhibits the greatest range of variation, with a “very distinctive biological population.” Second, the biological diversity of Bougainville, and to some extent that of its nearest neighbors, “suggests that the pre-Austronesian settlements in island Melanesia are very old, and possibly are the result of a number of separate migrations, and subsequent significant differentiation in situ of a lesser magnitude.” Third, the “Austronesian-speaking populations in Melanesia tend to show biological similarities in spite of their strong resemblances to immediately neighboring groups.” For example, there are virtually perfect correspondences between the presence or absence of certain Gm alleles and the distribution of Austronesian and Papuan languages. Likewise, fingerprint patterns show clear-cut distinctions that correlate with these major language distinctions (Froehlich 1987); and again, tooth sizes (odontometric patterns) “lend strong support to the . . . interpretation that [Non-Austronesian]-speaking peoples of Bougainville Island have an origin and history in Oceania significantly different from that of the more recent [Austronesian]-speakers” (Harris and Bailil 1987:259). Thus Friedlaender (1987:356–57) concludes that in Melanesia, “although the correspondence is hardly perfect, biologic and linguistic variation are often reflections (sometimes sharp, sometimes dim) of migrational histories.” Friedlaender modestly says that he is simply “restating a now obvious truth of anthropology,” yet it seems that such truths need to be periodically revalidated with empirical evidence for each new generation of anthropologists.

The big structure here is that the cultural, biological, and linguistic patterns characteristic of Oceania (and not just of Polynesia) are far from random. They are, rather, highly structured and, despite the complexities of their patterning, display regular correlations and concordances. This is precisely because they reflect a real history of human movements or migration into the Pacific, of dispersal or diaspora over its many islands. That such movements must have occurred, and were responsible for regularities in cultural patterning, was only roughly grasped by the pioneering ethnographers and linguists of the early twentieth century. What they lacked were the more sophisticated methodologies at our disposal today and—even more critically—a direct archaeological record. They struggled to infer a “migrational history” solely from synchronic patterns of variation, from history written in the present. It has been the contribution of archaeology to provide the properly diachronic evidence of human history in Oceania, against which the synchronic patterns in language, culture, and biology can be more fully interpreted. Knowing as we now do that the history of Near Oceania runs deep indeed—well into the Pleistocene—we can finally explain the great genetic and linguistic diversity that already existed in this region when the Austronesian-speaking peoples voyaged into the Bismarck Sea around 1300 B.C. The discovery of the Lapita cultural complex as well provides the archaeological signature of that particular human diaspora, which not only added to the heterogeneity and complexity of Melanesia but also was responsible for the peopling of the vastness of Remote Oceania.





THE ROLE OF DEMOGRAPHIC TRANSITIONS IN OCEANIC HISTORY


I turn now, in Tilly’s phrasing, from large structures to big processes, commencing with an essential demographic cycle that backgrounds the histories of a great many (possibly the majority of) Oceanic societies. First, however, we must revisit a critical contrast between the population structures of Near Oceania and those of Remote Oceania, a striking difference demanding a historical explanation. According to their ethnohistorical and archaeological records, the large archipelagoes of the Bismarcks, Solomons, and Vanuatu never supported high-density human populations, other than in very local areas. Vast parts of these islands remained in climax rainforests, which, although hardly pristine (for low-density human settlements are dispersed within them), remained largely untransformed by human activities, despite thousands of years of human presence. The absence of large populations in these fertile islands—even after indigenous food production systems arose in the Holocene—poses a puzzling contradiction. In Remote Oceania beyond Vanuatu the situation is reversed. Human populations again and again reached high density levels over relatively short time spans, even on such large landmasses as New Caledonia and Fiji.2 Moreover, these dense populations wrought enormous and often irrevocable changes in vegetation and other aspects of their ecologies.

Why this difference in population structures between the large archipelagoes of the western Pacific and those of Remote Oceania? What could possibly account for the disparity in population densities? Malaria—probably combined with other kinds of parasitic and infectious diseases. The geographic distribution of malaria in the Pacific corresponds closely to the distribution of the population structures just observed: it is found in lowland and midaltitude Near Oceania and within Remote Oceania only in the Santa Cruz Islands and Vanuatu. The effects of this disease, which one expert in the field claims has “probably resulted in more mortality in human populations than any other disease” (Livingstone 1984:413), are not to be underestimated. The one place in Near Oceania where malaria is absent—the New Guinea Highlands—is also the only region in which large and dense human populations did arise following the development of intensive agricultural production. In short, on a broad canvas the demographic history of Oceania has been intimately linked with the history of disease, especially malaria.

Once they entered Remote Oceania, human populations found themselves in a significantly healthier environment. While some Plasmodium parasites would at first have been carried in their bloodstreams, there were no Anopheles mosquitoes to act as vectors; the chain of malarial transmission was broken. These more isolated islands also lacked the diverse intestinal protozoa, bacteria, hookworms, and other disease-causing microorganisms of Near Oceania. Key conditions that had held human growth rates in check in Near Oceania were now lifted. In addition, the new islands discovered by the Lapita colonizers and their descendants were devoid of other human occupants; there were no others to fight with over territory. Rich in natural food resources (especially birds and seafoods), the high islands were well suited to planting food crops. In addition to the lack of epidemiological or environmental constraints on population increase, there were strong cultural pressures favoring high reproduction rates. With founding populations on new islands being initially small (in many cases probably consisting of the complement of but a single voyaging canoe), the risks of extinction were substantially lessened with each new birth.3 In short, conditions prevailing during the initial phases of human expansion into Remote Oceania—both environmental and cultural—almost ideally favored high rates of population increase.4

Many years ago, I put forward a model for human demographic transitions on Polynesian islands (Kirch 1980, 1984a), equally applicable throughout Remote Oceania (except Santa Cruz and Vanuatu, which lay within the malarious zone, and the temperate, nonagricultural cases of South Island and the Chathams). In the early stages of island colonization and settlement, the lack of constraints on population growth, reinforced by the obvious social advantage of increasing one’s own population (and economic production) base, favored a high intrinsic growth rate (symbolized in demographic equations by r) complemented by relatively low mortality. After some time, however (and this would vary depending on local conditions, especially island size and resources), Remote Oceanic populations typically increased to the point where human numbers began to exert various kinds of pressure (although these populations need not have reached the theoretical level of maximal “carrying capacity,” symbolized by K). The most immediate pressure would be that on food supply, although this might often be offset by intensifying production. The social marking and claiming of all available territory brought to bear another kind of pressure. That is to say, people may begin to perceive population pressure due to such socially and culturally defined criteria well before any absolute biological limits of food supply (K) are reached. In any event, during the later time periods on many Polynesian and Remote Oceanic islands, human populations clearly reached quite large numbers (in the hundreds of thousands on larger islands and archipelagoes) and—more significantly—high densities per area of arable land. Population densities frequently reached or exceeded 100 persons per square kilometer; in some cases, such as Tikopia, densities were much higher (Kirch and Rallu, eds., 2007). Under these conditions, high intrinsic rates of population increase (r) could not be sustained or tolerated, and cultural controls on population growth were implemented. As ethnographically documented, such controls included means of reducing fertility (celibacy, contraception, abortion), as well as of increasing mortality (infanticide, suicide voyaging, war, expulsion of certain groups, ritual sacrifice, and even cannibalism).5 The later stages in the demographic cycle of most Remote Oceanic islands are thus characterized by a reduced rate of population increase, in many cases probably oscillating around periods of growth and decline. In at least some cases there seems to have been dramatic decline following an actual overshoot of a diminishing or degraded resource base.

This kind of demographic cycle is sometimes referred to as an “r to K” transition, and in classical evolutionary ecology is described by a sigmoidal or S-shaped curve (the logistic), in which high initial r gradually declines as population approaches carrying capacity. MacArthur and Wilson (1967) argued that such r to K transitions were common in the evolutionary histories of island biota. More recently, the theory of “panarchy” has characterized entire ecosystems and the human social systems with which they are dynamically coupled as undergoing such r to K phase transitions (Gunderson and Holling, eds., 2002). As new models developed by the Hawai‘i Biocomplexity Project suggest, however, these demographic cycles can best be explained in terms of food availability and its effects on mortality and fertility (Kirch et al. 2012; Puleston and Tuljapurkar 2008). In the early stages of island colonization, when the population was small, available labor rather than land was the key limiting variable. Once large populations had built up, usually after at least three to four centuries, agricultural land became the limiting factor, as labor was abundant but food often in increasingly short supply. With persistent hunger—exacerbated by environmental perturbations such as drought, cyclones, or tsunamis—came damping of the population growth rate due to increased mortality (especially of infants and older people) and depressed fertility.

Some form of demographic transition—with initial high population growth rates followed by lowered rates in response to periodic food shortages as populations began to reach or oscillate around carrying capacities—seems to have been the case on most Remote Oceanic islands, even though there must have been innumerable variations on this overall theme (see Green 1993:229–30). A demographic shift from high-growth, “density-independent” to lowered-growth, “density-dependent” populations is one of the big processes underlying the histories of hundreds of Oceanic societies. I am not, however, asserting that the demographic histories of individual islands were completely identical, for everything depends on local conditions of founding group size, land and resources, elapsed time, and particular cultural and social behaviors in which reproduction and death are embedded. But I am more than ever convinced that the underlying cycles were frequently similar and had structurally similar consequences and outcomes. Among these were heightened competition for land and resources, technological and social measures to increase production, and increased impacts on the natural biota and landscapes of islands.

In highlighting an r to K population cycle as a big process of Oceanic history, I nonetheless emphatically reject a simple demographic determinism, or a unicausal explanation for the transformation of Oceanic societies. Population growth can never be a sufficient prime mover to explain cultural change; what it offers is a necessary condition without which other kinds of social, technological, ideological, or political changes are highly unlikely to occur. Here I draw insight from Ernst Mayr, whose clearheadedness has more than once enlightened evolutionary thinking, and who observed that there are nearly always both proximate and ultimate causes for any given phenomenon. What is more, “many famous controversies in the history of biology came about because one party considered only proximate causations and the other party considered only evolutionary [ultimate] ones” (Mayr 1997:67). In my view, the r/K cycle of population growth that has now been empirically demonstrated for a number of Remote Oceanic islands (and probably holds true for most others) is one fundamental kind of “ultimate” causation in the history of Oceanic peoples.6





OCEANIC POPULATIONS ON THE EVE OF EUROPEAN CONTACT


Douglas Oliver, a master of Oceanic ethnography, wrote that “estimating the number of pre-European inhabitants of Oceania is like trying to photograph a fast-moving train broadside, with a Brownie camera” (1989:27). Yet this did not stop historical demographers from “playing the numbers,” by reworking the estimates and rough census counts made by the early European explorers and missionaries. Demographers and statisticians such as McArthur (1968), Rallu (1990), and Schmitt (1968) expended much effort deriving estimates of Oceanic island populations at the time of initial contact with Europeans. Their estimates were based on a presumption that the earliest European accounts (usually made by explorers whose visits were fleeting) were typically inflated. They assumed that the depopulation that occurred following European contact (due primarily but not exclusively to the introduction of diseases to which the indigenous peoples had little or no resistance), while substantial, was not acute. Greater reliability was placed on the head counts made by missionaries who, unlike explorers, lived among their converts for extended periods and were often obsessed with quantifying the number of “pagan souls” to be saved. But in most island groups these missionaries arrived decades—and in some cases, a century or more—after initial contact with Europeans,7 leaving what happened during the intervening period a matter of dispute.8

As Pacific archaeologists increasingly turned their attention to the distribution of sites over whole landscapes and to paleodemography, these issues have come to the fore (Kirch and Rallu, eds., 2007). Consider the case of New Caledonia. Although the island was “discovered” by James Cook in 1774, the first attempt at census taking did not occur until 1887, with an estimate of 42,519 persons (Rallu 1990:272). Despite a total absence of data on what happened between 1774 and 1887, historical demographers were content to estimate the 1774 population of La Grande Terre at somewhere between 40,000 (Shineberg 1983) and possibly as high as 80,000 (Rallu 1990). Only the geographer Roux (1990), based on an evaluation of land and traditional subsistence practices, cautiously pushed the precontact population estimate as high as 100,000. The archaeologist Christophe Sand (1995:281–309), awed by the abandoned terraces that cover large expanses of the New Caledonian landscape, challenged the veracity of these estimates, suggesting that even the 100,000 figure was too low. He argued that the effects of epidemic disease were grossly underestimated, and that the demographic collapse suffered by the inhabitants of La Grande Terre after 1774 was truly catastrophic.

Spriggs (1997:253) echoed Sand’s comments for the rest of island Melanesia, pointing to the “archaeological evidence of massive population disruption and decline attendant upon European contact.” Among the archaeological signals of “catastrophic collapse” that Spriggs sees in the Melanesian archaeological record are “the abandoned village sites deep in the forests, the disused irrigation systems and other manifestations of once-intensive agricultural systems, and the marked discontinuity in settlement pattern often found between sites with European artifacts on them and those from the period immediately before contact” (1997:253–54). Such evidence occurs throughout Remote Oceania.

In Samoa, settlement pattern surveys reveal extensive and dense inland occupations, extending in some cases right up to the ridgetops.9 Yet the earliest missionary accounts of Samoa paint a landscape portrait of coastal villages with vacant interiors. Based on these vague accounts and on census counts made decades after first contact, McArthur (1968:115) suggested a maximum population for Western Samoa (‘Upolu and Savai‘i Islands) of no more than about 38,000.10 This figure represents a population density of just 13 persons per square kilometer on these fertile volcanic islands, an absurdly low figure given the high density of inland settlement revealed by archaeological survey. Ethnographically documented Polynesian subsistence agriculture regularly supported population densities of 100 persons per square kilometer; densities as high as 242 persons per square kilometer are recorded for favorable environments such as Tikopia (Kirch and Yen 1982:56). Thus it is hardly conceivable—especially in light of the archaeological evidence for dense inland settlement—that the maximum population of Samoa was less than 100,000; it might have been as great as 300,000 or more prior to European contact.11 To be sure, this hypothesis must be tested with further research.12

For no other Pacific archipelago has debate over the size of the indigenous population on the eve of Western contact become so intense as Hawai‘i. Lieutenant King, officer on Cook’s expedition at first contact in 1778–79, estimated 400,000 persons for the total population of the archipelago. Later demographic scholarship by Schmitt (1968, 1973) reduced this to 250,000 to 300,000, a widely quoted number. According to David Stannard (1989:27), however, “All the evidence available to us regarding the credibility of King’s 1778 population estimate points in precisely the opposite direction from that which most subsequent writers have alleged: King’s 400,000 estimate was, if anything, a serious under estimate of the actual figure. But how short was he?” Stannard answered his own rhetorical question by contending that the pre-1778 Hawaiian population reached a maximum of at least 800,000, and perhaps more than one million persons (1989:30). His argument turned largely on archaeological evidence, especially for dense inland agricultural field systems and settlements, and for a high windward regional population. Paleodemographic estimates of particular regions in Hawai‘i, such as Kahikinui on Maui and leeward Kohala on Hawai‘i Island (Kirch and Rallu, eds., 2007) have added empirical support for the claim that even in fairly marginal areas, the contact-era population of Hawai‘i was dense and large. Further research will be necessary before an acceptable estimate of the total population of the Hawaiian Islands at contact can be achieved. But that estimate will undoubtedly be much larger than the low numbers put forward by the late twentieth-century historical demographers.





THE POLITICAL ECONOMY OF DYNAMIC LANDSCAPES


If most Remote Oceanic islands were densely populated at the time of European intrusion in the sixteenth through eighteenth centuries, their landscapes had also been irrevocably transformed by generations of human effort. In Near Oceania, landscape transformation seems not to have been so pronounced (although the New Guinea Highlands, with their anthropogenic grasslands and intensively cultivated valley floors, are more like Remote Oceania in this regard), for as I have argued, population densities in the Near Oceanic region generally did not reach the levels seen in Remote Oceania. Even in the Bismarcks and Solomons, however, the hand of Homo sapiens left its mark in many ways, from the extinction of bird species to the translocation of the cuscus and other animals (even land snails) to the modification of lowland vegetation communities. But in Remote Oceania, impacts wrought by human populations on island ecosystems were often far-reaching and—if viewed from the perspective of the natural biota—frequently disastrous. The wholesale decimation and extinction of many species of sea- and land birds from these islands, the removal of vast tracts of indigenous forest and their replacement with pyrophytic grass- and fernlands (fig. 9.1), erosion and alluviation in valleys and along coastal plains—these and other impacts are now well documented for many islands (Kirch 2007a, 2007c; Kirch and Hunt, eds., 1997; Steadman 2006). Indeed, it can fairly be said that the island ecosystems of Remote Oceania exemplify what has recently been called the “Anthropocene Epoch” in geological history, an era in which humans have become the dominant force shaping the earth’s landscape, its biota, and even its climate (Rick et al. 2013).




FIGURE 9.1 The fern-covered hills of Mangaia Island, dotted here and there with scrub ironwood trees, typify the fire-resistant, degraded landscapes of many tropical Pacific islands. Note the coconut palms, which are restricted to the narrow valley bottom. (Photo by P. V. Kirch.)





In part, such cultural modifications of the natural ecology of islands stemmed directly from the food-getting and food-producing activities of Pacific islanders, whether hunting birds or clearing forest for shifting cultivation. Other impacts were indirect and followed from the introduction of domestic plants and animals, and of “weed species” (like the ubiquitous Pacific rat). Cumulatively and incrementally, even small inroads to natural biotic communities had far-reaching effects over time. Islands such as Mangareva and Rapa Nui were largely denuded of all arboreal vegetation prior to European contact; even on large high islands such as Viti Levu the lower-elevation vegetation communities were thoroughly modified (Ash 1992). Once again, this is a big process, an ecological process with enormous consequences for island landscapes and for island cultures.

Establishing a paleoenvironmental record for the Pacific islands—one that includes the role of indigenous human populations as major players in the transformation of insular ecologies—has been one of the more significant contributions of Oceanic archaeology, in collaboration with the natural sciences, over the past four decades. Not only has Pacific archaeology overthrown the old ethnographic bias of “static” island cultures (in which change was seen to derive primarily from migration; see chap. 1); it has demonstrated that in the process of transforming themselves, island cultures forever changed their microcosmic worlds (Kirch 1997b).

The outcomes of ecological change on islands differ radically depending on whether these are viewed from the perspective of the natural flora and fauna or the people who initiated these changes. For a species of flightless rail or native lorikeet, or for an endemic lobelia or palm, the outlook after the arrival of humans on its island home was frankly bleak. It is no exaggeration to say that among the Remote Oceanic islands, the “biodiversity crisis” began not recently but 3,200 years ago with the Lapita expansion. It now seems certain that literally thousands of endemic species of plants and animals (especially birds, insects, and land snails, as well as vascular plants and ferns) underwent extinction or local extirpation as a consequence—direct and indirect—of the human “conquest” of the Pacific islands.

Viewed from the perspective of the human “conquerors,” the transformation of island landscapes was altogether different, for the most part a highly desirable series of modifications, improvements, innovations, and intensifications that quite literally made the islands habitable. For indeed, oceanic islands—despite their famously salubrious climates, lack of disease-inducing organisms, and physical beauty—were not naturally well suited to supporting dense human populations. True, many islands abounded in birds, fish, and shellfish, which offered ample supplies of protein (at least in the early phases of colonization when human numbers were relatively low, prior to the effects of “resource depression”), but vegetable foods—especially the staple starches on which every complex society has ultimately been built—were entirely lacking. It was essential, as human populations burgeoned on these islands, for their landscapes to increasingly undergo a transformation from natural to cultural, from merely extractive to highly productive resource bases.

On many islands, the process of creating a productive infrastructure of gardens and fields was remarkably successful, evoking admiration from anyone who has ever struggled to wrest food from the ground, whether in an urban backyard plot or on a grander rural scale. The agroforests, or “orchard gardens,” of Tikopia, Pohnpei, and Kosrae are marvels of “sustainable agriculture,” and the irrigated taro pondfields of Hawai‘i, Mangaia, and Rapa were so efficient and successful that they continue to be used by contemporary farmers virtually without modification. The development of these and other innovative and intensified production systems literally fueled the rise of large and complex societies throughout the oceanic world.
