16. On the marsupial fauna of Near Oceania, see Carter, Hill, and Tate 1945; Flannery 1994. Another introduction—evidently purposeful—of the small wallaby Thylogale brownii from New Guinea to New Ireland occurred around the end of the Pleistocene. Flannery et al. (1988) discuss archaeological evidence for faunal changes in the Holocene period, and Flannery and White (1991) discuss animal translocations.

17. From a site in the Sepik-Ramu area of New Guinea, dated to 14,000 B.P., Yen (1990:262) reports the presence of Canarium nut shells, suggestive of initial efforts to domesticate this important Melanesian tree crop.

18. On New Guinea and the Near Oceanic region as a center for plant domestication, see Barrau 1965a, 1965b; Denham 2003, 2004, 2007, 2011; Denham, Fullagar, and Head 2009; Yen 1971, 1973, 1990, 1991, 1995.

19. On the indigenous development of agriculture in Near Oceania, see Fairbairn 2005; Denham 2003, 2005, 2011; Golson 1988, 1990, 1991a; Spriggs 1993b, 1993c; Yen 1990, 1995.

20. These complex drainage systems have been described many times in the anthropological literature (e.g., Feil 1987; Pospisil 1963).

21. The long-awaited monograph on the Kuk site appeared as this book was in page proof (Golson et al., eds., 2017). On the Kuk excavations see also Bayliss-Smith and Golson 1992; Denham 2004; Denham and Haberle 2008; Denham et al. 2003; Golson 1977, 1988, 1990, 1991a, 1997; Golson and Gardner 1990; Wilson 1985; see also Feil 1987.

22. Muke and Mandui (2003) report on a wetland agricultural site at Kana, near the confluence of the Wahgi and Minj Rivers, with a similar stratigraphy of superimposed ditches and other cut features, similar in many respects to Kuk. Radiocarbon dates from Kana, however, suggest that the earliest agricultural phase here dates to about 3,000 years ago.

23. Spriggs (1997:73–82) provides a more elaborate discussion of these sites.

24. Swadling et al. (1989) report key geomorphological and radiometric data, and further details are provided in Swadling, Araho, and Ivuyo 1991; Swadling 1997. Color photos of the sites and some finds are provided in Swadling et al. 1988.

25. Swadling et al. (1989) also reported the presence of betel nut (Areca catechu) at the Dongan site, although direct radiocarbon dating of the plant specimen later showed it to be a modern intrusion into the site (Fairbairn and Swadling 2005).

26. Yen (1991:84, fig. 6) notes that P. Gorecki recovered Canarium remains from a mid-Sepik site that may indicate domestication of this tree crop in the late Pleistocene.

27. One unresolved issue regarding these pre-Lapita subsistence economies in lowland Near Oceania is the presence of the domestic pig. Gorecki, Mabin, and Campbell (1991) report pig bones “throughout the deposit” of the Taora shelter on the Vanimo coast of New Guinea, dated to the mid-Holocene. In the Bismarcks, the only claim for pre–Lapita period pigs is by Allen (1996:22–23), for two pig teeth from levels dated by charcoal samples to 8000 and 6000 B.P., in Matenbek. Allen (pers. comm. 1998) believes these are in situ finds, although one cannot entirely discount the possibility that they are intrusive. An attempt to date one of the two teeth directly by the accelerator mass spectrometer 14C method yielded an age of “modern,” bolstering the argument for intrusion, although Allen (pers. comm. 1998) suggests “contamination.”

28. For Taora, Gorecki, Mabin, and Campbell (1991:120–21) report that 78 sherds were recovered but that “most are in the upper levels,” with an associated radiocarbon age of 2250 ± 70 B.P. (which postdates Lapita). Although they note the “possible vertical displacement of artefacts” in the site, they remain “confident that pottery first appears at Taora about 5400 years ago.” Judgment is best reserved until the promised additional analysis is provided. Similarly, the claims of Swadling et al. (1989) for pottery dated to 5600 B.P. in the Ramu area lack conclusive dating of their stratigraphic contexts and full ceramic analysis and description.

29. Of these, the most notable is the α-thalassemia deletion, ubiquitous in Near Oceanic populations, which must already have been present there by 3500 B.P., because it was transferred to certain Austronesian-speaking groups who subsequently migrated into Remote Oceania and Polynesia (Hill et al. 1985; Martinson 1996).





CHAPTER FOUR


1. Kirch 1997a is a book-length review of the Lapita cultural complex. Sand and Bedford, eds., 2010 is a collection of essays in conjunction with an exhibit of Lapita objects at the Musée Quai Branly in Paris. Other overviews of Lapita include Golson 1971; Gosden 1991a; Green 1978, 1979b, 1991a, 1997, 2003; Kirch and Hunt 1988; Specht 2007; Specht et al. 2014; Spriggs 1984, 1997; Summerhayes 2000a, 2001b, 2007a, 2007b. Several edited volumes also offer contributions to the Lapita literature, too numerous to cite individually: Addison and Sand 2008; Chiu and Sand 2007; Clark, Anderson, and Vunidilo 2001; Galipaud and Lilley 1999; Lilley 2006; Sand 2003; Sand, Chiu, and Hogg 2015; Sheppard, Thomas, and Summerhayes 2009; Stevenson, Lee, and Morin 2001; Summerhayes and Buckley 2013.

2. Buried midden sites dating to ca. 6000–5000 B.P. in the Sepik-Ramu River basin, such as Dongon (Swadling, Araho, and Ivuyo 1991), may be an exception, but their geographic extent has yet to be determined.

3. This diversity was doubtless even greater ca. 2000 B.C., because Non-Austronesian languages once spoken throughout the Bismarck and Solomon groups were likely replaced by Austronesian languages or went extinct.

4. On the α-thalassemia deletion and its significance, see S.J. Allen et al. 1997; Boyce, Harding, and Martinson 1995; and Hill et al. 1985.

5. Early syntheses of the Lapita radiocarbon chronology were provided by Green (1979b), followed by Kirch and Hunt, eds. (1988), Specht and Gosden (1997), Spriggs (1990b), and Specht (2007). Denham, Ramsey, and Specht (2012) applied Bayesian calibration methods to dates from Lapita sites both in the Bismarcks and in Remote Oceania; their chronology is followed here.

6. Arguments for an indigenous, island Melanesian origin of Lapita were presented by Allen (1984a), Allen and White (1989), and White, Allen, and Specht (1988).

7. Among his key writings on Lapita in relation to island Southeast Asian culture history, see Bellwood 1993, 1998, 2017.

8. For overviews of Austronesian linguistics, see Blust 1985, 1996, 1999; Pawley and Ross 1993, 1995; Ross 1989.

9. Dendritic, or “tree,” models are by no means the only kinds used by Austronesian and Oceanic linguists; they have developed sophisticated dialect chain and linkage models as well (see Pawley and Ross 1993, 1995; Ross 1995, 1997). The importance of linkage joining and breaking models for Oceanic culture history is discussed in Kirch and Green 2001.

10. An asterisk before a word indicates that it is a reconstruction rather than a cognate of the proto-lexeme in a descendant language. In the case of Proto Austronesian *tales (taro), a few of the reflexes occurring in modern Austronesian languages are Bunan (Taiwan) tai?, Tongan talo, and Hawaiian kalo.

11. This deletion was incorporated into the gene pool of Lapita populations who expanded eastward into then-unoccupied Remote Oceania, so that this genetic marker shows up even today in low frequency in Polynesian populations (Hill et al. 1985).

12. See also Gosden 1991b, 1992a; Kirch 1996a, 1997a.

13. Green (1979b) and Kirch (1997a) provided early inventories of Lapita sites. Anderson et al. (2001) greatly expanded that inventory, which was further updated by Bedford, Sand, and Connaughton, eds. (2007), for a total of 227 sites.

14. On radiocarbon dates from Lapita sites, see Denham, Ramsey, and Specht 2012; Green 1979b; Kirch 1997a:table 3.1; Nunn and Petchey 2013; Sand 1997; Specht 2007; Specht and Gosden 1997; Spriggs 1990b; Summerhayes 2001a.

15. This age range follows the Bayesian calibration of Denham, Ramsey, and Specht (2012). Recent AMS dates on Canarium nut shells from the ECA site in Mussau (Kirch, unpublished data), however, suggest to me that the onset of Lapita in the Bismarck Archipelago may not be much earlier than about 1250 B.C.

16. On the late Lapita and other ceramic assemblages of the New Georgia group, see Felgate 2001, 2003; Summerhayes and Scales 2005.

17. On Lapita sites in Vanuatu, see also Bedford 2006a, 2006b, 2007; Bedford and Spriggs 2000, 2007, 2008, 2014.

18. On the Teouma site and its chronology see Bedford, Spriggs, and Regenvanu 2006; Petchey et al. 2014, 2015. On lithic artifacts, see Reepmeyer et al. 2011. On the analysis of the Lapita skeletal population from various perspectives, see Bentley et al. 2007; Kinaston et al. 2014; and Valentin et al. 2010, 2011, 2016.

19. Key literature on Lapita sites in New Caledonia and the Loyalty Islands includes Frimigacci 1974, 1975; Galipaud 1988, 1992; Gifford and Shutler 1956; Sand 1995, 1997, 2000b, 2001, 2010, 2013, 2015.

20. On Lapita voyaging capabilities, see Irwin 2008; Irwin and Flay 2015.

21. On Lapita sites in the Lau Islands, see Best 1984, 2002.

22. For linguistic evidence bearing on early Austronesian and Proto Oceanic social organization, see Bellwood (1996a; Fox 1994; Lichtenberk 1986; Pawley 1982.

23. See, e.g., Hawaiian myths cited in Beckwith 1970; Luomala 1955.

24. On Oceanic languages and their relationships, see Pawley 2007, 2009; Pawley and Green 1973, 1984; Pawley and Ross 1993, 1995; Ross 1988, 1989.

25. Two exceptions in Micronesia are the languages of Palau and the Marianas, which are Austronesian (specifically, Western Malayo-Polynesian) languages but not Oceanic (see chap. 6).

26. Donohue and Denham (2008, 2012) question the association of Lapita with Oceanic, partly due to a posited “Papuan influence” on the languages of Vanuatu. However, the presence of certain Non-Austronesian linguistic features in the Vanuatu languages can just as readily be explained by a “second wave” of Papuan-speaking migrants following the first “wave” of Oceanic speakers, who later abandoned their language(s) “in favor of the more uniform and widely dispersed speech of late Proto-Oceanic speakers, but not before leaving traces of their former presence in the form of typological divergence toward a pattern that is more typical of Papuan languages” (Blust 2008:445).

27. Malcolm Ross (1988, 1989) discusses the high-order subgrouping of Oceanic languages in Near Oceania and the breakup of the Proto Oceanic dialect chain into several first-order clusters. On the difference between linguistic divergence by dialect differentiation and by separation, see Ross 1995, 1997.

28. On the notions of foundation cultures and languages, see Pawley and Green 1973, 1984.

29. It was previously thought that another exception to exclusively Oceanic languages within Remote Oceania was the Reef–Santa Cruz Islands, where it had been posited that one or more languages were Non-Austronesian or Papuan. However, Ross and Næss (2007) show that all of the languages spoken throughout this culturally diverse group of islands are Oceanic.

30. See, for example, the essays in the volumes edited by Pawley and Ross (1994) and Ross, Pawley, and Osmond (1998). Blust (1987) outlines methodological aspects of lexical and semantic reconstruction.

31. On the 9-base-pair deletion, see Hagelberg 1997; Hertzberg et al. 1989; Merriwether et al. 1999.

32. Some key papers on the “genetic trail” include the chapters in Hill and Serjeantson 1989, as well as Friedlaender et al. 2008; Lum 1998; Lum and Cann 1998; Lum et al. 1998; Martinson 1996; Melton et al. 1998; Merriwether et al. 1999; Serjeantson and Gao 1995; Serjeantson, Ryan, and Thompson 1982.

33. On sand tempers and their significance for tracing interisland movement of pottery, see Dickinson 1998; Dickinson and Shutler 1979.

34. Key references on Lapita pottery decoration include Anson 1983, 1986; Cochrane 2013; Donovan 1973; Golson 1971; Green 1976b, 1978, 1979a, 1979b, 1990; Kirch 1988b, 1997a; Marshall 1985, 2008; Mead et al. 1975; Sharp 1988, 1991; Shaw 1975; Summerhayes 1997;

35. On Lapita anthropomorphic designs, see Kirch 1997a:132–40; Spriggs 1990a, 1993a.

36. On tattooing and Lapita, see Ambrose 2012; Kononenko 2012.

37. The distinction between pervasive and partitive art styles is from De Boer 1991.

38. On the Mulifanua site and its geological context, see Dickinson and Green 1998.

39. I base these estimates on ethnographic information for house sizes in Oceania, with roughly 50 square meters for a “typical” dwelling, surrounded by another 500 square meters of open activity space incorporating cook sheds or other ancillary structures.

40. Details of the important RF-2 site have been reported in Green 1976b, 1986, 1991c; Sheppard 1992, 1993; Sheppard and Green 1991.

41. This argument was set out by Groube (1971) and countered in articles by Green (1979b) and Kirch (1979b).

42. On linguistic evidence for Proto Oceanic crops and horticulture, see French-Wright 1983; Osmond 1998; Ross 1996b. Lichtenberk (1994) deals with food preparation methods. The linguistic evidence for horticulture and food preparation is contrasted with archaeological evidence in Kirch 1997a:192–217.

43. On plant remains from Lapita sites, see Gosden 1992b, 1995; Kirch 1989b. Microfossil plant evidence for aroids and yams in Lapita contexts is presented by Horrocks and Bedford (2005) and Horrocks and Nunn (2007).

44. Kirch (1989b) describes the plant remains from Talepakemalai, while a detailed analysis of their morphology and metrical characteristics is given by Lepofsky, Kirch, and Lertzman (1998).

45. See Gosden and Webb 1994:46; this erosional layer has an associated radiocarbon date of 2870 ± 70 B.P.

46. Diffusion of terracing and irrigation technology out of Asia was argued by Spriggs (1982) but countered on archaeological and linguistic evidence by Kirch and Lepofsky (1993).

47. For a review of DNA evidence for the origins of pigs, dogs, and chickens in the Pacific, see Matisoo-Smith 2009.

48. Marine faunal remains from Lapita sites are discussed in V.L. Butler 1988, 1994; Green 1986; Nagaoka 1988; Swadling 1986. Kirch and Dye (1979) and Walter (1989b) treat aspects of Lapita fishing.

49. Oceanic peoples widely use the grated or pounded roots or bark of such plants as Barringtonia asiatica and Derris elliptica as narcotics to stupefy fish in shallow waters. The names of these plants can be reconstructed for Proto Oceanic, and their narcotic properties were probably known to the Lapita peoples.

50. On shell fishhooks from the Arawe Islands sites, see Specht et al. 2016.

51. On the Sylviornis remains, see Balouet and Olson 1989; Sand 1995. On horned tortoises, see Hawkins et al. 2016; A.W. White et al. 2010.

52. On the extinct iguana, see Pregill and Dye 1989; Steadman (2006) discusses the extinct megapodes of Samoa.

53. On the kula, see Leach and Leach 1983; Malinowski 1922; Weiner 1992. Davenport (1962) discusses the Santa Cruz exchange network. Aspects of these networks are formally analyzed by Hage and Harary (1991, 1996).

54. The literature on sourcing and material evidence for Lapita exchange is extensive; some key articles include Ambrose and Green 1972; Bellwood and Koon 1989; Best 1987; Green 1974b, 1996a; Kirch 1990d, 1991c, 1996a; Lilley 1986, 1988; Sheppard 1993; Spriggs 1984, 1991a; Summerhayes 1997, 2000a; Wickler 1990. The argument for shell valuables was advanced by Kirch (1988c; 1997a:236–39). An excellent overview of shell working and shell artifacts is provided by Szabó (2010).

55. Evidence for the Mussau exchange system is presented in Kirch 1987, 1988d, and 1990d.

56. In keeping with the terminology proposed by Kirch and Green (1987), I would distinguish between Ancestral Oceanic culture and societies, the Proto Oceanic language they spoke, and the Parental Oceanic populations (as biological entities). In this sense, Lapita is the archaeological manifestation of Ancestral Oceanic culture.

57. See Lichtenberk 1986; Pawley 1982.

58. The concept of heterarchy has been elaborated by several archaeologists in recent years (e.g., Ehrenreich, Crumley, and Levy, eds., 1995).

59. Most likely, these basic social groups constituted some form of what Austronesian scholars now refer to as a “house,” based on the “house society” concept (sociétés à maison) of Claude Lévi-Strauss (1982) and other works; see Carsten and Hugh-Jones 1995; Fox 1995.





CHAPTER FIVE


1. Overviews of Melanesian archaeology include Spriggs 1993b, 1997; White and Allen 1980.

2. Classic ethnographic accounts of these trade and exchange networks include Malinowski 1922; Seligman 1910; Williams 1932. For an ethnographic overview of the hiri system, see also Skelly and David 2017.

3. Allen (1972) excavated at Nebira and did extensive work at Motupore Island, a major trading center (Allen 1977, 1984b; Allen, Holdaway, and Fullagar 1997). Bulmer (1971, 1975) provides settlement pattern and ceramic data on the Port Moresby area. Mailu was intensively studied by Irwin (1974, 1978a, 1978b, 1985a; Irwin and Holdaway 1996). The early site of Oposisi has been reported in summary form by Vanderwal (1978). Bickler (1997), David (2008), David et al. (2009, 2010), Frankel and Rhoads (1994), and Rhoads (1980) deal with the Gulf of Papua.

4. Reconnaissance data are reported by Egloff (1978) and Lauer (1970, 1971), while stratigraphic excavations at Collingwood Bay are reported by Egloff (1979). Irwin (1983) provides additional perspective on the prehistory of the kula region.

5. This hypothesis is supported by the occurrence of one piece of Fergusson Island obsidian in a Lapita site in the Reef–Santa Cruz Islands (Green and Bird 1989), suggesting that Lapita peoples had some connection to the Massim region.

6. Burenhult, ed., 2002 presents some archaeological reconnaissance data from the Trobriand Islands, including illustrations of potsherds with a variety of decorative techniques.

7. Some influences may have filtered into the Highlands via the Sepik-Ramu drainages. If the arrival of the pig in Near Oceania was in fact associated with the expansion of Austronesian speakers, then its presence in the Highlands—where it became the economic foundation of social exchanges—would constitute a significant impact.

8. The main sources for this period in the Highlands are the pioneering study by Bulmer and Bulmer (1964), White’s (1972; White and Thomas 1972) excavations at four rockshelters, Watson and Cole’s (1977; Cole 1996) excavations at eight open sites in Kainantu, Christensen’s (1975) work at four rockshelters in the Manim Valley, and Golson’s study of the Kuk agricultural site (Bayliss-Smith and Golson 1992; Golson 1977, 1988, 1990, 1991a, 1997; Golson and Gardner 1990). On connections between the Highlands and the northern coast, see Gorecki and Gillieson, eds., 1989; Swadling 1997.

9. For summaries of the pollen sequences and their implications for changing vegetation patterns, see Haberle 1996; Haberle, Hope, and DeFretes 1991; Walker and Hope 1982.

10. White and O’Connell (1982:table 6.5) summarize these data.

11. On Highlands trade, see the historical ethnographic synthesis by Hughes (1977).

12. Goodenough (1997) disagrees with aspects of Ross’s model for Proto Oceanic, suggesting that a Proto Kimbe language was part of an Eastern Oceanic dialect chain that was part of the Lapita trade network, and later broke up into several dialect groups after the W-K2 Witori volcanic eruption.

13. On Lossu, see White 1992; White and Downie 1980; on the Lasigi excavation and ceramics, see Golson 1991b; and on Fissoa, see White and Murray-Wallace 1996.

14. Watom was the site of Otto Meyer’s discovery of Lapita (Meyer 1909, 1910). It was reinvestigated by Specht (1968b), and by Green and Anson (1987, 1991) as part of the Lapita Homeland Project.

15. On Manus ceramics, see Ambrose 1991.

16. See Ambrose 1988, 1991; and the discussion in Spriggs 1997:162–65, pl. 24.

17. The Sabah obsidian is reported by Bellwood and Koon (1989).

18. The ethnographic system is described by Harding (1967).

19. There are additional indications of contacts between Mussau and Micronesia, such as the presence of the backstrap loom in both localities and possible Micronesian loan words into Mussau (Blust 1984; Ross 1988).

20. For a review of archaeological research in the Solomon Islands see Walter and Sheppard (2009). Sheppard and Walter (2006) present a generalized “revised model” for Solomon Islands culture history.

21. Specht (1968a) initially defined the Buka ceramic sequence, while new work has been carried out by Wickler (1990, 2001). The Nissan sequence is reported by Spriggs (1991c, 1997). Terrell (1986) incorporates data from the large island of Bougainville.

22. Chikamori (1967) reconnoitered New Georgia, followed in 1971 by work by Yen, Rosendahl, and myself on Kolombangara Island (see Yen 1976a). Miller (1979, 1980) carried out site surveys in the western Solomons in the late 1970s. The Panaivili site is reported by Reeve (1989). The most important recent work in the New Georgia group has been carried out by Sheppard and Walter (2006, 2009); Sheppard, Walter, and Nagaoka (2000); Sheppard, Walter, and Aswani (2004); T. Thomas (2009, 2014); and Walter and Sheppard (2001, 2006, 2009).

23. On the late Lapita intertidal ceramic sites in New Georgia, see Felgate 2001, 2003; Sheppard and Walter 2009; Summerhayes and Scales 2005. Carter et al. 2012 reports ceramics in a similar intertidal setting on Santa Isabel Island, although the age of these ceramics has not yet been determined.

24. Davenport (1972) reports on an excavation on Santa Ana Island. Green (1976c, 1976d), Hendren (1976), Kaschko (1976), and Ward (1976) provide archaeological and ethnoarchaeological reports from Uki and Ulawa.

25. An earlier date of A.D. 1055–1295 was also obtained from the Su‘ena site but was rejected as being inconsistent with the four other dates (Walter and Green 2011:28).

26. The first phase (1970–71) of the SSICHP was summarized in Green and Cresswell, eds., 1976. No final report of the second phase (1977–78) was produced, but key results are presented in Kirch 1983; Kirch and Yen 1982; McCoy and Cleghorn 1988.

27. On the dating of early Lapita sites in the Santa Cruz group, see Green and Jones 2008; Green, Jones, and Sheppard 2008; Sheppard, Chiu, and Walter 2015.

28. On pottery traditions of Vanuatu, see Galipaud 1996a, 1996b.

29. Congruence between oral traditions and archaeology in the late period of Tikopia is discussed by Kirch and Yen (1982). On Vanuatu oral traditions and archaeology see Rivierre 1996.

30. See Robin, Monzier, and Eissen 1994; Spriggs 1997:215–18.

31. For overviews of the Polynesian Outliers, see Bayard 1976; Feinberg and Scaglion 2012. Pawley (1967) reviews the linguistic relationships of the Polynesian Outlier languages. The Micronesian Outliers Nukuoro and Kapingamarangi are discussed in chapter 6.

32. For Tikopia, Firth (1961) documents oral traditions regarding the origins of lineages from various sources, mostly to the west. The most famous of these involves the Taumako clan, tracing its ancestry to a reputed offspring of the Tu‘i Tonga, or paramount chief, of Tonga. See Kirch and Yen 1982:362–68 for a discussion of these traditions in relation to the archaeological record.

33. This was part of the study of drift voyaging probabilities in the Pacific by Levison, Ward, and Webb (1973); see also Ward, Webb, and Levison 1973.

34. On Nukuoro, see Davidson 1967a, 1967b, 1971. The Anutan archaeological sequence presented by Kirch and Rosendahl (1973, 1976) was revised by Kirch (1982b). Poulsen (1972) excavated on Bellona, while Chikamori (1975; Chikamori and Takasugi 1985) studied Rennell. Other work on the Outliers Futuna, Fila, and Aniwa in Vanuatu is reported by Shutler and Shutler (1965, 1968) and excavations on Mele Island by Garanger (1972a).

35. Archaeological sequences from the Outliers are synthesized by Kirch (1984b).

36. Key archaeological results from Tikopia are presented in Kirch and Yen 1982; see also Kirch 1997a; Steadman, Pahlavan, and Kirch 1990.

37. Kirch and Yen (1982) put initial settlement at 900 B.C., but new AMS dates from the earliest excavation contexts indicate that 800 B.C. is more accurate (P.V. Kirch and J. Swift, unpublished data).

38. Dickinson (in Kirch and Yen 1982:372) examined these sherds petrographically; they are similar to Mangaasi-style sherds from Emo in Vanikoro. Both the Tikopia and Vanikoro pottery wares were probably imported from Vanuatu (possibly Santo).

39. The genetic evidence is provided in Blake et al. 1983, although its cultural historical significance was first pointed out by Kirch (1985b).

40. Leach and Davidson (2008) put the beginning of the Tavatava Phase at 1000 B.C., but this is clearly too early in light of their two radiocarbon dates for this phase.

41. On the extinct biota of New Caledonia, see Balouet and Olson 1989; Flannery 1994; Sand 1995.

42. Classic ethnographic summaries of New Caledonian societies include Guiart 1963; and Leenhardt 1937. On linguistic diversity in New Caledonia, see Kasarherou 1989.

43. For example, the French ethnographer Maurice Leenhardt maintained that the Kanak peoples were a “paleo-mélanésien” group, even retaining traits “plus primitifs que ceux de l’Australien . . . et que ceux de l’homme de Néanderthal” (1937:12–13)!

44. In addition to Gifford and Shutler 1956, Green and Mitchell 1983, and a reanalysis of the Gifford-Shutler collections in Kirch et al., eds., 1997, important works on New Caledonian and Loyalty Island archaeology and prehistory include the PhD dissertations of Frimigacci (1975), Galipaud (1988; see also Galipaud 1990, 1992, 1995), and Sand (1993a, 1994, 1995; see also Sand 1996a, 1996b, 1996c, 1996d, 1998c). Sand’s bibliography (1995) encompasses more specialized articles and reports. Sand (1996c) offers an English-language overview of New Caledonian archaeology. The Service Territorial des Musées et du Patrimoine of New Caledonia publishes a monograph series, Cahiers de l’Archéologie en Nouvelle-Calédonie.

45. On the paddle-impressed Podtanean pottery, see Galipaud 1988, 1990, 1992.

46. Sand and Ouétcho (1993) define Puen Ware.

47. The tradition takes its name from Gifford and Shutler’s site 26, Oundjo village, the ceramic assemblages of which are reanalyzed in Kirch et al., eds., 1997.

48. On palynological and sedimentary evidence for human impacts to the New Caledonian landscape, see Stevenson 1998, 2004; Stevenson and Dodson 1995; Stevenson, Dodson, and Prosser 2001. Dotte-Sarout et al. (2013) discuss the use of charcoal evidence to trace vegetation changes in New Caledonia. Faunal extinctions in New Caledonia are discussed by Balouet and Olson (1989), Flannery (1995), and White et al. (2010).

49. These highly intensive agricultural systems are described in Barrau 1956.

50. The Païta work is described in Sand and Ouétcho 1993; see also Sand 1995:138–40, 171–81.

51. On the history of the Fijian languages, see Geraghty 1983.

52. In addition to Gifford (1951), see Birks (1973) on the initial Sigatoka excavations, Shaw (1967) on a reanalysis of Gifford’s collections, and Davidson and Leach (1993) and Davidson et al. (1990) on the early Lapita site at Natunuku. The early Lapita site on Naigani Island is reported by Kay (1984) and by Irwin et al. (2011), while Nunn et al. (2007) report a Lapita occupation at Naitabale, and Nunn et al. (2004) provide a brief report on the early Bourewa Lapita site. Anderson and Clark (1999; Clark and Anderson 2001) discuss radiocarbon dates for initial Fijian settlement, and the full results of the ANU “Early Prehistory of Fiji” project are presented in Clark and Anderson, eds., 2009. Sand, Valentin, and Sorovi-Vunidilo (2000) report on archaeological research on the islands of Cikobia and Naqelelevu.

53. Renewed investigations at Sigatoka are reported in Burley 2005; Burley and Edinborough 2014; Marshall et al. 2000; Anderson et al. 2006.

54. Parry (1977, 1981, 1984, 1987) used aerial photography in his study of fortifications and agricultural features. Frost (1974, 1979) reports on ceramic typology as well as fortifications. Rechtman (1992) excavated two fortified sites on Wakaya Island. Best (1984) traces the cultural sequence of Lakeba from Lapita colonization to the early contact period. Palmer (1969) reports fortified sites on Viti Levu. Parke (1998) describes rescue excavations at the Navatanitawake ceremonial mound on Bau Island, a proto-historic and early postcontact chiefly center. Field (1998, 2003, 2004) has extensively investigated fortifications and intensified agricultural landscapes in the interior of the Sigatoka Valley. Settlement patterns and fortifications of the Vatia Peninsula of Viti Levu are described by Robb and Nunn (2012).

55. Shaw (1967) modified a few aspects of Green’s sequence; see also Babcock 1977. Rossitto (1995) discusses ceramic change in the postcontact Ra Phase. For a perspective on Fijian ceramics based on a “selectionist” evolutionary approach, see Cochrane 2009. The “orthodox” four-phase model of Fijian prehistory has been critiqued by Marshall et al. (2000), but see Anderson et al. 2006.

56. Claims for a date of 1220 B.C. for the Bourewa Lapita site (Nunn et al. 2004) are not supported by the radiocarbon evidence; Clark and Anderson, eds. (2009:175) put the age of Bourewa at 2870–3080 B.P.

57. Linguistically, a dialect chain linked these early settlements in Fiji, Lau, and Tonga-Samoa (Geraghty 1983).

58. Bellwood (1979:264) relates Vunda Phase developments to the arrival of a historical figure, named Lutunasobasoba, a prominent ancestor in Fijian oral traditions.

59. For sedimentological and palynological evidence of these human-induced environmental changes, see Bayliss-Smith et al. 1988; Hughes et al. 1979.

60. Kirch (1988c) recovered Fijian sherds on Niuatoputapu, while Dye (1988) noted the same for sites in the Ha‘apai group. See also Davidson (1977) and Green (1996a) regarding prehistoric contacts between these archipelagoes.

61. The “Tongan maritime empire” has been discussed by various authors, including Guiart (1963), Kaeppler (1978), and Kirch (1984a); see further discussion in chapter 8.

62. On the Lau Islands and their relationship to Tonga, see Hocart 1929; Thompson 1940.

63. One example is the elaboration of kava ritual as an overtly political activity. In technology, another example may be the diffusion of pondfield terracing from Fiji to Futuna (see Kirch 1994).

64. The terms exchange and trade are sometimes used interchangeably by archaeologists. I use the former for systems in which economic transactions underwrite, or are accompanied by, other kinds of social relations (e.g., marriage), where the transactions are ritualized and the return “gift” need not be immediate. Trade, in contrast, I see as more overtly economic or commercial, with an immediate transaction of goods expected. Specialized trade, such as occurred in the Papuan hiri system, was often characterized by high volumes of goods passing through the networks. From the archaeological record alone, it can sometimes be difficult to discern whether one is dealing with exchange or trade.

65. On these points, see Kirch 1994.

66. Here I call attention to the facile distinction often drawn between Polynesia and Melanesia (in part, based on Sahlins 1963), that the former is characterized by chiefdoms and the latter by “big-man” political structures. In fact, many of the Austronesian-speaking societies of island Melanesia (as opposed to the New Guinea Highlands, where the big-man model is probably accurate) fit well within the chiefdom paradigm (see Scaglion 1996). Part of the confusion resulted from social collapse and “devolution” of complex political structures following initial European contact, in part due to massive depopulation.





CHAPTER SIX


1. Peterson (2009) presents an overview of Micronesian peoples and cultures.

2. Christian (1899), Hambruch (1911, 1919, 1936), and Kubary (1874) made early contributions to archaeology on Pohnpei and Kosrae. In the 1920s, Commander J.C. Thompson and H.G. Hornbostel excavated in the Marianas, sending their collections to the Bishop Museum, where they were analyzed by L.M. Thompson (1932). Chapman (1964) provides a survey of pre–World War II German and Japanese work, as well as sources up to the early 1960s. Overviews of Micronesian archaeology include Athens 2014; Cordy 1980, 1982; Craib 1983; Davidson 1988; Fitzpatrick 2014; Rainbird 1994, 2004. Intoh (1992a) summarizes Micronesian ceramic traditions; see also Takayama 1984.

3. For a geoarchaeological study of how cyclones can affect the archaeological record of atolls, see Dye, ed., 1987.

4. See the bibliographies in Rainbird 1994, 2004; as well as the symposium proceedings edited by Hunter-Anderson (1990).

5. On the subgrouping of Oceanic languages, see Pawley and Ross 1993, 1995; Ross 1988, 1996a.

6. The linguistic classification of Yapese, along with that of Nauru, has been debated, but Blust (2013) regards it as an Oceanic language. Bender and Wang (1985:54) were unable “to make a definitive statement on the relationship of either to the unquestionably nuclear Micronesian languages.” Ross (1996a) argued that Yapese is Oceanic but has been influenced by no fewer than four phases of borrowing, two from Non-Oceanic languages.

7. The one exception may be Nauruan (see Bender and Wang 1985:54).

8. For the tortured history of Spoehr’s Chalan Piao oyster shell date, see Butler 1994; Kurashina and Clayshulte 1983.

9. Carson (2014) provides an overview of the early Marianas sites; see also Carson 2010, 2011; Carson et al. 2013; Hung et al. 2011. On the Chalan Piao site, see Amesbury, Moore, and Hunter-Anderson 1996; Spoehr 1957:60–65. Investigations at Tarague Beach are reported by Athens (1986), Kurashina and Clayshulte (1983), and Kurashina et al. (1984). For the Unai Bapot site, see Carson 2008, 2014.

10. A third coring site, Lake Hagoi on Tinian Island, produced a date for human presence at about 3444 B.P. (Athens and Ward 1998).

11. The earliest radiocarbon date reported by Takayama (1982:91) for the Pemrang site is 360 ± 80 B.C., which, as in the Marianas, is associated with CST pottery quite different from the later Yapese “laminated” ware. At the Rungruw site, Intoh and Leach (1985:68) obtained a radiocarbon date of 1905 ± 65 B.P. in association with CST pottery.

12. On the ceramic finds at Nan Madol, see Athens 1990b; Bryson 1989; on the Lelu pottery, see Athens 1990a.

13. Regarding Nan Madol, Bath and Athens (1990:280) write that “radiocarbon dates of islet fill indicate an initial occupation on sand beaches, bars or possibly on stilt houses over the reef as early as A.D. 1.”

14. Athens (ed., 1995:267; see also Athens 1990a) indicates that Kosrae pottery is different from that of Pohnpei and more like ceramics from Chuuk. However, all three assemblages fall within the range of variation known for late Lapita ceramics in the southeastern Solomons.

15. See also summaries of sea level change in Athens, ed., 1995:239–51; and in Kirch and Hunt, eds., 1993.

16. For the Majuro and Arno sites, see Dye, ed., 1987; Riley 1987; the Maloelap date is from Weisler 1999. See Beardsley 1994 for an archaeological study of Kwajalein Atoll.

17. For additional information on Marianas prehistory, see Amesbury, Moore, and Hunter-Anderson 1996; Athens 2011; Bonhomme and Craib 1987; B. Butler, ed., 1988; B. Butler 1992; Carson 2008, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2014; Carson et al. 2013; Carson and Kurashina 2012; Craib 1986; Dixon et al. 2006; Dye and Cleghorn 1990; Graves 1986a, 1986b, 1991; Hung et al. 2011; Hunter-Anderson and Butler 1995; F. Petchy et al. 2016; Reinman 1966, n.d. [1966]; Russell 1998; Russell and Fleming 1986; Takayama and Egami 1971; Takayama and Intoh 1976; Thompson 1932.

18. On Palau prehistory, see Athens and Ward 2001; G. Clark 2005; G. Clark, Anderson, and Wright 2006; Dickinson and Athens 2007; Fitzpatrick 2001, 2003, 2007; Fitzpatrick and Callaghan 2013; Gumerman, Snyder, and Masse 1981; Hazell and Fitzpatrick 2006; Masse 1989, 1990; Masse, Snyder, and Gumerman 1984; Morgan 1988; Osborne 1966, 1979; Van Tilburg 1991; and Welch 2002.

19. On Chuuk archaeology, see Brooks 1984; Clune 1974; Knudson 1990; Parker and King 1984; Takayama and Intoh 1978, 1980; Takayama and Seki 1973; Takayama and Shutler 1978.

20. On Pohnpei archaeology, see Athens 1980, 1983, 1984a, 1984b, 1990b; Ayres 1992; Ayres and Haun 1985, 1990; Ayers, Haun, and Mauricio 1983; Ayres, Haun, and Severance 1979; Ayres and Mauricio 1987; Bath 1984a, 1984b; Bath and Athens 1990; Christian 1899; Hambruch 1911, 1936; Kubary 1874; Levin and Ayres 2016; McCoy et al. 2016.

21. As noted in chapter 5, Terebra shell adzes also appear in island Melanesian sequences in the last 1,000 years.

22. Athens (2007b) suggests that the Saudeleur reign lasted from about A.D. 1200–1300 to 1500–1600.

23. Key works on Micronesian atoll archaeology include Beardsley 1994; Craib 1984; Davidson 1967a, 1967b, 1992; Davidson and Leach 1996; Dye, ed., 1987; Fujimura and Alkire 1984; Harris, Lambrides, and Weisler 2016; Harris and Weisler 2016; Intoh 1984, 1986, 1991, 1992b, 1996; Leach and Davidson 1988; Leach and Ward 1981; Moir 1989; Poteate et al. 2016; Rosendahl 1987; Weisler 1999, 2001a, 2001b; Weisler, Yamano, and Hua 2012; Yamaguchi, Kayanne, and Yamano 2009.

24. Kiribati remains a major gap in the archaeological record for Micronesia. Some excavations were conducted by Takayama, but only preliminary accounts are available (Takayama and Takasugi 1987; Takayama, Takasugi, and Kaiyama 1989, 1990).

25. On Nukuoro archaeology, see Davidson 1967a, 1967b, 1992; and on Kapingamarangi, see Leach and Ward 1981. Leach and Davidson (1988) discuss faunal evidence for prehistoric fishing on both islands.

26. An excellent overview of atoll environments, including human ecology, is Wiens 1962. The Atoll Research Bulletin, published by the Smithsonian Institution, includes many important studies of human ecology on atolls.

27. The land snail faunas of atolls reveal the extent to which these islands have been influenced by human agency and are dominated by synanthropic species transported by people (e.g., Gastrocopta pediculus, Lamellaxis gracilis, and Assiminea nitida). Harry (1966) provides an informative account of the snail fauna of Ulithi Atoll.

28. For a classic ethnographic description of traditional navigation, see Gladwin 1970; see also Goodenough and Thomas 1987.

29. In addition to Spoehr 1957 and Thompson 1932, important archaeological surveys or excavations of latte sites include Athens 2011; Bayman et al. 2012; B. Butler, ed., 1988; B. Butler 1992; Craib 1986; Dixon et al. 2006; Reinman n.d. [1966]. Graves (1986b) synthesizes data pertaining to latte, while Morgan (1988) offers an architect’s viewpoint. Cordy (1983; see also 1986) also discusses Chamorro sociopolitical organization (but see Graves 1986a).

30. Butler (1992:223) lends cautious support to Craib’s position, based on independent data from Aguiguan Island.

31. Osborne (1966, 1979) included many of these sites in his survey and excavated at Bairulchau; Van Tilburg (1991) documented 38 monoliths. Morgan (1988:13–17) provides additional information on the Melekeok and Bairulchau sites.

32. Intoh (1991) raises the possibility of cultural contacts with the Solomon Islands. Specifically, shell fishhooks from Fais are of distinctive Solomon Island forms.

33. Labby’s suggestions regarding changing patterns of agricultural production find support in the paleoenvironmental sequence of Dodson and Intoh (1999).

34. Reisenberg (1968) analyzed the traditional sociopolitical system of Pohnpei, and additional ethnographic material of relevance to the archaeological record is contained in Christian 1899 and Hambruch 1936. See also Hanlon 1988; Petersen 1990.

35. An 1849 map of Madolenihmw Harbor by M.J. de Rosamel shows Nan Madol but in a stylized manner. For an annotated bibliography of early references to Nan Madol, see Athens 1981.

36. For archaeological studies at Nan Madol, see Athens 1980, 1983, 1984a, 1990b, 2007b; Ayres 1992; Ayres, Haun, and Mauricio 1983; Bryson 1989; McCoy et al. 2016; Seikel 2011.

37. The main source for Pohnpei oral traditions is The Book of Luelen (Bernart 1977); see also the summary by Maurico in Ayres, Haun, and Mauricio 1983; and Mauricio 1987.

38. Katau has sometimes been identified as Kosrae Island, but as Goodenough (1986) argues, the term refers to a mythical “sky world.”

39. This kind of arboriculture, what Athens, Ward, and Murakami (1996) call an “agroforest,” is similar to that found in the southeast Solomon Islands (Yen 1974a), such as on Tikopia (Kirch and Yen 1982). Given that Kosrae was probably settled from the southeast Solomons, these arboricultural systems may have a common origin in Lapita subsistence practices. See also Levin and Ayres 2016 on Pohnpei agroforests.

40. These accounts have been summarized and analyzed by Cordy (1993).





CHAPTER SEVEN


1. The To‘aga site excavations are reported in Kirch et al. 1990; and Kirch and Hunt, eds., 1993. An account of our Manu‘a fieldwork is also given in Kirch 2015:214–23.

2. For a time, it was thought that Tonga had been settled as much as a millennium before Samoa, leading Les Groube (1971) to propose Tonga as the original Polynesian homeland. The accidental discovery of Lapita pottery at Mulifanua, on ‘Upolu in Western Samoa, demonstrated that the lack of Lapita sites in Samoa was a problem of archaeological “visibility” due to coastal submergence, and thus an issue of sampling error.

3. For a somewhat different view of Polynesian origins, one that has echoes of Hiroa’s (1938b) Micronesian route theory, see Addison and Matisoo-Smith (2010), and a response by Davidson (2012).

4. On interisland relationships, see Best 1984, 1987; Davidson 1979; Green 1996a; Green and Kirch 1997; Kirch 1988c.

5. Geraghty (1983) provides a network or chain model of linguistic differentiation among Fijian and Polynesian languages.

6. On the ceramic sequences of Western Polynesia, see Burley 1998; Burley and Clark 2003; Dye 1988; Green 1974a; Kirch 1988c; Kirch and Hunt, eds., 1993; Poulsen 1983, 1987; Sand 1990, 1992.

7. Green (1971, 1974a) discusses the development of the Polynesian adz kit.

8. On the Polynesian “homeland,” see Green 1981.

9. Archaeological overviews of Polynesia include Bellwood 1979, 1987a; Green 1993; Jennings, ed., 1979; Kirch 1982a, 1984a, 1989a.

10. The phrase “segment of cultural history” is from Romney (1957), one of the originators of the phylogenetic model; see also Bellwood 1996b; Bellwood, Fox, and Tryon 1995; Kirch and Green 1987, 2001; Vogt 1964, 1994.

11. On the internal subgrouping of Polynesian, see Biggs 1971; R. Clark 1979; Green 1966; Marck 2000; Pawley 1966; Wilson 1985; Walworth 2014.

12. A number of Polynesian languages are extinct, such as Moriori in the Chatham Islands, or have been replaced, such as some of the languages of the Austral chain (largely replaced by Tahitian). Other Polynesian languages, such as Mangaian and Mangarevan, remain inadequately documented.

13. Such statistical analyses have been carried out by, among others, Howells (1970) and Pietrusewsky (1970, 1990, 1994).

14. See Hill and Serjeantson, eds., 1989; Kayser et al. 2006; Merriwether et al. 1999.

15. In a controversial theory, Houghton (1991) argues that critical selection pressures on the Polynesian genotype resulted from the risks of long-distance voyaging, with its exposure of the human body, at sea level, to prolonged periods of cold.

16. Kirch (1990b) discusses differences between the temple architecture of Tonga (a Western Polynesian culture) and Hawai‘i (Eastern Polynesian).

17. On adz sourcing in Polynesia and the movement of adzes among Eastern Polynesian communities, see M. Allen 1996a; Best et al. 1992; Rolett 1996, 2002; Rolett et al. 2015; Walter 1996; Weisler 1993, 1998a, 2002; Weisler, ed., 1997; Weisler and Kirch 1996; Weisler et al. 2016.

18. On the concept of heterarchy in social organization and its significance for the evolution of complex societies, see Ehrenreich, Crumley, and Levy, eds., 1995.

19. The domestication of kava, based on botanical and ethnographic evidence, is discussed by Lebot and Levesque (1989).

20. The Samoan research was published in Green and Davidson, eds., 1969, 1974; see also Davidson 1977, 1979. On the Tongatapu excavations, see Poulsen 1968, 1983, 1987.

21. On Samoan archaeology, see Burley and Clark 2003; J.T. Clark 1996; Clark and Herdrich 1993; Clark and Michlovic 1996; Clark et al. 2016; Cochrane et al. 2015; Herdrich 1991; Jennings and Holmer, eds., 1980; Jennings et al. 1976; Kirch and Hunt, eds., 1993; Kirch et al. 1990; Morrison and Addison 2009; Pearl 2004; Quintus and Clark 2012; Quintus et al. 2015; Rieth and Hunt 2008; Rieth, Morrison, and Addison 2008; Wallin, Wallin-Martisson, and Clark 2007; Wallin-Martinsson 2007; Wallin-Martinsson, Wallin, and Clark 2007.

22. On Tongan archaeology, see Burley 1994, 1998; Burley and Connaughton 2007; Burley et al. 2001, 2010, 2012, 2015; G. Clark 2016; G. Clark, Burley, and Murray 2008; G. Clark and Reepmeyer 2014; G. Clark, Reepmeyer, and Melekiola 2016; G. Clark et al. 2014; Dickinson, Burley, and Shutler 1994; Dye 1988; Kirch 1988c; Poulsen 1968, 1983, 1987; Reepmeyer, Clark, and Sheppard 2012; Shutler et al. 1994; Spennemann 1989; Valentin and Clark 2013.

23. On Futunan archaeology, see Frimigacci 1990; Kirch 1981, 1994; Sand 1990, 1993b. Results from ‘Uvea are reported in Frimigacci 2000; Frimigacci and Hardy 1997; Sand 1998b, 2000a; Sand and Valentin 1991. Burrows (1937) discusses ‘Uvean oral traditions.

24. Trotter (Trotter, ed., 1979) conducted an initial archaeological survey of Niue, and Walter and Anderson (2002) carried out limited excavations at several sites. The cultural sequence begins around 250 B.C. and is aceramic from the beginning.

25. On Western Polynesian prehistory in general, see Davidson 1977, 1979; Green 1968. For the Tokelau Islands, see Best 1988.

26. The chronology of ceramic loss in Western Polynesia is debated. Green (1974a) suggested that pottery was abandoned in Samoa by around A.D. 300–400, while Clark and Michlovic (1996) argue—on the basis of evidence from the ‘Aoa Valley—that it persisted much later, until ca. A.D. 1500. For Niuatoputapu, I believe the evidence supports a date of ca. A.D. 800–900 for terminal pottery use (Kirch 1988c).

27. On changes in adz forms, see Green 1971, 1974a; Green and Davidson, eds., 1969.

28. On these methodological issues, see Athens 1997; Kirch 1986; Kirch and Ellison 1994; Spriggs and Anderson 1993.

29. This model was proposed by Emory and Sinoto (1965) and elaborated by Emory (1968) and Sinoto (1967, 1970, 1983). Finney (1985) proposed that a direct voyage from Western Polynesia to the Marquesas may have been facilitated by periodic wind shifts associated with El Niño weather patterns.

30. The “orthodox scenario” was critiqued by Kirch (1986); see also Irwin 1981; Rolett 1993; Sutton 1987.

31. For the flavor of these debates, see, e.g., Irwin 1992, 1998; Kirch 1986; Kirch and Ellison 1994; Spriggs and Anderson 1993.

32. See, e.g., M.S. Allen 2004; M.S. Allen and Wallace 2007; Anderson, Binney, and Harris 1994; Anderson and Sinoto 2002; Conte and Anderson 2003; Dye 2011, 2015; Green and Weisler 2002; Kirch et al. 2010; Rolett1998; Rolett and Conte 1995; Weisler 1994, 1995. Wilmshurst et al. (2011) claimed that the settlement of all but the Society Islands occurred after A.D. 1190–1290. However, their argument depends on a fairly extreme form of chronometric hygiene, and not all archaeologists accept their conclusion. For a discussion of problems with the Wilmshurst et al. (2011) analysis, see Mulrooney et al. 2011.

33. The claim of Lepofsky, Harries, and Kellum (1992) for initial settlement of Mo‘orea Island by A.D. 600, based on anaerobically preserved coconuts from sediments in the lower ‘Opunohu Valley, can now be rejected, given the discovery by Kahn et al. (2015) that coconuts were naturally present on Mo‘orea for thousands of years prior to human arrival.

34. On voyaging traditions, see Beckwith 1970; Howe, ed., 2006; Luomala 1955; Thrum, ed., 1916–20.

35. The experimental voyages of the Hokule‘a and the work of the Polynesian Voyaging Society are discussed in Babayan et al. 1987; Finney 1977, 1988, 1996a, 1996b; Finney et al. 1986, 1989. Anderson (2001) and Irwin and Flay (2015) discuss Polynesian voyaging canoe design and performance.

36. On Eastern Polynesian fishhooks, see M.S. Allen 1992, 1996b; Duff 1950; Emory, Bonk, and Sinoto 1959; Sinoto 1962; Suggs 1961; Walter 1989a. On adzes, see Duff 1956; Emory 1968; Green 1971.

37. The problems of pottery in the Marquesas and of whether the sherds found at four sites on three islands are the product of local manufacture or were exotic imports remain contentious. For various positions, see Dickinson and Shutler 1974; Green 1974b; Kirch, Dickinson, and Hunt 1988; Sinoto 1966, 1979b; Suggs 1961. Dickinson et al. (1998:119) assert that while potsherds from the Ha‘atuatua site were without doubt manufactured on the Rewa Delta of Viti Levu, Fiji, sherds from other sites on Nuku Hiva, Hiva Oa, and Ua Huka “contain contrasting quartz-free basaltic temper sands compatible with local derivation from Marquesan volcanic assemblages.”

38. On the geochemcial characterization and souring of basalt and other volcanic rocks in Polynesia, see Best et al. 1992; Collerson and Weisler 2007; Di Piazza and Pearthree 2001; McAlister, Sheppard, and Allen 2013; Mills and Lundblad 2014; Weisler 1993, 1998a; Weisler, ed., 1997; Weisler et al. 2016.

39. On Ma‘uke, see Walter 1989a, 1990; on Aitutaki, see M.S. Allen 1996a; Allen and Schubel 1990; Allen and Steadman 1990.

40. The Pacific Equatorial Islands, a dispersed group of atolls lying between central Eastern Polynesia and Hawai‘i, were presumably also settled at this time, although all were later abandoned, probably due to the high frequency of drought. On archaeological sites in the Equatorial Islands, see Di Piazza and Pearthree 2004; Emory 1934a; A. Sinoto 1973.





CHAPTER EIGHT


1. The estimate of canoes is from Ledyard (1963:103), who calculated that at least 15,000 people greeted Cook’s expedition as it entered Kealakekua Bay on Hawai‘i Island.

2. On the Makahiki and other Hawaiian rituals of kingship, see Valeri 1985.

3. On Cook as Lono, see the masterful work of Sahlins (1981, 1995).

4. For overviews of evolutionary paradigms incorporating a chiefdom stage, see Fried 1967; Renfrew 1984; Service 1967. Earle, ed. (1991), reviews archaeological perspectives on chiefdoms, and Carneiro (1981) summarizes the evolutionary significance of chiefdoms.

5. On the “domestic mode of production” and its significance for chiefdoms, see Sahlins 1972. Friedman and Rowlands (1978) present an “epigenetic model” of sociopolitical evolution in which the chiefdom stage figures prominently.

6. The exceptions are New Zealand, where resource restriction was due not to size limitation but to a temperate climate that posed great challenges to the Ancestral Polynesian production system based on a tropical horticultural complex, and Samoa, a large and fertile archipelago. I believe that Goldman (1970) was in error in placing Samoa within the Open society group. As Sahlins (1958) more correctly realized, Samoa possessed a Stratified sociopolitical system much more similar to that of Tonga, the Society Islands, and Hawai‘i.

7. The results of the Mangaia Project are reported in Kirch, ed., 2017; see also Kirch 1996b, 1997b; Kirch, Steadman, et al. 1995; Steadman 2006; Steadman and Kirch 1990. Bellwood (1978) surveyed marae sites on Mangaia. Trotter, ed. (1974), reports on similar marae sites elsewhere in the Southern Cook Islands, and Chikamori, Yoshida, and Yamaguchi (1995) report on marae in the Northern Cook Islands (Tongareva).

8. Mangaia society was described by Hiroa (1934), who relied on early missionary accounts as well as his own field research. Reilly (2003, 2009) has greatly augmented our understanding of traditional Mangaian society and history.

9. Handy 1923 remains the classic source on Marquesan ethnography, augmented by the historical work of Dening (1980) and Thomas (1990).

10. Linton (1925) pioneered surface survey throughout the Marquesas, while Suggs (1961) conducted the first stratigraphic excavations on Nuku Hiva and outlined the prehistoric sequence. Work by Sinoto (1966, 1970, 1979b, 1996b), especially at the Hane dune site, augmented our understanding of the cultural sequence. Subsequent excavations by M.S. Allen and colleagues (Allen 2004, 2009, 2010, 2014; Allen and Addison 2002; Allen and McAlister 2013), Conte and Molle (2012, 2014; Molle and Conte 2011), Molle (2011), and Rolett (1989, 1992, 1998; Rolett and Conte 1995) have amplified the archaeological record. Settlement pattern studies by Bellwood (1972), Chavaillon and Olivier (2007), Kellum-Ottino (1971), and Ottino (1985, 1990a, 1990b; Ottino-Garanger 2006) added information on architecture and land use. Millerstrom (1997, 2003) discusses Marquesan rock art in its settlement context.

11. For further details on the Marquesan pottery sherds, see Allen, Dickinson, and Huebert 2012; Dickinson and Shutler 1974; Dickinson et al. 1998; Kirch et al. 1988.

12. The Marquesan faunal sequence was first outlined by Kirch (1973), augmented by Dye (1990, 1996) and Rolett (1992). Steadman (1989, 1997, 2006) and Steadman and Rolett (1996) discuss bird extinctions and extirpations. Huebert and Allen (2016) offer insights into the development of arboriculture, while Richards et al. (2009) apply stable isotope analysis to understanding pig husbandry.

13. Ernst Mayr (1997) drew a distinction between proximate and ultimate forms of causation. Prestige rivalry in the social arena may have been a proximate cause for aspects of Classic Period Marquesan culture, such as the elaboration of material arts that were elite sumptuary items. Underlying such a proximate cause, however, one can identify other ultimate causes, including resource restriction, periodic environmental perturbations (drought), and high population density.

14. On Green’s 1959 Mangareva excavations, see Green and Weisler 2000, 2002, 2004; Weisler and Green 2013. Weisler (2003) reports on excavations on Temoe Atoll, a part of the Gambier group.

15. For discussion of the “mystery islands” and a complete listing of these, see Kirch 1984a:89–92, table 9.

16. Early and important surveys of Rapa, Ra‘ivavae, and Tubuai were carried out by Stokes (n.d. [1930], n.d.) and Aitken (1930), while the Norwegian Archaeological Expedition visited Rapa and Ra‘ivavae in 1955–56 (Heyerdahl and Ferdon, eds., 1965).

17. A great deal of ethnohistoric data on Rapa Nui culture is also synthesized in Edwards and Edwards 2013.

18. The literature on Rapa Nui archaeology is extensive, and only a few key works can be referenced here. The results of the pioneering Norwegian Archaeological Expedition were published in Heyerdahl and Ferdon, eds., 1961, 1965; see also Golson 1965. Important excavation reports include Ayres 1973; Mulloy and Figueroa 1978; Skjølsvold, ed., 1994. Van Tilburg (1994) provides a masterful synthesis and excellent bibliography; see also P.C. McCoy 1979. Lee (1992) describes the island’s notable rock art. On the statue cult, see Lipo, Hunt, and Haoa 2013; Shepardson 2005; Van Tilburg 1986, 1988; Vargas 1988. Evidence for precontact agriculture is discussed in Ladefoged, Flaws, and Stevenson 2013; C.M. Stevenson 1997; Stevenson, Wozniak, and Haoa 1999; Stevenson et al. 2006, 2015; Vitousek et al. 2014; Wozniak 2001, 2003. The volumes edited by Vargas (1998), Fischer (1993), and Stevenson and Ayres (2000) also contain much valuable information.

19. P.C. McCoy’s settlement pattern survey (1976) of the Rano Kau area was fully analyzed. Preliminary results of the more extensive University of Chile survey may be found in Cristino and Vargas 1980; Cristino, Vargas, and Izaurieta 1981; Vargas 1993, 1998; Vargas, Cristino, and Izaurieta 2006; Vargas et al. 1998. Mulrooney (2012, 2013) discusses the chronology of settlement.

20. Palynological studies and vegetation history are presented by Flenley and King (1984), Flenley et al. (1991), and Orliac (1998).

21. See also Hunt 2006, 2007; Hunt and Lipo 2006; Lipo and Hunt 2005; Lipo et al. 2016.

22. Although I have not conducted primary fieldwork on Rapa Nui, I have been fortunate to visit on two occasions, in 1984 and 1996, giving me the opportunity to visit most of the major archaeological sites.

23. Later excavations at Anakena by Hunt and Lipo (2006) yielded radiocarbon dates that are interpreted as supporting a late colonization date of A.D. 1200. In my view, both the Steadman and Hunt and Lipo date series support initial Polynesian settlement between ca. A.D 1000 and 1200.

24. Steadman (2006) and Steadman, Vargas, and Cristino (1994) present zooarchaeological evidence for former seabird populations on Rapa Nui. On Rapa Nui soils and their nutrient properties, see Ladefoged et al. 2010; Stevenson et al. 2015; Vitousek et al. 2014.

25. Important contributions to the historical ecology of Rapa Nui include Commendador et al. 2013; Horrocks and Wozniak 2007; Loret and Tanacredi, eds., 2003; Mann et al. 2008; Mieth and Bork 2005, 2010; Orliac 1998; Tromp and Dudgeon 2015.

26. Hunt and Lipo (2011; Lipo et al. 2013), using experimental archaeology, argue that the statues “walked” upright from the quarries to the temple sites, essentially rocked back and forth on specially carved convex bases while held by guide ropes. Timber rollers, sledges, and levers were almost certainly also used in various stages of quarrying, transport, and statue erection.

27. The assertions of Hunt and Lipo (Hunt 2006, 2007; Hunt and Lipo 2011) that the island’s population never exceeded 3,000 have no empirical validity, nor do they make any sense demographically.

28. Note that this labor force included not just the stone workers in Rano Raraku, Puna Pau, and other quarries but also those who kept the former supplied with food and with such materials as great lengths of sturdy rope and a continual supply of basalt picks.

29. I draw attention to this gaze deliberately, for the emphasis accorded the eye in the Rapa Nui statues give the images a visual presence that must be seen to be appreciated.

30. On Rapa Nui soils and rock mulch gardening, see Wozniak 2001, 2003; Stevenson, Wozniak, andHaoa 1999; Stevenson et al. 2006, 2015. See also Ladefoged et al. 2010; Ladefoged, Flaws, and Stevenson 2013; Louwagie, Stevenson, and Langohr 2006.

31. The term literally means “statue toppling.”

32. It has been claimed that the mata‘a were not weapons at all but agricultural implements (Lipo et al. 2016; Hunt and Lipo 2011:95–99), although considerable ethnohistoric information refutes this.

33. According to Anderson, Binney, and Harris (2014), this name may be a nineteenth-century invention.

34. An important exception to the unsystematic nature of excavations prior to Golson is Duff’s (1950) work at the Wairau Bar site. The careful excavations and analysis of the Wairau Bar assemblages established the Eastern Polynesian origins of Maori culture.

35. Davidson’s (1984) initial synthesis of New Zealand prehistory is still worth consulting, although it has been superseded by that of Anderson et al. (2014). See also Furey and Holdaway, eds., 2004; Prickett, ed., 1982; Wilson, ed., 1987. Leach (2006) offers a synthesis of archaeological data on prehistoric fishing in New Zealand.

36. For key site reports and analyses of such South Island Archaic sites, see Anderson 1982, 1989a; Anderson and Smith 1992, 1996; Anderson et al., eds., 1996; Duff 1956.

37. On the environmental transformation of South Island by fire and other human actions, see McGlone 1983, 1989.

38. Furey (2006) has synthesized the archaeological evidence for prehistoric horticulture in New Zealand; see also Barber 1989; Best 1925; Horrocks et al. 2008; Jones 1986, 1989, 1991; Leach 1984; Leach and Leach, eds., 1979; Sullivan 1985.

39. On pa variation, classification, and distribution, see Bellwood 1971; Best 1927; Davidson 1984; Fox 1976; Groube 1965. For examples of excavated pa sites, see Fox 1978; Fox and Green 1982; McFadgen and Sheppard 1984; Phillips 1986, 2000; Shawcross 1976; Sutton, Furey, and Marshall 2003. Irwin, ed. (2004), describes the Kohika site, a lake village with remarkable preservation of wooden artifacts.

40. The linkage between pa and a horticultural economy is made clear by the absence of pa sites in South Island south of the Banks Peninsula. According to Davidson (1984:193), virtually all of the approximately 100 pa recorded for South Island are concentrated in “the area in which Polynesian horticulture was marginally possible.”

41. Key studies on the temporal development of pa and pa-based settlement patterns include Groube 1970; Irwin 1985b; Schmidt 1996; Sutton 1990; Sutton, ed., 1990, 1993; Sutton, Furey, and Marshall 2003.

42. Barber (1996:877) also suggests that pa construction “is consistent with a heightened concern for territorial visibility and, as a corollary, the physical and spiritual maintenance of local production and harvest by cognate groups.”

43. On Futunan archaeology, see Frimigacci 1990; Kirch 1981, 1994; Sand 1990, 1993b.

44. On the development of Futunan agricultural systems, see Di Piazza 1990; Frimigacci 1990; Kirch 1994.

45. In addition to the references cited here, other contributions include Garanger’s (1967) study of marae in the Tautira region of Tahiti; Descantes’s (1990) analysis of Green’s ‘Opunohu Valley settlement pattern data; Eddowes (1991) on marae; Emory and Sinoto’s various studies (Emory 1979; Emory and Sinoto 1965; Sinoto 1979a, 1996a); Wallin (1993); and the paleoenvironmental analyses of Orliac (1997) and Parkes (1997).

46. Regarding Samoan settlement patterns and sites, see J.T. Clark 1996; Clark and Herdrich 1993; Clark and Michlovic 1996; Clark et al. 2016; Herdrich 1991; Jennings and Holmer, eds., 1980; Jennings et al. 1976; Kirch and Hunt, eds., 1993; Pearl 2004; Quintus 2012, 2015; Quintus and Clark 2012, 2016; Quintus et al. 2015; Wallin, Wallin-Martinsson, and Clark 2007; Wallin-Martinsson 2007.

47. The term maritime empire was first coined by Guiart (1963).

48. Kirch (1985a) provided a synthesis of Hawaiian archaeology up to the early 1980s (see also Kirch 1990a, 1990b). Other overviews include Bayman and Dye 2013; and Tuggle 1979.

49. Examples of early survey work include Bennett 1931; Emory 1924, 1928; McAllister 1933; Stokes 1991. Hawaiian rock art is surveyed by Cox and Stasack 1970; and Lee and Stasack 1999.

50. Some of the classic culture-historical works in Hawaiian archaeology include Emory, Bonk, and Sinoto 1959, 1969; Emory and Sinoto 1961; Pearson, ed., 1969.

51. The literature of Hawaiian archaeology is too vast to enumerate here. Selected examples of empirical studies are Bayman and Moniz-Nakamura 2001; Cordy 1981, 2000; Earle 1978; Field and Graves, ed., 2015; Green 1980; Hommon 1986, 2013; Kirch 1977, 1979a, 1990a, 1990b, 2004, 2014; Kirch, ed., 2011; Kirch and Kelly, eds., 1975; Kirch and Sahlins 1992; Kirch et al. 2010; Kirch and O’Day 2003; Kolb 1992, 1994, 1999; Kolb and Dixon 2002; M.D. McCoy 2006, 2007; Mills and Lundblad 2014; Pearson, ed., 1968; Tuggle and Griffin, eds., 1973; Tuggle and Tomonari-Tuggle 1980; Weisler and Kirch 1985.

52. On changes in Hawaiian archaeological practice, and some of the political problems this has engendered, see Graves and Erkelens 1991; Kawelu 2015; Kirch 1999; Spriggs 1989. For examples of archaeological research generated by CRM contracts, see J. Clark and Kirch, eds., 1983; Kirch 1979a; Schilt 1984; Weisler and Kirch 1985.

53. The contact period population of Hawai‘i has been debated (see Stannard 1989) but seems unlikely to have been less than about half a million, possibly considerably more.

54. For other culture historical periodizations of Hawai‘i, see Cordy 1974; Hommon 1986; Kirch 1985a, 1990b; Kolb 1994, 1992; Weisler 1989.

55. On the dating of initial Polynesian settlement of Hawai‘i, see Athens, Rieth, and Dye 2014; Dye and Pantaleo 2010; Kirch 2011.

56. On these voyaging traditions, see Thrum, ed., 1916–20; and the discussion in Finney 1996b.

57. See Emory et al. 1969; Kirch and Kelly, eds., 1975; and Pearson, Kirch, and Pietrusewsky 1971 for reports on these sites.

58. See Athens 1997; Athens and Ward 1993; Athens, Ward, and Wickler 1992.

59. On initial attempts at demographic modeling, see Cordy 1981; Hommon 1986; Kirch 1984a:104–16.

60. This approach was first applied by Dye and Komori (1992), and has been used as well by Carson (2005).

61. On Hawaiian irrigation systems, see Jane Allen 1991; Earle 1978; Kirch 1977, 1984a; Kirch and Kelly, eds., 1975; Kirch and Sahlins 1992; McCoy and Graves 2010; McElroy 2007; Tuggle and Tomonari-Tuggle 1980; Yen et al. 1972.

62. On the dryland field systems of Hawai‘i Island, see M.S. Allen, ed., 2001; Field et al. 2010, 2011; Kirch 1984a, 1994; Ladefoged and Graves 2000; Ladefoged, Graves, and McCoy 2003; Ladefoged, Graves, and Coil 2005; Mulrooney and Ladefoged 2005; Rosendahl 1972, 1994; Vitousek et al. 2004. On the Maui systems, see Coil and Kirch 2005; Dixon et al. 1999; Holm 2006; Kirch, Coil, et al. 2005; Kirch, Hartshorn, et al. 2004; Kirch, Holson, and Baer 2009. And on the Kalaupapa, Moloka‘i, system, see M.D. McCoy 2005, 2006.

63. The argument regarding “wet” versus “dry” modes of agricultural intensification is advanced in detail in Kirch 1994; see also Kirch, ed., 2011.

64. On the temporal development of Hawaiian heiau, see Kolb 1992, 1994, 1999; as well as the overview in Kirch 1985a) and references cited therein to specific heiau site studies. Other important heiau studies include Bennett 1931; Emory 1924, 1928; Masse, Carter, and Somers 1991; McAllister 1933; Stokes 1991; Weisler and Kirch 1985.

65. Archaeological evidence for craft specialization has focused primarily on basalt adze production, regarding which see Bayman and Moniz-Nakamura 2001; Cleghorn 1986; Kahn et al. 2009; M.D. McCoy, Asner, and Graves 2011; Mills and Lundblad 2014.





CHAPTER NINE


1. Greg Dening (e.g., 1992) brought anthropology and history closer together, but his own approach was that of a particularistic history informed by anthropology rather than a generalizing anthropology informed by the details of history. Nonetheless, I admire his work.

2. The significant exception to this generalization would be New Zealand, but only for South Island, since where intensive sweet potato cultivation was feasible in North Island relatively high population densities were also achieved, in less than 1,000 years.

3. McArthur, Saunders, and Tweedie (1976) show the striking differences in extinction probabilities that result from small increases in the size of the founding population. For example, a founding group of three couples (six people) in which the only marriage rule is a ban on incest has an extinction probability of 77 percent, whereas increasing that founding group to seven couples lowers the extinction probability to only 19 percent.

4. The speed at which even a small founding group can quickly produce a dense population on Oceanic high islands with good agricultural resources is shown by the case of Pitcairn Island, settled by the Bounty mutineers and their Tahitian accomplices in 1790. The founding population of 28 had given rise to 193 descendants by 1856, when the British government stepped in to remove them to Norfolk Island because of overcrowding and a shortage of agricultural land. The net rate of intrinsic increase during this period had been about 3.7 percent.

5. The classic ethnographic account of population control in Oceania is certainly that of Firth for Tikopia (Borrie, Firth, and Spillius 1957; Firth 1936; see also Kirch and Yen 1982; Kirch 1997b).

6. This is, presumably, what Douglas Yen had in mind when he wrote that “human population level may be regarded as the primary incentive to technological development or adoption of greater intensity in production methods” (1973:80).

7. In the case of Hawai‘i, initial European contact occurred with Cook’s 1778–79 voyage, when the population was estimated by Lieutenant King at around 400,000. The earliest missionary “census” (a rough estimate, really) was carried out 53 years later, and it suggested a total population of around 130,000 for the archipelago (Schmitt 1968). A decline from 400,000 to 130,000 in five decades is regarded by many historical demographers as too abrupt, and thus King’s estimate was reduced to 250,000 at first contact (Schmitt 1968; but see Stannard 1989). In the case of certain Western Polynesian islands such as Tongatapu, Niuatoputapu, and Futuna, “first contact” dates to the voyages of Tasman (1643) and Schouten and Le Maire (1616), with the first serious attempts at population estimate not made until some two centuries later. In these latter cases there would have been time for major population collapse but also for possible recovery.

8. Consider the case of Tahiti. In one of the earliest attempts at estimating Oceanic island populations, Johann Reinhold Forster, who had sailed with Cook in 1772–75, devoted a section of his famous Observations to a disquisition on “the numbers of inhabitants in the South-Sea-Isles” (1996 [1778]:145–52). Forster used estimates of naval forces as well as a carefully argued calculation of agricultural carrying capacity to estimate 150,000 persons for Tahiti and Emeo Islands and 200,000 for the rest of the Society Islands. Oliver (1974:35) calls Forster’s estimates “pure fantasy,” but it is not evident that Oliver’s reduced estimate of about 35,000 for Tahiti is any more accurate.

9. See Green and Davidson, eds., 1969, 1974; Jennings et al. 1976; Leach and Witter 1987, 1990; Quintus 2012, 2015.

10. The U.S. Exploring Expedition under the command of Commodore Wilkes estimated the Samoan population at 56,600 in 1839 (McArthur 1968:table 18), decades after first contact. Yet McArthur’s view was that there had been “no very great decrease in population numbers in Samoa before mid-century” because “sporadic wars, famines, and outbreaks of disease checked its growth” (1968:115).

11. Green and Davidson, eds. (1974:281–82), briefly discuss the matter of pre-European population in Samoa. Without giving their own estimate, they aver that “only a population twice the size of that in the 1840s [i.e., around 80,000] . . . would require the amount of arable land which archaeology indicates was once in use.” In a long, unpublished manuscript, Green (pers. comm. 1998) argued the case for a precontact Samoan population of about 200,000.

12. Another example of the gap between population estimates based on historical texts and those based on archaeological evidence is the Marquesas, where Suggs (1961:192) proposed a figure of 100,000 for the archipelago, but Rallu (1990) would go no higher than 43,000. For Tahiti in the Society Islands, there is tremendous disagreement, ranging from McArthur’s (1968) low estimate of 17,000 to 19,000 to Oliver’s (1974:34) cautious 35,000 up to Rallu’s (1990) estimate of 70,000.

13. The “failures” would presumably include attempts to colonize some of the minute or otherwise ecologically marginal islands, such as the Equatorial Islands, Nihoa, the Kermadecs, Pitcairn, Henderson, and others that had been abandoned by the time of European arrival in the Pacific.

14. For a review of some of the literature and ideas regarding intensification, see Morrison 1994, 1996; on specialization, see Brumfiel and Earle, eds., 1987.

15. One reason for this is that the flooded pondfield is “self-weeding” as long as the water level is maintained, whereas in dryland taro cultivation constant weeding (and usually mulching as well) is required. For Futuna Island (Kirch 1994), I estimated that the yield of irrigated pondfields was between four and eight times greater than that of shifting cultivation (per unit area).

16. For a more elaborate discussion of the theory invoked here, and the production-function model on which it builds, see Kirch 1984a:160–67; 2010:190–201.
